headline: Constellation Energy to Buy Power Producer Calpine
text:
Constellation Energy deal to buy Calpine is being driven by fast-rising demand for electricity in part by the technology industry investments in artificial intelligence 
Constellation Energy, the nation largest nuclear power plant operator, has agreed to buy another electricity producer, Calpine, for $16 4 billion, a deal that shows how fast-rising demand for power, partly a result of the data centers being built for artificial intelligence, is having far-reaching effects on the economy  
 The cash-and-stock deal, announced Friday, ranks among the power sector biggest, and indicates that natural gas is likely to play a larger role than many expected a few years ago in meeting the nation electricity needs  That could undermine efforts to address climate change unless companies quickly figure out how to capture and store emissions from gas power plants 
 The tie-up would broaden Constellation portfolio as companies like Microsoft, Google and Amazon are scrambling to secure energy for data centers used to run artificial intelligence and other services  Electricity demand is also increasing because of the building of new factories in the United States and greater use of electric vehicles and heat pumps  The growth is reshaping a traditionally sleepy industry that has not been accustomed to turbocharged growth 
 ''Lots of people who were not paying any attention to electricity a year ago are now trying to figure out how to participate in meeting what seems to be inevitable growth in demand,'' said Daniel Yergin, the vice chairman of S&P Global, who won a Pulitzer Prize for his book ''The Prize: The Epic Quest For Oil, Money and Power ''
 Calpine, which is based in Houston and privately held, operates a large fleet of natural gas power plants in several states as well as the Geysers geothermal energy complex in California 
 Constellation, which is based in Baltimore, said in a statement that it expected Calpine natural gas assets to help ensure the reliability of the electric grid  The combination also would broaden the company presence in Texas, where power demand is growing quickly, and add more renewable energy to its portfolio 
 ''We believe that natural gas and geothermal, along with nuclear, will be critically important for the nation,'' Joseph Dominguez, chief executive of Constellation, said on a call with investors and analysts on Friday morning 
 He added that it was important to ensure that energy resources were not only sustainable, but reliable as well  ''We believe that natural gas and clean energy, blended together, will be very attractive to customers,'' Mr  Dominguez said 
 Constellation stock price soared more than 20 percent in early trading on Friday and closed the day up 25 percent, an unusually large jump for an acquiring company  Its shares had already more than doubled over the past year as expectations for U S  power demand growth rose 
 Constellation would pay $4 5 billion in cash and assume roughly $12 7 billion of Calpine debt as part of the deal 
 Nuclear power plants, which can operate around the clock without releasing planet-warming emissions, have been among the early beneficiaries of booming investment in artificial intelligence  Constellation agreed last year to spend $1 6 billion to restart a nuclear reactor at Three Mile Island near Harrisburg, Pa  -- a project for which Microsoft is effectively footing the bill 
 But there are only a few mothballed nuclear plants that can be restarted  Some companies are also betting on new, smaller reactors, but those are not expected to begin producing meaningful amounts of power for at least several years if all goes well 
 As a result of those challenges, many energy and tech companies are increasingly looking to natural gas, even though its use releases carbon dioxide and methane, two leading greenhouse gases that are warming the planet 
 ''It going to be hard for the utilities to provide the power that these data centers need without gas,'' said Andrew Gillick, an energy strategist for the analytics firm Enverus 
 Power demand from data centers is poised to increase 15 percent a year on average through the end of the decade, Goldman Sachs estimated last year 
 Andrew Novotny, chief executive of Calpine, said the combined company would be able to invest in new power generation  ''Together, we will be better positioned to bring accelerated investment in everything from zero-emission nuclear to battery storage that will power our economy in a way that puts people and our environment first,'' he said in a statement 
 A diverse group of power plants could let the new company be more effective in how it manages its resources, depending on how electricity needs change  Adding more natural gas to its portfolio would, however, expose Constellation to more risk related to fluctuating commodity prices, Enverus said 
 The deal with Constellation is the culmination of a big turnaround for Calpine, which had come under pressure in recent years as California and other states sought to move away from fossil fuels  A group of investors including Energy Capital Partners took Calpine private several years ago in a deal valued at $5 6 billion, not including debt 
 The companies said they expected the transaction to close within a year, subject to regulatory approvals  Constellation would address any potential concerns raised by antitrust officials about its market power by selling assets, Mr  Dominguez said 
 Ivan Penn contributed reporting Ivan Penn contributed reporting 

Graphic
 
PHOTO: Calpine natural gas power plants, like this one in Texas, are increasingly valuable because of the rising demand for electricity from data centers  (PHOTOGRAPH BY TAMIR KALIFA FOR THE NEW YORK TIMES) This article appeared in print on page B4

headline: Consumer Electronics Show: Here are the trends shaping technology in 2025
text:
Consumer Technology Association
Attendees of the 2024 Consumer Electronics Show are pictured browsing the show floor  Despite some high highs and low lows in 2024, the tech sector is still brimming with innovation and optimism for the future 
1
2024 was a busy year for the tech world 
The rise of generative artificial intelligence dominated headlines as companies raced to get out in front of harnessing the technology for themselves and making it available to consumers, offering a sense of excitement and possibility 
On the other hand, the tech sector was forced to face a wave of layoffs that swept through the industry during the summer months, with the aftermath leading to the layoffs being dubbed the "Great Tech Reset "
Despite some high highs and low lows, the tech sector is still brimming with innovation and optimism for the future 
"Innovation is tangible  We can touch it and feel it  We know it when we see it," said Brian Comiskey, senior director of innovation and trends and futurist at the Consumer Technology Association  "When we face an obstacle, a challenge or a conflict, humans adapt, create or design technology to provide solutions "
Comiskey spoke ahead of the 2025 Consumer Electronics Show on Sunday night to shine a light on a few of the emerging trends shaping technology that consumers can expect to see in 2025 
Gen Z and tech
Born between 1997 and 2012, Gen Z represents the largest global generation ever, good for about one-third of the world population 
"To understand how technology changes, we first have to understand the people behind technology," Comiskey said  "Political, economic and sociocultural shifts certainly impact tech adoption and use  However, it generational change that cuts at the very core of the evolution in the relationship between humanity and technology "
For the tech industry, the rise of Gen Z represents a big change 
Why?
While millennials and Gen X are digitally fluent from the internet, Gen Z grew up with smartphones and tablets making them constantly connected and, thus, arguably the first true digital natives - and that impacts the generation tech purchasing patterns 
Research has shown Gen Z increasingly values sustainability and other cultural values in their purchasing habits  At the same time, the generation also engages in fast fashion and other less-than-sustainable habits, creating the idea of a value action gap 
"If Gen Z was exposed to products that had multiple sustainability values like recyclability and energy efficiency, they are two-and-a-half times more likely to purchase the product," Comiskey said, adding that marketers and product designers will need to account for that when building the technology that Gen Z will engage with 
AI into 2025
In 2024, the AI retail market was $7 billion  By the end of the decade - only five years from now - Comiskey said the market is projected to be $30 billion 
Comiskey said that over half of U S  consumers are using generative AI to personalize shopping, and at an individual shopper level, the use of AI generates a 40% higher likelihood of a purchase 
"AI will remain a key trend at CES this year  I don't think that surprises," Comiskey said  "It going to be about how does (AI) increase productivity?"
He added that companies like Chatbase are working to evolve AI into "AI agents" that can autonomously do business tasks such as payroll and client relationship management 
Additionally, companies like SES AI are developing batteries to power emerging humanoid robots 
It not just the upper-echelon of tech where AI is going to have an increased presence, either, as consumer devices are going to be equipped with stronger AI capabilities 
"AI integration in the next generation of consumer hardware - whether that a smartphone, a laptop, a TV or even smart glasses and vehicles - are transforming them from devices to intelligent platforms," Comiskey said 
Human security
"Human security really speaks to human rights like environmental protection, food security, access to health care and others," Comiskey said  "Technology has an opportunity and role to play in addressing and helping solve some of these challenges "
He highlighted the energy transition as one avenue where he sees technology answering the bell when it comes to global challenges 
Companies are building power sources directly into our grids, with Eaton developing cooling technology for data centers and Siemens and Samsung working to automate home energy operations with the use of AI 
Another notion of human security is something that interconnected with energy and the environment, and that food security 
So, how can tech help with that?
Kubota is developing robotic technologies that help manage farmland, and John Deere on Monday revealed a host of new, autonomous machines that combine advanced computer vision, AI and cameras to help the machines navigate their environments 
Comfort and content in smart homes
So much attention has been given to technological advancements of transportation, and while that won't be stopping in 2025, Comiskey posed a question for CES attendees 
What happens when you get home?
"When we're there, we're naturally seeking out comfort, and technology plays a role in supporting this," he said 
There perhaps no better indicator of this than an item that is a staple of households across the U S  and beyond: TVs 
Comiskey said TVs are transforming from entertainment consoles to "a smart home command center "
"Smart home hubs will further health integration, greater energy management capabilities and deeper personalization via AI to build the home in a way that supports financial, physical and overall well-being," Comiskey said 
Samsung, LG and Hisense are showcasing the next generation of AI-integrated TVs to help consumers manage their smart homes and flaunt the future of what entertainment could look like 
Ever envy a character outfit in a show or movie? Soon, you could have the capability to pause what you're watching and buy that outfit right from your screen 
Comiskey said that video streaming services and video games will likely have more focus on live services in 2025  Netflix recently broke streaming records with the most streamed National Football League game ever in the U S  on Christmas Day, and the success of video games like Marvel Rivals shows live services are a trend that won't be going away anytime soon 
"We're having our first track at the midpoint of this decade, all setting up to the 2030s," Comiskey said  "If the 2020s is the intelligence decade, then the 2030s will be the quantum decade "

headline: Cool Kids Don't Use AI Instead of Google
text:
Artificial Intelligence is as inescapable as the Atlanta pollen, but users are glossing over the extreme environmental and ethical issues as companies replace jobs and students trade hard work with AI-generated garbage  Generative AI is an unregulated beast that will take everything from entry-level jobs to authentic artwork and leave us with nothing if we do not change  AI squanders the resources it requires and must be trained on the backs and minds of actual people without their consent, often failing to provide helpful or accurate answers  Using generative AI platforms in place of a normal search engine is not only bad for the user due to potential misinformation, but is also unethical and lazy  
The training and maintenance of generative AI models utilize gargantuan amounts of energy and water and create ridiculously high carbon emissions  Quantifying exact numbers is difficult for the industry as a whole, but researchers estimate that in the next two years, AI could make up 0 5% of the world electricity consumption  This might not sound like much until you consider that this would mean AI would utilize as much as the entire country of Argentina  
A study from Cornell estimated that within the same timeframe, the amount of water utilized to cool AI data centers will reach half of England annual water consumption, or more than four times the usage of the entire country of Denmark  This is simply a waste of valuable resources that we cannot afford in the face of a growing climate crisis  
While the exact impact of the entire AI industry is difficult to measure, the impact of individual models is easier to track  For example, the training of GPT-3 created about 500 tons of carbon dioxide  This is equivalent to the average annual electricity consumption of over 127 American households and 108 cars  Recall that this is only for the training of a single version of a single AI model, and it dwarfs the impact that a single person has throughout their entire lifetime of living or driving  
The training of GPT-3 also used over 180,000 gallons of water, equivalent to the daily drinking water requirements of about 218,000 people  In a world where 1 in every 11 people lacks access to enough clean drinking water, this is both unethical and disturbing  
Beyond the environmental impact, the ethical concerns behind every single AI model are wide- reaching  In particular, the data that developers use to train these models is sourced broadly and without consent  In 2023, the Atlantic reported that Meta trained its Large Language Model on thousands of stolen and pirated works, and experts are warning that developers are training AI on any content you have posted online that isn't behind a login wall (such as a public Instagram account or a LinkedIn profile)  Artists and writers across the board have made their anger public as massive companies use their works to train various models that generate both images and text, leading to over 30 copyright infringement cases in the United States alone  
Training these models on personal data, articles, stories, artworks and any other creations that users like you and me have shared online is inherently wrong, especially for posts made before the advent of generative artificial intelligence entering the mainstream  Those who shared their creations had no way to agree to companies training AI models on their work and retroactively opting out is hardly an option  
The information generative AI programs peddle is false far more often than many people realize  Researchers have found that GPT-3 agrees with false statements up to over a quarter of the time and cites incorrect sources up to 60% of the time  When the energy required for a single request from ChatGPT is ten times that of a single Google search and gives you the wrong answer, I question what scenario generative AI is ever better than a traditional search engine  Even if the massive amounts of water and energy AI demands or the art it steals doesn't matter to you, the fact that you are quite likely to receive an incorrect answer should  
Do you really want to support technology that is stealing your data, destroying the environment and stunting your capabilities every time you use it? 
If so, it is time to do some self-reflection 
If not, it is time to stop using AI 
Advertising

headline: COP30 calls for climate action and global bottom-up mobilization
text:
The second letter from the COP30 Presidency delivers two clear messages: to elevate local climate solutions from civil society worldwide, and to update global governance with a new cooperation infrastructure focused on implementing decisions already taken in 30 years of UN climate conferences 
The new letter, released on Thursday (May 8), summarizes its approach in two phrases: "Preparing now for an unpredictable future: calling on science and ancestral wisdom" and "embracing complexity for upgrading cooperation "
On the latter point, the text proposes leaving behind "old bureaucratic models that impair speed and scale" and "hierarchical approaches of the past, based on linear thinking," when climate challenges instead require systems-thinking, decentralization, and distributed action "
The letter clearly said that the goal is not "duplicating the UNFCCC," the structure of the Climate Convention, also known as the UN Climate 
"Our concern is not with negotiation, but with implementation-action," said COP30 President, Ambassador Andr'e Corr^ea do Lago, speaking to reporters  "The structure we have was designed for negotiation  We negotiate, but then we don't see the consequences we expect-the decisions don't translate into action, and that frustrating for many "
"If we are in a climate emergency, with extreme events already happening, and we've already negotiated everything-why don't we see more action?" he added  "Part of the reason is that the UNFCCC and the Paris Agreement lack the mandate and force to carry things forward  What we are proposing is to consider how we can strengthen the institutional foundations of climate implementation "
"This discussion is not about negotiation, which is the UNFCCC role, but about implementation," the COP30 President emphasized  He noted that this debate could unfold at the UN General Assembly in September 
"Without duplicating the UNFCCC, upgraded global governance may additionally combine emerging technologies and ancestral wisdom to introduce a whole new cooperation infrastructure for collective intelligence and exponential results in climate implementation-precisely what we need to win the race against the first climate-induced tipping point," the letter said  It refers to an idea floated by President Lula at the close of the 2024 G20 summit: the possibility of a climate council at the UN, perhaps modeled after the Human Rights Council 
"Our global climate cooperation should not only work for the reality of 2025 but also be ready to evolve to respond to those of 2030, 2035, and 2050  Though particularly daunting in such a complex, fast-changing and deeply uncertain context, this task is no less imperative," the letter added 
Global "Mutir~ao"
In addition to the governance debate, COP30 President Corr^ea do Lago and Ana Toni, the conference CEO and executive director, are promoting a "bottom-up and de-hierarchized" mobilization-emerging from "indigenous and urban afro-descendant peripheric communities or other collectivities "
It the idea of a "Global Mutir~ao," aimed at addressing the climate crisis through real-life initiatives that have already been implemented, are underway, or are about to begin  The presidency believes this local movement can inspire other places and communities  The initiative is rooted in "learning by doing "
"Our climate fight may be suffering from the 'Last War Syndrome'-the tendency to confront new crises with outdated strategies, overlooking shifts in science, technology, and broader political, economic, and social realities," the letter noted 
The second letter gives examples: a group of farmers adopting regenerative agriculture practices with local support; youth-led projects installing solar panels in underserved communities; coastal towns organizing mangrove restoration brigades; tech companies forming coalitions to decarbonize data centers; afro-descendant communities creating climate education programs for cities 
"These actions, though diverse, are united by a common spirit: collective, immediate, and self-driven contributions to a sustainable future," the letter said-an initiative based on learning by doing 
The idea of sharing climate solutions developed around the world will begin at the 2025 UNFCCC Climate Week in Panama, from May 19 to 23  The aim is to gather initiatives on a digital platform 
The letter also outlines four leadership circles that will help broaden the scope of global mobilization: the Circle of Peoples, led by Minister Sonia Guajajara; the Circle of Finance Ministers, chaired by Minister Fernando Haddad; the Circle of COP Presidents, led by Laurent Fabius; and the Global Ethical Stocktake Circle, to be chaired by Minister of the Environment Marina Silva 
The letter does not explicitly mention fossil fuels  "That will be the subject of the next letter," Mr  Corr^ea do Lago said  "But all four leadership circles are addressing the issue of fossil fuels," added Ms  Toni 
(Essa publicac~ao 'e parte integrante do jornal Valor Econ^omico e se sujeita aos termos de uso do site )

headline: Corporations continue to push sustainability; Efforts continue despite federal shift in approach
text:
Despite the recent reversal in the federal funding environment, companies are continuing to drive toward the innovation and adoption of more sustainable processes and products because, they say, that what the market demands 
"Our CEOs identified sustainability as a major market opportunity and market imperative," Baiju Shah, president and CEO of the Greater Cleveland Partnership, told attendees at the 2025 Sustainability Summit held at the Huntington Convention Center on Tuesday, March 4 
"We are here in 2025, once again at our sustainability summit, because it being driven by markets  It being driven by companies  It (being driven by) a market opportunity," he added 
Shah referred to "uncertainty in federal policy," with major grants and subsequent private investment spurred by the U S  Environmental Protection Agency Solar For All program and the Inflation Reduction Act and the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law under fire from the current administration 
Sustainability is just another term for innovation, and businesses are looking for a way to deliver products or services at a cost- competitive basis that is delivered with "a lighter footprint "
"Leading companies know how important it is to embrace this movement because it what their consumers and what their customers, whether they're businesses or individuals, are demanding," Shah said  "And for all companies, it becoming more and more of a competitive advantage "
Food giant makes big moves
Nestle in Solon saved about 5 million pounds of cardboard waste by removing the "crisper sleeve" from its Hot Pocket packaging after more than 30 years 
Adam Graves, Nestle president of pizza and snacking, said the advent of air fryers means consumers use microwaves less and barely use the sleeve at all 
"We changed the crust and we removed this very simple little piece of cardboard," Graves said  "Five million pounds of cardboard is saved by removing this small thing, that scales to a big thing "
Changes like that are not a fad, he said  Cost and waste savings serving as drivers for business decisions, particularly ones that make processes more sustainable, are "here to stay," he said 
Nestle, like many other companies, has committed to a target of having net zero emissions by 2050 
Nestle DiGiorno Frozen pizzas are leading in this area of food chain resiliency, a huge issue in the sustainability field  The Solon- produced pizza is the first to be baked with 100% regenerative wheat, which is grown using a farming method that improves soils and biodiversity 
"We made a commitment about 18 months ago, where we were going to commit to 100,000 acres of regenerative wheat  And we are leading in this space," Graves said 
Energy costs and demands
The economic challenges for companies looking to meet increasing energy demands and incentives for adopting more localized and sustainable energy technologies are also driving sustainability 
World Kinect director of sustainability solutions, Nicole Stika, said in a panel discussion that the solar market recently hit a tipping point where prices have come down significantly, meaning a quicker path to a better return on investment 
"Now it about how fast can these companies get a developer to their site and can they pair a solar project with battery storage for greater reliability and resiliency," Stika said 
Some of her clients are taking "a pause" to see what federal incentives created under the last administration will still be available, but she is not hearing that projects are "off the table altogether 
More tech companies or companies that use a lot of data are using "behind-the-meter" energy to offset rising costs and keep up with increasing demand 
Kevin McGrath, Progressive director of engineering and data center operations, said the business now consumes all the electricity produced with the solar field located on the Mayfield Heights property 
"We took the opportunity to create generation behind the meter because it made sense economically for us," McGrath said about the solar power that runs directly to the adjacent data center  "It is about five acres of our campus, which is not an insignificant amount  The only regret is we don't have more space to do that," he added 
Ohio, he said, has about 170 sunny days per year on average, and with the added number of cold temperature days (which boosts output), the production from the Progressive solar field has exceeded cost-benefit analysis estimates 
When the sun is not shining in Northeast Ohio, rain helps produce Renewable Natural Gas (RNG), methane gas captured from covered landfills 
"We require rainfall for efficient gas collection  Essentially, the moisture that falls on our landfills and permeates into the landfill helps feed the bugs that ultimately decay organic material and great methane," said Ryan Parill, director of origination and business development at WM Renewable Energy LLC 
WM is looking to recover up to 65% of that gas to sell as a renewable energy source but hasn't yet found the technology to achieve that goal  WM is willing to collaborate on the process because the consumer demand for the resiliency of RNG is high 
"Costs change, the environment changes, appetites change, and that allows us to be open to looking at new technologies, ones that perhaps didn't sound like they would make sense three years ago but will in two years," Parnell said

headline: Corvette faces changes ahead, but enthusiasts are confident
text:
As General Motors prepares to launch the fastest, most expensive Corvette ever, the vehicle that J D  Power considers the only real American sports car must adroitly handle some fresh curves in the road ahead "The Corvette is an American icon, an automotive icon, that holds a special place among enthusiasts and the industry itself  It so important to General Motors, even without profit," said Sam Fiorani, vice president of global vehicle forecasting at Auto Forecast Solutions  "But it coming up on a number of issues that are clouding its future "
Among them: Turnover - one retirement that was long expected and two cuts out of the blue - has created a need for new leadership  Also, driving itself is changing: Customers contending with major leaps in electrification technology may feel nostalgic for the human side of driving 
Yet the largest hurdle for Corvette couldn't be clearer: the aging of its target demographic 
Registration data from S&P Global Mobility notes that the over-55 crowd composes the bulk of Corvette ownership, at 59%  Drivers younger than 34, for example, made up 4 6% of Corvette owners last year - the smallest proportion in that age range based on data going back to 2020 
Losses at the top
Tadge Juechter, the Corvette executive chief engineer, retired last summer, after nearly 50 years at the company 
The company also lost two high-profile managers in February after letting go of Corvette product marketing manager Harlan Charles and exterior design manager Kirk Bennion  Both men had decades with the company and were involved in several key launches  Bennion has worked on Corvette exteriors since 1984, according to his LinkedIn profile 
The fruits of their labor heading into production now are expected to yield great results for the auto brand  The Detroit Free Press confirmed an Automotive News report that the Bowling Green Assembly plant - home of Corvette production including the upcoming fastest, most expensive one ever, the ZR1 - was idle for one week in February, two weeks in March and one in May 
The retooling work was likely routine enhancements needed to make high-performance vehicles at this range, experts say  The ZR1 is the first Corvette with what GM calls a "turbocharged" V-8 engine, capable of reaching 233 mph during testing in Germany with the standard configuration 
The Corvette target market
That a lot of speed, much more than many of its customers can handle 
A Corvette "is not an old person car," Fiorani said  "They're not comfortable for somebody who might have arthritis or back pains "
Corvette enthusiasts such as Detroit resident Mahlon Cooks, who retired from a Wayne County job after 30 years, may not be in the market much longer 
Cooks' most recent purchase, an eighth-generation Corvette, may be his last, he told the Free Press  "Heck, I'm 70 years old," he said  "The car is about 4 inches off the ground "
Another issue affecting older buyers can be the changes to the features most drivers come to expect from a Corvette 
While automakers aim to improve vehicles through each product cycle, sometimes the technology requires a bit of time to get used to  That even more important when the vehicle touts such an enormous price tag - the ZR1 starts at $174,995 for the 1LZ coupe and $184,995 for the 1LZ hardtop convertible 
Are cars losing their romance?
Matt Anderson, transportation curator at the Henry Ford Museum in Dearborn, Michigan, said there might be a reason people don't write many songs about cars anymore 
"Young people just aren't interested in them anymore in the way they were maybe 30 years ago  That a concern  Part of that, too, is the cars have become point-and-shoot - you don't engage with the car in any meaningful way anymore," he said  "The car does all the hard work for you "
Some of the roar came out of the Corvette with the hybridization of the E-Ray, and starting with the C7, Corvettes come standard with automatic transmissions, a change that requires some getting used to from the vehicle rabid fan base 
Still, a slow shift toward younger buyers - or at least, fewer older ones - is noted in the data  In 2019, the average age of a Corvette buyer was even older than it is currently, with 62% older than 55, Cox Automotive noted 
Transitions to the mid-engine car created styling and performance benefits that naturally expanded the company customer set, Trevor Thompkins, senior manager for Chevrolet Communications, told the Free Press  In the luxury sports segment, Corvettes are the highest seller among customers under 35 
The dual-clutch automatic transmission is standard on the C8, Thompkins added, meaning drivers can engage with the vehicle gears more directly without losing momentum, and can choose to operate in manual mode 
Corvette typically sells about 30,000 vehicles per year, with notable declines during the Great Recession, according to Automotive News' Data Center  They dipped meaningfully below that line in 2018, to 18,791 vehicles, as Chevrolet wrapped up the seventh-generation Corvette production  The next year was impacted by the 40-day strike against GM that delayed the launch of the 2020 Corvette Stingray, the first mid-engine model  In 2021, the brand rebounded to 33,041 and remains close to that figure 
When people like Cooks started buying Corvettes, "it was a fun car with no practical purpose whatsoever," Anderson said  "Even into the '60s, even '70s, they didn't want the air conditioning or the radio because it wasn't important to the car performance "
Many enthusiasts enjoy the little things, he said, like being able to work on the vehicles themselves or handle how they drive 
"It a tough balance for the automakers to strike," Anderson said  "There no question automatic transmissions they're using are better than any human ever could be, but they're a lot less fun than doing it yourself "
An enthusiast since age 18
Cooks, who has purchased as many as 20 Corvettes in his lifetime, bought his first in 1973 - a maroon third-generation 1969 Corvette  He was 18 years old 
"It was a different time  A beautiful high-performance car, it was probably $2,700," he said  "It was wonderful  I worked every day so I had no problem owning it "
Today, he has four: a 1977 C3, a 1999 C5, a 2014 C7 and a 2023 C8 
In all his years of driving them, the jump from the C7 to the C8 has been most pronounced due to the mid-engine innovation, the most radical engineering change since the first Corvette debuted in 1953  The change had been teased for years and finally unveiled in 2020 when the team pushed back the power from under the hood and tucked it between the passenger compartment and the rear wheels  Moving the engine back increased stability and balance, allowing the vehicle to safely handle faster and faster speeds 
The neutral balance of the C8 is incredibly smooth - dangerously so, Cooks said 
"I'm having trouble feeling what it doing  With my other Corvettes, you could read it some," Cooks said  "    You could be going 125 mph, and it doesn't do anything  It takes you into a curve no problem at all, but when you're coming out of it, you've got to drive the car "
No surprise, Cooks prefers a stick shift  But he said he is getting used to the automatic 
"I keep joking with my wife, we've got to end up buying an electric car eventually, but I've already owned a lot of old cars," he said  "I can't imagine owning anything other than a combustion engine "
According to experts, it a question of when, rather than if, an all-electric Corvette rolls off an assembly line  Estimates from Automotive News' Future Product Pipeline posit that change could come as early as 2028 
Big fan: 'Not one bit concerned'
Any concerns over the Corvette future are not shared by the largest Corvette collector in the world, famed NASCAR team owner Rick Hendrick 
Hendrick, who owns Hendrick Automotive Group in Charlotte, North Carolina, is an avid Corvette collector with 150 unique and rare Corvettes  His collection is housed at his 58,000-square-foot garage called the Heritage Center, located about 900 miles from Michigan in Concord, North Carolina 
Hendrick, who paid a record $3 7 million to own the first retail ZR1 that rolls off the line, has been a Chevy dealer for more than 50 years and said he can't recall a time when he has seen this much interest from customers in a Corvette as they are in the ZR1 
"I've had hundreds of people try to get in line for a car  I'm not taking orders because it'll be so slow at getting them out," Hendrick said, noting the engines are hand-built, and it has several modifications that make production complex 
"I've spoken to the board before and been in those meetings, I am not one bit concerned about the future of the Corvette," Hendrick said 
Tyson Jominy, vice president of data and analytics at J D  Power, told the Free Press that an aging buyer population is hardly unusual for the exotics, and Corvette recent product updates actually orient the brand for a smoother road amid ever-evolving consumer preferences, production costs and regulatory environment 
"Certainly, the electrification of the industry stands to get in the way of the reason most of these sports cars exist, the visceral side of driving," Jominy said  "If it just about speed, electric vehicles are going to beat the internal combustion engine in almost every situation  But Corvette is taking a unique approach with the E-Ray; they're electrifying but leaving the best parts there "
The V8 engine, the exhaust sound - Corvette is slow-walking its customer base to the future, Jominy said, bringing along those nostalgic features most likely to tug at the eardrums of devoted customers 
"It shows they're not going to sit still  No one needs a $100,000 sports car  It emotional, and probably from a promise you made to yourself when you're younger in life," Jominy said  "All respect to the king here  It probably the one best positioned to adapt to the times "
'Most popular exotic car on the planet'
After bringing back the famed Stingray name, Corvette current lineup of the E-Ray, Z06 and the ZR1 combine high performance design with affordable pricing options that belie the vehicle outstanding reputation among buyers and critics 
"It is the most popular exotic car on the planet," Fiorani said  "When you compare it to the likes of a Ferrari or Lamborghini, they'll sell as many Corvettes in the U S  as Ferrari will sell in a year "
Last year, around 4,000 Ferraris were sold in the Americas, which includes the United States, Canada, Mexico, the Caribbean and Central and South America, compared with 33,330 Corvettes in the United States alone 
Hendrick said he has great faith in the brand future because Ken Morris, GM senior vice president of product programs, product safety and motorsports; and Mark Reuss, GM president, are hands-on when it comes to product development 
"They won't let a Corvette out of there unless it sounds right  You've got all these engineers, but (Reuss) is the one who is going to sign off," Hendrick said  "These two guys are tremendously involved in the sign-off to ensure that it handles, it performs, and it bulletproof "
Room to hit the road
Emery Burk, a retired university police officer, takes lengthy road trips with Cooks and several fellow Corvette owners multiple times a year  He has had two so far - the C7 and a C8 - and said that as soon as someone who wants to get a Corvette can afford it, they do 
He pined for almost 20 years before purchasing his first Corvette at the urging of his wife, who saw the picture he kept pinned to the refrigerator - blue with a white top - while their four children grew up 
When they left the nest, he finally pulled the trigger 
Asked what he expects a new Corvette to be, Burk paused before answering  "Well, gosh, I'm getting old, so I don't know if faster is the right thing to say  You can only drive them so fast, plus you got the police out there who might decide to stop you," he said  "But at least you know the speed is under the hood, even if you don't use it "
Graphic
 
A 1963 Chevrolet Corvette shines at Detroit Autorama in March  The automaker faces challenges as the target demographic ages 
Eric Seals/usa today network

headline: Could data centers be coming to the Idaho-Oregon border?
text:
Idaho Power is working on a project that could build data centers on the Idaho-Oregon border 
The Snake River Economic Development Alliance, which provides economic development assistance to the Idaho counties of Payette and Washington as well as Oregon Malheur County, is working on the project with Idaho Power, according to the minutes of its April meeting  The goal is to identify and submit information on potential sites that are or are almost shovel-ready for small- to medium-sized data centers 
Idaho Power did not respond to inquiries by press time  The project is reportedly being spearheaded by Megan Ronk, director of business innovation & development, previously Idaho Director of Commerce under Gov  C  L  "Butch" Otter  During her tenure at Commerce, the department started efforts to pass a bill giving sales tax breaks to data centers to entice them to locate in Idaho  The bill finally passed last year 
"Our rates and power availability are one of Idaho great strengths," Ronk said in a 2018 presentation sponsored by the Boise Metro Chamber of Commerce, adding that Idaho had the fourth-lowest large commercial rates in the country  Idaho Power was particularly interested in getting data center projects started because the company believes that it will have a power surplus until 2026, she said at the time 
One of the sites under consideration is the Ontario Treasure 200 site, which has been selected to receive a site visit from an unnamed site selection company based out of Chicago, according to the minutes  "This company has a strong relationship with data centers as well as with Business Oregon Jill Miles (Recruitment Officer)," the minutes noted  "They have been retained by Idaho Power to assist in marketing the properties for data centers  "
Idaho Power services the Treasure 200 site, and has promoted its suitability for data centers on its website 
The city of Ontario did not respond to inquiries by press time 
Kit Kamo, executive director of the SREDA, said in an email message that she couldn't provide more details because it was an Idaho Power project, but indicated that unspecified sites on the Idaho side of the border were being considered as well 
Proponents of the data center sales tax exemption bill said the tax break was more than made up by revenue such as property tax, corporate income tax, taxes paid by employees and sales taxes on other equipment  Data centers also tend to improve broadband infrastructure in areas where they're situated, supporters said 
All the states surrounding Idaho either had such a sales tax exemption or no sales tax at all, such as Oregon  Supporters of the bill said that why Idaho didn't have the large data centers those states have attracted, despite its low power rates 
"Data centers bring not only high-end jobs and tons of capital investment, but they are a boon for local small businesses that service them (i e  HVAC companies, landscapers, security companies, etc )," Bill Connors, president and CEO of the Boise Metro Chamber, said last year  "Finally, (there is) the realization that these things won't be going into downtown urban areas, but will likely be great opportunities for more rural areas of the state  "
The Boise Metro Chamber, along with Idaho Power, had promoted the issue since at least 2017  The bill struggled because some legislators didn't like the idea of giving tax breaks to companies, particularly giants like Facebook and Google that are thought to be well able to afford to pay taxes 
But a 2019 informational hearing before the Idaho Legislature appeared to turn the tide  Presenters included Stephen Thomas, a partner with Hawley Troxell; and Steve DelBianco, president, and Barbara Comstock, advisor, to NetChoice, a Washington trade association representing eCommerce businesses and online consumers  They painted a rosy picture of data centers' impact, such as a 2015 study indicating 43,000 jobs, $3 2 billion in labor income with wages averaging $100,000 a year and $10 billion in economic output 
While critics said data centers aren't a good investment because they only create about 300 jobs once constructed, Comstock noted the low number of employees meant data centers didn't put pressure on infrastructure such as roads and schools

headline: COUNTERPOINT
text:
Aaron Brown April 21 column "Capital for companies, consequences for us," about Allete/Minnesota Power pending purchase by the Canadian pension fund and Global Infrastructure Partners, carries weight because Aaron is an important voice for northern Minnesota  But I dissent that the risks of the sale outweigh the benefits, and I urge Minnesota state agencies to approve the purchase plan 
Minnesota Power - which serves northeastern Minnesota - is doing a lot of things right, and folks like Aaron are concerned whether that will continue under new ownership  Some large industrials on the Iron Range are concerned that change is too fast, and some environmental groups fear a new owner will slow down the energy transition  But I believe that the sale of its parent company Allete is definitely in the public interest 
In 2007, when the first renewable-electricity standard gained support of Gov  Tim Pawlenty and 90% of legislators, Minnesota Power had no renewable energy except the hydro dam at Jay Cooke State Park  At that time, Minnesota Power fuel mix was 95% coal  By 2030, its fuel mix will be 80% wind and solar and batteries, supported by energy efficiency and transmission and managing electricity loads  It is the only Minnesota utility that has exceeded state energy efficiency goals 15 years in a row  This is one of the most remarkable clean-energy transitions of any utility in America 
In January 2023, when the 100% clean-electricity standard was ready for debate in the Minnesota Legislature, Minnesota Power did not oppose it  Instead, it offered improvements to the bill to assure continued reliability and affordability for customers  The very moment the 2040 carbon-free-electricity bill was signed into law, Minnesota Power needed a new owner  Practically speaking, the small utility balance sheet was not able to handle all the new investment required 
But Minnesota Power parent company did not just put itself up for sale on the auction block  The board and management sought out two strategic partners they admired and could work with: the pension funds of Canadian public employees, the very definition of a long-term public-interest investor; and Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP), a unit of BlackRock  GIP is one of the largest and most sophisticated infrastructure investors in the world 
As big a task as Minnesota Power faces in winding down its coal fleet to create an all-new sustainable supply, the challenge ahead is much greater than that 
The electric sector is also being called upon for additional needs obvious to all well-informed people: Transport and buildings must electrify for energy security and the climate imperative  That could double the amount of electricity needed over the next decade or two  On top of that, new industries such as better iron mining, and potentially green iron or steel production on the Range, plus new data centers, could drive a tripling of electricity loads or even more  Both these industries of the future need abundant carbon-free electricity  Northern Minnesota is ready to do big things again, including on-shoring industries, jump-starting hydrogen-based industries and electrifying everything we can 
Minnesota Power current management will stay in place, committed to place and to culture  CEO Bethany Owen is one of America few women utility CEOs, and she has gender balance on her board and senior management  With this sale, she brings exactly what northern Minnesota needs - top-level expertise and deep pockets in infrastructure  Everyone wants shared prosperity and abundance for northern Minnesota 
The company will be regulated by Minnesota excellent regulators just as it is now, with transparency on costs and earnings, and just and reasonable rates  If Minnesota Power new owners want to make more money, they will have to help grow industries of the future on the Iron Range and in the Arrowhead  Over the past decades, electricity use in northern Minnesota has been flat, but new clean electricity will be the engine for a better future  The watchdogs in the state agencies will look at this deal with a steely eye, but a Minnesota electric utility in the north can help Minnesota do big things - and Minnesota Power with a new ownership group is that utility 
Michael Noble is an energy and environment consultant to industry, government and philanthropy

headline: County Council members clash over approach to data center siting and design
text:
As the clock runs out on an executive order preventing land in Frederick County from being rezoned for potential data center development, County Council members remain at odds about how to regulate the growing industry 
The council on Tuesday held a workshop on a bill from Council President Brad Young and Council Member Renee Knapp based on legislation proposed, then pulled, by County Executive Jessica Fitzwater administration earlier this year 
But the workshop on Knapp and Young bill was at times dominated by discussion about a different bill drafted by Council Members Steve McKay and Jerry Donald, which they say better reflects the wishes of a work group on data centers that was convened by Fitzwater and chaired by Knapp 
In an interview on Wednesday, Young said the two bills are so different it would be confusing to workshop both at the same time 
Young said he intends to add McKay and Donald proposal to the council agenda only after the bill currently on the table has made its way through the legislative process 
McKay said on Tuesday that he respected Young authority as president to set the council agenda, but it a bad decision to hold off on scheduling his and Donald bill 
McKay made a motion to add his and Donald bill to the council agenda earlier than Young suggested, but that motion failed by a vote of 4-3 
McKay and Donald, along with Council Member Mason Carter, supported the motion  Young, Knapp and Council Members M C  Keegan-Ayer and Kavont'e Duckett were opposed 
Knapp said on Tuesday she did not feel that simultaneously debating two bills that are in conflict with one another would be efficient 
"This bill deserves to be considered by its own merit, as does Council Member McKay," Knapp said, adding that the council needs to be mindful of the deadline for the executive order 
McKay argued that the impending deadline "is even more reason that we should be having a fuller discussion in these next few months "
"Maybe after going through it, we find that we can bridge these things together - that actually my hope," McKay said  "But we can't if we're not talking about it actively "
Young said on Wednesday that his and Knapp goal is to craft legislation that puts some additional guide rails on the data center industry, while still garnering enough support to pass 
Young said the bill McKay attempted to add to the agenda does not accomplish this, as evidenced by the outcome of the vote on Tuesday 
"He can no longer say that I'm trying to quash anything, which I wasn't," he said 
REGULATORY ENVIRONMENT
Under the current county code, data center projects are allowed "by right" on land zoned Limited Industrial (LI) and General Industrial (GI) 
That means plans to build data centers there will be approved by default, so long they meet design requirements and other regulations attached to the zone 
In 2022, the council passed a resolution adding critical digital infrastructure facilities and critical digital infrastructure substations to the zoning code as distinct uses 
The 2022 resolution included some design requirements for data centers such as restrictions on noise, vibration, building height and lighting  However, it did not address common elements of data centers such as fuel storage and generators 
In June 2023, Fitzwater convened a data center work group  Among other things, the work group was tasked with examining appropriate locations for data centers in Frederick County and recommending potential changes to the existing data center regulations 
At the same time, Fitzwater signed an executive order directing the county Division of Planning and Permitting not to accept, consider, or process applications to change the zoning of individual parcels of land to LI or GI 
The executive order was set to terminate on March 30, 2024, but Fitzwater opted to extend it while her administration worked with the council on legislation to tighten siting and design criteria for data centers 
In September 2024, Fitzwater announced that her administration would introduce to the council two pieces of legislation - one to establish a critical digital infrastructure floating zone and another concerning design requirements for data centers 
Floating zones are districts that, when applied, replace the underlying zoning of a specific parcel of land 
In its final report, the data centers work group recommended the creation of a floating zone that would have to be applied by the County Council over qualified parcels before data centers could be developed there 
The floating zone that the work group recommended could only be applied over parcels that were zoned for light industrial or general industrial use and that met a host of other criteria, such as access to water and sewer service and proximity to high-voltage power transmission lines 
The recommended floating zone could not have been applied over special planning areas such as agricultural preservation areas or the Sugarloaf Treasured Landscape Management Area 
After workshopping the floating zone and design bills in October and November 2024, respectively, Fitzwater administration decided to do away with concept of a floating zone, about which she had previously expressed reservations 
The result was a bill that combined elements of the floating zone bill and the design bill - keeping the siting criteria as originally proposed, but removing the council discretion to approve or not approve specific data center projects 
Fitzwater combined bill was scheduled to be introduced at a meeting on Jan  7, but the council voted to table it over concerns that substantive changes had been made without explanation 
Fitzwater on Jan  16 sent a letter to Young stating that, while she expected the council to exercise its prerogative to amend her bill, she did not expect the council to delay "perhaps indefinitely, any action whatsoever" on the data center issue 
Fitzwater also said she did not intend to extend her executive order on piecemeal rezonings beyond its current expiration date of April 8 
"I urge the Council to be conscious of that timeline as you consider your next steps," she wrote 
NEW LEGISLATION
The bill from Knapp and Young closely mirrors the combined bill the Fitzwater administration planned to introduce last month, but includes a few changes based on feedback the council received from the public 
One change would be moving from a complaint-driven process for noise and vibration, in which requirements would be enforced whenever officials received credible evidence of a violation, to a process based on periodic reporting 
Knapp and Young also raised the maximum allowable size of above-ground fuel-storage tanks - from 1,100 gallons to 20,000 gallons - and provided for the use of generators that are not certified as meeting the U S  Environmental Protection Agency latest emissions standards, but are designed to be equivalent 
McKay and Donald bill is similar to the pulled administration bill in its design requirements, but differs on siting in that it includes a floating zone with criteria that go beyond the data center work group recommendations 
To qualify for the floating zone proposed by McKay and Donald, a data center developer would have to demonstrate that a project would minimize the use of potable water for cooling 
The developer would also have to demonstrate that sufficient electrical capacity to support the data center already exists within the local and regional grid, could be generated on the site through means other than diesel generators used for back-up power, or some combination of the two 
The need for additional land zoned for critical digital infrastructure would also be a factor in granting or denying applications for the floating zone proposed by McKay and Donald 
The McKay and Donald bill diverges slightly from the data center work group recommendation on proximity to high-voltage transmission lines, instead requiring that data centers be located within two miles of an existing high-voltage electrical substation 
According to Young, he intends to add his and Knapp bill to the council agenda for introduction at next week meeting 
McKay and Donald bill will still be put on the council agenda for a workshop, at the discretion of council leadership, Young said

headline: County Council nears vote on amended data center bill; will soon consider overlay zone
text:
The Frederick County Council on Tuesday is set to vote on new data center siting and design criteria that have been in the works for nearly a year 
The new criteria, contained in a bill sponsored by Council President Brad Young and Council Member Renee Knapp, would build on a 2022 ordinance that added critical digital infrastructure facilities and substations to the list of permitted uses in the county general and limited industrial zones 
The Knapp and Young bill would further restrict data center development within those zones; add guidance on diesel generators, lighting and other design elements; and require data center operators to take corrective action if noise or vibration from the facilities exceed certain levels 
Stakeholders in the data center industry have been supportive of the Knapp and Young bill with a handful of amendments, some of which the council approved at a meeting this month 
Opponents of the bill have said the initial draft did not go far enough to protect county residents from the negative impacts of data centers, and the amended version is even more favorable to the industry 
At a Frederick County Planning Commission hearing in April, multiple industry stakeholders requested that data centers and associated infrastructure for which site plans had been submitted to the Division of Planning and Permitting by the effective date of the Knapp and Young bill be exempted from the new criteria 
Initially, only data centers and associated infrastructure that had received conditional or final site plan approval by the effective date of the Knapp and Young bill would have been exempt from the new criteria 
The amendment to the site-plan requirement was introduced on May 6 by Council Vice President Kavont'e Duckett and Council Member Mason Carter 
It was approved by 5-2, with Council Members Young, Knapp, Duckett, Carter and M C  Keegan-Ayer in the majority  Council Members Steve McKay and Jerry Donald were opposed 
Other amendments approved by the council on May 6 would:
* reduce the frequency of required noise and vibration monitoring from twice a year to every two years
* allow data centers and associated infrastructure to abut land zoned for residential use as long as the facilities are at least 200 yards away from the property line in all directions
* add colleges and universities to the list of places for which the impacts of data centers and associated infrastructure must be avoided or minimized "to the maximum extent practicable"
* clarify that any new criteria for mechanical equipment also applies to electrical equipment
* The electrical equipment amendment was suggested by the Planning Commission, which voted 4-2 against recommending Knapp and Young bill before it went through the amendment process 
The council also rejected a number of proposed amendments on May 6, including amendments that would have:
* required noise and vibration testing prior to, rather than within six months of, occupancy of a data center building
* required sound testing along all property lines if the county receives credible evidence that a data center or associated infrastructure is violating noise limits
* decreased the maximum height of a data center facility from 75 feet to 60 feet, as well as increased the minimum required lot and yard size
* stated that data center facilities must be located within two miles of a high-voltage electrical substation, rather than within two miles of high-voltage electric transmission lines
* required that all site plans for data centers and associated infrastructure be evaluated by the county Department of Energy and Environment
The rejected proposal to require site plan review by the Energy and Environment Department also was recommended by the Planning Commission 
In an interview on Monday, Young said he is confident that his and Knapp bill has enough support to pass  If the council does pass the measure, it would take effect after 90 days 
As of Monday afternoon, Young said he had not received any additional proposed amendments, but he would not be surprised if some are put forward ahead of the final vote on Tuesday 
Once the council has voted on the data center siting and design bill, it will begin its work on an overlay zone that would restrict data center development to the area around the former Alcoa Eastalco Works site near Adamstown 
Several data center buildings have already been approved for the 2,200-acre site, which is now being managed by Catellus Development Corporation 
Young on Monday said he expects the overlay bill to be fast-tracked, given that it is being sponsored by all seven council members  Still, it will have to go through the usual public process for new legislation 
Legislative Director Ragen Cherney on Monday said the County Council is scheduled to workshop the data center overlay bill on May 27  The measure would then be formally introduced on June 3 
After the overlay zone is created through legislation, the County Council will have to separately approve a map of the overlay, which could include other properties in the area of the former Alcoa Eastalco Works site

headline: Court limits environmental impact statements' reach
text:
'A course correction of sorts is appropriate'
WASHINGTON - The Supreme Court on Thursday sharply limited the reach of environmental impact statements in a victory for developers 
The justices said these claims of the potential impact on the environment have been used too often to delay or block new projects 
"A 1970 legislative acorn has grown over the years into a judicial oak that has hindered infrastructure development under the guise of just a little more process  A course correction of sorts is appropriate," Justice Brett M  Kavanaugh said for the court 
He said procedural law has given judges and environmentalists too much authority to hinder or prevent development, he said 
"Fewer projects make it to the finish line  Indeed, fewer projects make it to the starting line  Those that survive often end up costing much more than is anticipated or necessary," he said  "And that in turn means fewer and more expensive railroads, airports, wind turbines, transmission lines, dams, housing developments, highways, bridges, subways, stadiums, arenas, data centers, and the like  And that also means fewer jobs, as new projects become difficult to finance and build in a timely fashion "
The decision could loom large in California and the West because the 9th Circuit Court of Appeals has taken a broad view of environmental protection and the scope of impact statements 
The National Environmental Policy Act of 1970s was the first of a series of landmark environmental laws  It required federal agencies to prepare a report assessing the likely impact of projects that will be funded or approved by the government 
In a unanimous decision, the high court ruled for the developers of a proposed 88-mile railroad in Utah, a spur line which could carry crude oil that would be refined along the Gulf Coast 
The project needed the approval of the U S  Surface Transportation Board which produced 3,600 pages of analysis on the potential impact 
In blocking the proposal, the D C  Circuit Court of Appeals cited its potential to spur more drilling for oil in Utah and more pollution along the Gulf Coast  The judges said these "upstream" and "downstream" impacts of the railroad must be considered before the new rail line is approved 
Seven counties that favored the development appealed to the high court and argued that the potential environment impact should be limited to the building of the railroad itself  The court agreed

headline: Coveted territory
text:
Remote, icy and mostly pristine, Greenland plays an outsized role in the daily weather experienced by billions of people and in the climate changes taking shape all over the planet 
Greenland is where climate change, scarce resources, tense geopolitics and new trade patterns all intersect, said Ohio University security and environment professor Geoff Dabelko 
The world largest island is now "central to the geopolitical, geoeconomic competition in many ways," partly because of climate change, Dabelko said 
Since his first term in office, President-elect Donald Trump has expressed interest in acquiring Greenland, which is a semiautonomous territory of Denmark, a longtime U S  ally and a founding member of NATO  It is also home to a large U S  military base 
Why is Greenland coveted?
Think of Greenland as an open refrigerator door or thermostat for a warming world, and it in a region that is warming four times faster than the rest of the globe, said New York University climate scientist David Holland 
Locked inside are valuable rare earth minerals needed for telecommunications as well as uranium, billions of untapped barrels of oil and a vast supply of natural gas that used to be inaccessible but is becoming less so 
Many of the same minerals are currently being supplied mostly by China, so other countries such as the United States are interested, Dabelko said  Three years ago, the Denmark government suspended oil development offshore from the territory of 57,000 people 
But more than the oil, gas or minerals, there ice - a "ridiculous" amount, said climate scientist Eric Rignot of the University of California, Irvine 
If that ice melts, it would reshape coastlines across the globe and potentially shift weather patterns in such a dramatic manner that the threat was the basis of a Hollywood disaster movie  Greenland holds enough ice that if it all melts, the world seas would rise by 24 feet  Nearly a foot of that is so-called zombie ice, already doomed to melt no matter what happens, a 2022 study found 
Since 1992, Greenland has lost about 182 billion tons of ice each year, with losses hitting 489 billion tons a year in 2019 
Greenland will be "a key focus point" through the 21st century because of the effect its melting ice sheet will have on sea levels, said Mark Serreze, director of the National Snow and Ice Data Center in Boulder, Colorado  "It will likely become a bigger contributor in the future "
That impact is "perhaps unstoppable," NYU Holland said 
Are other climate factors at play?
Greenland also serves as the engine and on/off switch for a key ocean current that influences Earth climate in many ways, including hurricane and winter storm activity  It called the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation, or AMOC, and it slowing down because more fresh water is being dumped into the ocean by melting ice in Greenland, Serreze said 
A shutdown of the AMOC conveyor belt is a much-feared climate tipping point that could plunge Europe and parts of North America into prolonged freezes, a scenario depicted in the 2004 movie "The Day After Tomorrow "
"If this global current system were to slow substantially or even collapse altogether - as we know it has done in the past - normal temperature and precipitation patterns around the globe would change drastically," said climate scientist Jennifer Francis of the Woodwell Climate Research Center  "Agriculture would be derailed, ecosystems would crash, and 'normal' weather would be a thing of the past "
Greenland is also changing color as it melts from the white of ice, which reflects sunlight, heat and energy away from the planet, to the blue and green of the ocean and land, which absorb much more energy, Holland said 
Greenland plays a role in the dramatic freeze that two-thirds of the United States is currently experiencing  And back in 2012, weather patterns over Greenland helped steer Superstorm Sandy into New York and New Jersey, according to winter weather expert Judah Cohen of the private firm Atmospheric and Environmental Research 
Because of Greenland mountains of ice, it also changes patterns in the jet stream, which brings storms across the globe and dictates daily weather 
Often, especially in winter, a blocking system of high pressure off Greenland causes Arctic air to plunge to the west and east, smacking North America and Europe, Cohen said 
Why is Greenland location so important?
Because it straddles the Arctic Circle between the United States, Russia and Europe, Greenland is a geopolitical prize that the U S  and others have eyed for more than 150 years  It even more valuable as the Arctic opens up more to shipping and trade 
None of that takes into consideration the unique look of the ice-covered island that has some of the Earth oldest rocks 
"I see it as insanely beautiful  It eye-watering to be there," said Holland, who has conducted research on the ice more than 30 times since 2007  "Pieces of ice the size of the Empire State Building are just crumbling off cliffs and crashing into the ocean  And also, the beautiful wildlife, all the seals and the killer whales  It just breathtaking "

headline: Cox details tech community's role in addressing Utah housing, energy needs
text:
Logan Stefanich, KSL com
Utah Gov  Spencer Cox speaks during a keynote at the 2025 Silicon Slopes Summit in Orem on Wednesday  Cox thinks the state vibrant tech community can help navigate some problems facing Utah 
1
Like any state experiencing rapid growth, Utah has a list of demands it has to meet to ensure a prosperous future 
Luckily, Utah Gov  Spencer Cox thinks the state vibrant technology community can help navigate some of these problems 
"The one area where we are really good at building is in the tech world  We're leading the world, still, in this space," Cox said Wednesday during the 2025 Silicon Slopes Summit in Orem  "So many great ideas are happening in the virtual world and the tech world  We're not as good at building things in the real world "
Some of these things, Cox said, are housing and energy infrastructure 
Following the June Republican primary debate for governor, Cox said his biggest regret from his first term was his inability to do more on what he says is his top issue: housing affordability 
"This is the single most important issue in our state," Cox said 
In 2023, Cox appointed Steve Waldrip as the first senior adviser for housing strategy 
This was followed by a litany of of housing bills passed in 2024 seeking to simplify regulations, subsidize homeownership and incentivize starter-home construction with the goal of building 35,000 homes in five years  However, Waldrip said the fruit of these efforts "is many years off "
"This is pure economics 101  It is supply and demand  Demand is high, supply is low (and) prices go up," Cox said  "We can either decrease demand, which is what the Fed is doing by raising interest rates, which isn't really decreasing demand     or we can build more houses," Cox said 
He noted that one of the reason Utah Silicon Slopes revolution happened was because Silicon Valley "became unattainable for far too many people "
To ensure future prosperity for Utah tech ecosystem, Cox said the state needs to achieve prosperity in other areas, like housing 
Another area mentioned was energy, with Cox saying there an energy crisis in the U S 
He talked about the need for data centers across the country and the incredibly high amount of energy the centers demand  When a data center reached out to Utah, Cox said it would've required 1 4 gigawatts of power  Cox did not identify the data center  For some comparison, the entire state of Utah runs on 4 gigawatts of power 
"We stopped building power plants in this country in meaningful ways  We have the technology capability to do these things; we've just gotten very stupid about it  It time we stop being dumb and actually start unleashing the potential of building in our country and in the state of Utah," Cox said 
Building - whether it new houses, power plants or data centers - requires permits 
On Jan  9, Cox announced an executive order requiring state environmental agencies to streamline the permitting process for infrastructure projects  It was the first executive order of his second term 
The executive order instructs the Utah Department of Environmental Quality and the Division of Oil, Gas and Mining in the Utah Department of Natural Resources to identify ways to expand permit review processes that allow projects to get started more quickly 
"The idea is, if you want to build something, you have to get government permits, you know, clean air permits, local building permits, all of those different things  How can we do that faster?" Cox said 
An example, Cox said, was if a company was seeking to build a power plant in Utah  In Cox perfect world, Utah could grant a plant-wide permit, meaning the company wouldn't have to get a different permit for "every single piece of equipment "
"We're just trying to streamline and make things easier while still protecting the environment (and) doing the things that everybody cares about," Cox said 
At the end of the day, Cox said Utah DNA of being giving and community-focused is what separates it from other tech hubs like Silicon Valley, and it also why he optimistic Utah can continue to grow and lead when it comes to sectors like tech 
"If we want Utah to stay unique, if we want Utah to stay the best place in the world to live and play and raise a family and build a business, we have to take care of each other, and we need your help in doing that," Cox told the crowd
