headline: Decarbonization leads nuclear energy to overcome resistance
text:
Mayara Gerevini gestures toward a pair of yellow underpants-part of the old factory uniform-and explains the symbolism: "Back then, no women worked at nuclear power plants " The year was 1978, and the Zwentendorf nuclear facility, located 58 kilometers from Vienna, had just been completed  After four years of construction, growing public opposition prompted Austrian Prime Minister Bruno Kreisky to call a referendum on whether the $1 billion plant should be activated  
Minister Kreisky, a Social Democrat with backing from most trade unions and the business community, raised the stakes by pledging to resign if voters opposed the plant  The gamble didn't pay off  By a margin of fewer than 30,000 votes-or just 0 94%-Austrians decided against nuclear energy  Minister Kreisky remained in office, but he couldn't stop Parliament from enshrining a ban on nuclear power in the Constitution  Today, Austria sources around 60% of its electricity from hydroelectric power, 26% from other renewables, and 14% from fossil fuels 
Mayara wasn't born yet  Her father, Marcelo, a S~ao Paulo native and professional beach volleyball player, had not yet moved to Austria in the 1990s, where he eventually became an adventure sports instructor  Now, Mayara is responsible for guiding tours through the Zwentendorf plant, which never went into operation and technically isn't even a museum  Though the reactor was once loaded with uranium, it was ultimately returned to its manufacturer, Siemens  
Today, solar panels stretch across the grounds of this quiet corner along the Danube River, accessible by a road built four meters above the waterline  The adjacent fields now host music festivals  With a dedicated fan page on Facebook, the plant attracts 15,000 visitors annually-including journalists from 20 media outlets across four continents, among them Valor, which visited in late May 
The initiative behind the visit sheds light on the story of its organizer-the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)  Established in 1957 and headquartered in Vienna, the agency operates out of a sprawling complex that employs 2,300 people  Often referred to as the nuclear energy "watchdog" by the international press, the IAEA is one of the United Nations' most autonomous bodies  Yet its leadership, especially Managing Director Rafael Grossi, a 64-year-old Argentine now in his sixth year at the helm, firmly rejects any characterization of the agency as a lobbyist for the sector 
Given the IAEA extensive portfolio-from endorsing national nuclear programs and monitoring radiotherapy to sterilizing Aedes aegypti mosquitoes and tackling banana pests-its caution around perceptions of lobbying seems well-placed  The agency reach now extends far beyond its original mandate  Although only nine countries possess nuclear weapons, the IAEA is active in 40 nations and oversees 100 projects  
Mr  Grossi, who became interested in nuclear issues early in his career while working on the bilateral nuclear treaty between Brazil and Argentina, has built what some describe as a "UN within the UN" in Vienna  The COVID-19 pandemic further broadened the agency scope  At the height of the crisis, Mr  Grossi gathered his department heads and posed a single question: "What can the agency do?" This led to the deployment of PCR testing kits developed by the nuclear sector to detect the virus in real time using nuclear technology 
Despite the IAEA expanding portfolio, its original mandate remains its most powerful  From the embattled Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant in Russian-occupied Ukraine to Tehran, where Mr  Grossi maintains direct contact with Iranian Minister Abbas Araghchi, and even to Russian President Vladimir Putin office, Mr  Grossi has emerged as the sole international figure maintaining open lines of communication with all major players in today geopolitical flashpoints  
The most tense of these is Iran nuclear program  Tensions spiked on the last day of May when a confidential IAEA report-submitted to its 35-member Board of Governors, drawn from the agency 180 member states-was leaked  The report revealed a 50% surge in Iran uranium enrichment since February  The country now possesses an estimated 408 6 kilograms of uranium enriched to 60%  By comparison, a nuclear bomb requires roughly 42 kilograms enriched to 90%  The disclosure drew an immediate response from Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who called for urgent action amid the ongoing diplomatic stalemate between Washington D C  and Tehran  
Mr  Grossi activism underscores the enduring relevance of multilateralism at a moment when former U S  President Donald Trump is attempting to dismantle it-from global trade, where his tariffs exposed the World Trade Organization (WTO) inability to rein him in, to peace mediation, which has suffered from the increasingly limited influence of United Nations Secretary-General Ant'onio Guterres 
The IAEA Managing Director ability to remain equidistant from major geopolitical conflicts-a key asset in his bid to succeed Mr  Guterres-is under constant strain  For his candidacy, Mr  Grossi has the backing of Argentine President Javier Milei, Trump closest ally in South America  Yet he also faces scrutiny over Taiwan nuclear program, which Beijing alleges is being developed with U S  support 
The broad range of nuclear applications and the IAEA role in mediating funding for nuclear initiatives have brought the agency into closer alignment with global industrial powerhouses  In December, it will further expand its influence by hosting a high-level seminar in Vienna focused on the tech sector  The event will bring together major players from an industry whose data centers are emerging as a key source of demand for small modular reactors (SMRs) 
This technology could usher nuclear energy into the race to supply power for data processing-an arena in which Brazil hopes to take a leading role, leveraging its status as one of the world top producers of renewable energy  During a recent U S  congressional hearing, Senator Marco Rubio cited Brazil purchase of surplus power from the Itaipu Dam as a compelling reason for American tech companies to establish operations in Paraguay 
Google and Amazon have already begun investing in SMR projects-technology that only Russia has fully mastered to date  The nuclear threat today looms not just over flashpoints like Ukraine, Iran, the India-Pakistan border, and North Korea-countries outside the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty that possess atomic weapons-but also through the rising global deployment of small reactors 
While SMRs present a lower individual risk than large-scale nuclear plants, their broader distribution introduces more frequent potential hazards  The threat is not solely military; the environmental impact is also significant  Their growing association with data centers stems from these facilities' enormous consumption of energy and water-both needed to cool reactors and data operations alike  
This risk is not exclusive to small reactors  Cear'a, the Brazilian state most aggressive in attracting data centers, also leads in wind and solar energy production  Although these renewable sources are far less water-intensive than nuclear power, few states in Brazil suffer from droughts as severe as those experienced in Cear'a 
This isn't the only source of tension with environmentalists  The global climate-meteorological and political-is no longer what it was when Austria enshrined a ban on nuclear power in its Constitution  Nor does it resemble the climate of 2011, when the Fukushima disaster in Japan prompted then-German Chancellor Angela Merkel to propose phasing out the country nuclear plants, a process that concluded in 2023  Since then, Germany has become increasingly reliant on Russian gas-and, after the war in Ukraine began, on gas from the Middle East and the United States, as well as nuclear energy imported from France  In the final week of May, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz told the French he was no longer opposed to classifying nuclear power as "green " While Chancellor Merz has voiced criticism of Merkel decision to shutter the reactors, there are no indications he intends to reverse it 
In Brazil, resistance to the so-called "nuclear renaissance" remains strong  Earlier this year, three former environment ministers-Izabella Teixeira, Carlos Minc, and Jos'e Carlos de Carvalho-signed a letter to President Lula, urging him to halt the completion of the Angra 3 nuclear plant  Their arguments range from Brazil plentiful renewable energy sources to the high costs and operational risks associated with nuclear facilities  The estimated cost to decommission Angra 3 now stands at US$21 billion, while the price tag to complete the project is projected at US$23 billion  
This is one of the reasons why Brazil Minister of Mines and Energy, who accompanied President Lula on his recent trip to China, toured state-owned nuclear power plants  These Chinese companies have expressed interest in acquiring 36% of the voting capital and 68% of the total capital that Eletrobras still holds in Eletronuclear, a stake that was preserved during the company privatization  The staggering costs of nuclear energy in Brazil were thrown into sharp relief by Eletronuclear layoff plan, which was halted by a provisional ruling  The fiscal strain was underscored again in Provisional Measure 1,300, which expanded the participation of large consumers in the free energy market-at the expense of Angra 1 and 2, whose financing currently falls on residential users and small private consumers  The funding challenges extend beyond power plants  The nuclear-powered submarine project faces delays while growing pressure mounts to break the state monopoly on uranium mining-a sector where Brazil holds the world eighth-largest reserves 
The twin post-war disasters-Chernobyl in 1986 and Fukushima in 2011-continue to fuel resistance to nuclear energy  "It a carbon-free energy source and part of the solution  Let replace fear with facts  No one died in Fukushima," said Mr  Grossi during the opening panel of a global gathering of mayors and community leaders from cities with nuclear facilities  Mr  Grossi lamented that prejudice against nuclear energy persists even in major institutions like the World Bank, where he recently sougt funding for nuclear projects in developing countries  To date, the IBRD has never financed a single civilian nuclear energy project 
Izabelle Boemeke and Robert Stone joined Rafael Grossi at the opening of the event  Ms  Boemeke, a Brazilian model who became a nuclear energy advocate after moving to the United States, has used her platform on social media to challenge public misconceptions  One of her most-viewed TikTok videos highlights the 1975 Banqiao hydroelectric dam collapse in China as one of the world worst energy-related disasters, aiming to reframe nuclear power in the broader context of energy risks  Mr  Stone, by contrast, is a convert-once an anti-nuclear activist, he is now known for directing the documentary "Pandora Promise," which makes the case for the benefits of atomic energy  During the panel, Mr  Stone addressed what remains the industry greatest liability: the enduring tension between environmentalists and nuclear waste  
Roughly 7 million people live within 75 kilometers of nuclear facilities worldwide  According to the IAEA Planning and Economic Studies Section, 31 new countries-between COP28 and COP29-began advocating for nuclear plant installations  Among the agency experts, there is consensus that net-zero carbon emissions are unattainable without nuclear energy  On the IAEA environmental and operational safety scale, nuclear energy ranks second only to solar  The agency ideal roadmap for meeting climate targets envisions a global energy mix composed of 80% renewables and 20% nuclear  In some regions, this shift is already underway: Russia, Hungary, and Switzerland now use nuclear energy to heat buildings, with at least six more countries-South Africa, Poland, Romania, China, Slovakia, and the United States-moving in the same direction 
Still, the absence of a permanent solution for nuclear waste disposal remains a critical barrier to winning over public opinion in countries like Germany and Austria, where skepticism runs deep  In France, which has a long-standing nuclear tradition, 30% of fuel is recycled, while the remainder is stored  Yet the retirement of aging reactors is outpacing advancements in recycling and reprocessing  Currently, 471 nuclear reactors are in operation across 31 countries, with 61 under construction  Another 241 have already been shut down, and around 200 more are slated for decommissioning by 2050  
The waste Issue extends beyond large nuclear reactors  In 1987, a discarded radiotherapy device in Goi^ania was found and dismantled by scrap collectors, resulting in the death of four people who came into contact with cesium and causing long-term health effects in more than 100 others  Today, the United States purchases used radiotherapy capsules from around the world and recycles them for industrial use  The increasing demand for these capsules stems from the fact that the isotopes they contain are not found naturally-they are by-products of uranium processing 
To this day, the incident remains the most serious radioactive accident involving medical equipment ever recorded  Radiotherapy itself has since become one of the most effective treatments for cancer  In earlier years, much of the radioactive material that could not be recycled was immobilized in concrete and stored in deep, hard-to-access underground facilities, which are still subject to routine inspection  Though radioactive waste remains a source of resistance, it has not stopped nuclear energy-driven by decarbonization-from gaining momentum 
The reporter travel costs were covered by AIEA

headline: Decision expectedon produced water rule
text:
The Water Quality Control Commission is inches away from creating guardrails for water reuse "pilot projects" using a controversial water source The commission met Tuesday to discuss proposed rules around reusing wastewater from oil and gas hydraulic fracturing operations, known as fracking 
At the heart of the deliberations was a debate over whether a permit was needed for pilot projects that don't discharge any treated water, or if a notice of intent would be sufficient  Without a permit, some commissioners worried there would be no option for enforcement 
After several hours of deliberation, commissioners decided to call it quits and wait for the hearing officer to draft potential permitting language  They agreed to resume proceedings at 9a m  Wednesday 
Commissioners face a May 24 deadline to make a decision 
Produced water - a byproduct from the mixture injected into the ground during fracking - has been the subject of heated debate for the past several years 
Some industry members, state officials and legislators have floated oil and gas wastewater reuse as a way to lessen the strain on the state water supply, find another way to dispose of produced water and reduce seismicity associated with the reinjection of the byproduct  But others have pushed back, saying there not enough evidence the industrial byproduct can be reused safely 
A feasibility study published by the state Environment Department last year showed the reuse of produced and brackish water could save the state substantial amounts of fresh water  But the study also cited obstacles, including high treatment costs and data gaps 
Nevertheless, the administrative and legislative process has marched toward the launch of fracking wastewater reuse projects in earnest  The 2019 Produced Water Act directed the Water Quality Control Commission to draft regulations for the reuse of produced water 
In May 2024, commissioners heard testimony on the proposed regulations, and in April of this year they voted on the final language of the regulations, although they opted to wait until May to vote on a full redlined version 
The rules will sunset in five years, with commissioners hoping that by then the Environment Department will know more about produced water and the technologies to treat it 
But environmental groups weren't satisfied with all of the provisions adopted in April 
Ahead of the Tuesday discussion, stakeholders in the rulemaking filed a trio of motions asking the commission to reevaluate certain parts of the language drafted in April 
Amigos Bravos and the Sierra Club filed a motion asking for the commission to reevaluate its decision to exempt fracking wastewater "pilot projects" from a general ban on the discharge of treated produced water 
Ultimately, commissioners voted to prohibit discharge from pilot projects 
Other stakeholders filed a joint motion asking the commission to look at changing a provision that would require a notice of intent - rather than a permit - when the Environment Department decides a project doesn't need to discharge treated produced water, arguing a permit should be required either way 
"The contemplated 'notice of intent' process is not protective of human health and the environment     and violates the Produced Water Act," the motion states  "Under the Produced Water Act, any use of produced water, whether treated or untreated, off the oilfield requires an entity to obtain a permit from the department "
Water Quality Control Commission Chair Bruce Thomson said the joint filing gave him the "greatest concern," and said upon reviewing the Produced Water Act, he thinks a permit may be required for reuse, whether or not treated water is discharged from a project 
"We're trying to make groundwater discharge permits fill that purpose," Thomson said  "I think it a stretch "
He added: "So for instance, if you wanted to use it for industrial-use cooling water, you wanted to use it to make cement, you wanted to use it for generating green hydrogen     it not clear to me that those would be subject to a groundwater discharge permit "
Commissioner William "Bill" Brancard said he shared that concern  He said he interpreted the law directive to create regulations beyond just discharges 
"They're asking us to regulate not just the discharge of produced water, but the treatment, storage, disposal, etc , of produced water," Brancard said 
Takeaways
After hearings in May of last year, the Water Quality Control Commission is close to deciding on proposed regulations for pilot projects to reuse fracking wastewater 
On Tuesday, commissioners' discussion focused on whether a permit would be required for "closed-loop" projects - projects that don't plan to discharge treated fracking wastewater into the environment but still plan to use the water for some purpose like mixing cement or cooling data centers 
Commissioners didn't vote on whether to create a pilot project permit on Tuesday, agreeing to revisit the issue Wednesday morning  They did vote to ban discharge from pilot projects altogether  The regulations will sunset in five years, at which point the state Environment Department is expected to know more about fracking wastewater reuse

headline: DeepSeek's cybersecurity failures expose a bigger risk. Here's what we really should be watching.
text:
The release of DeepSeek R1 model (DeepSeek-R1) on Jan  20, 2025 dominated news headlines and sparked lively debates across the tech and policy worlds  Some are already referring to the release as AI "Sputnik" moment, calling for outright bans, reassessment of our chip export control policies, and even criminalizing downloads of the model  Others argue the United States should view this moment as a wake-up call and focus its efforts on advancing our ability to innovate and develop AI at home  As policymakers and technologists disputed DeepSeek impact on America technological leadership, market competition, and innovation strategy, many cybersecurity researchers emphasized DeepSeek numerous cybersecurity failures-from successful jailbreaking attempts to leaked chat histories 
Yet, despite myriad concerns, leading technology companies including Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, and Cerebras were busy finding secure ways to integrate DeepSeek-R1 into their platforms, services, and ecosystems  Within days, each of these firms had successfully done so  At first glance, this might seem like a contradiction-Microsoft, in particular, is the largest cybersecurity company in the United States  However, this jump to embrace DeepSeek-R1 highlights a reality that has been overlooked: Securing leadership in artificial intelligence (AI) is not just about whose models are the most accessible-it is also about who is the most secure, reliable, and trustworthy 
Instead of leaning into panic or reacting with restrictive policies, we should pay close attention to the following shifts that are already transforming the landscape of cybersecurity, innovation, and governance  
1  DeepSeek Rise Is Part of a Larger Trend, Not a New or Isolated Challenge 
The ongoing attention surrounding DeepSeek-R1 may give the impression that it represents an unprecedented breakthrough; however, this is far from the first time an open-weight AI model was touted as a game-changer upon release  In fact, DeepSeek itself is not new  Founded in 2023, the company prior DeepSeek Coder series had already gained traction among AI developers in 2024  The latest model has simply accelerated that momentum, particularly because its reasoning capabilities appear to rival those of OpenAI GPT-o1 model while allegedly operating at a lower cost and on lower-end infrastructure 
But DeepSeek is only one player within the broader field of open-source AI development  Only days after the R1 release, Alibaba unveiled its Qwen 2 5-Max model, which has reportedly already outperformed R1 in several performance benchmarks  Despite this, Qwen 2 5 has received far less media and policy scrutiny even though it represents another major stride in China AI capabilities  Other major players, including France Mistral AI and America Meta continue to advance open-weight AI models, further challenging the notion that proprietary AI development will always hold an advantage 
This shift carries important national security, innovation, and cybersecurity implications  First, if China and DeepSeek claims about the R1 model performance capabilities hold true, the AI arms race between China and the United States may be closer than many have anticipated  This is a critical moment for us to define what it means to "win" and establish clear measures of AI competitiveness  Second, the line between open-source and closed-source AI models is blurring quickly, complicating traditional cybersecurity frameworks that assume greater control equals greater resilience  This also means open-source AI is here to stay, holding the potential to surpass even proprietary AI models in the future 
Finally, policymakers should recognize that AI development and deployment do not operate in complete isolation  Unlike traditional software or hardware, AI models rely heavily on shared datasets, distributed infrastructure, and iterative improvements from a broad ecosystem of users, researchers, and developers  This makes controlling or restricting AI more complex than simply banning a mobile application like TikTok or regulating hardware imports  Once an open-weight model is released, it remains widely accessible-meaning any restrictions within the United States would have little impact on global use  
2  Friend or Foe? The DeepSeek Debate Risks Missing the Point: Open-Source AI Shouldn't Be All or Nothing
Recent reports about DeepSeek grave cybersecurity failures have also reinforced ongoing concerns about the national security implications of open-source AI development and use within the United States  For example, researchers recently discovered a publicly exposed DeepSeek database that leaked chat histories, application programming interface keys, and back-end details  This security flaw was not the result of a sophisticated cyberattack but rather a basic security lapse that left sensitive information exposed-a sobering reminder that we cannot ignore cybersecurity fundamentals when developing and deploying AI  Compounding this vulnerability, DeepSeek guardrails have repeatedly collapsed when tested, allowing researchers to successfully jailbreak the model with ease  In this case, the jailbreaks enabled researchers to extract malware scripts, phishing templates, keyloggers, and even instructions for incendiary devices  Unlike proprietary AI systems, which undergo extensive adversarial testing and security hardening, DeepSeek appears dangerously susceptible to cyberattacks 
Given all these cybersecurity flaws and China long history of conducting cyber espionage, intellectual property theft, and data exploitation operations against the United States, policymakers have every right to question whether regulatory action is needed to proactively mitigate security risks associated with DeepSeek-R1  One concern already articulated in the ongoing debates around DeepSeek impact is whether China could use its open-source AI models to collect data about American users  However, moving to ban open-source AI models like the R1 model would be shortsighted  Its open nature means that just as China could use the model to collect data on Americans, researchers and developers across the United States can just as easily deploy and study it, leveraging its capabilities to accelerate and scale AI innovation 
Microsoft decision to incorporate DeepSeek-R1 into Azure AI Foundry offers a blueprint for secure deployment  Rather than blocking it, Microsoft quickly brought R1 into a controlled, monitored environment, allowing users to work with the model without exposing sensitive data or expanding attack surfaces  A similar approach-running open-source AI models locally on air-gapped servers and enforcing encryption and access controls-can allow independent developers to study, customize, and leverage open-weight AI securely  Policymakers are right to be vigilant, but they should also recognize that overly restrictive bans on open-source AI models could hinder the United States' ability to compete effectively in the ongoing AI arms race  For this reason, the United States should focus on building security frameworks and best practices that allow for safe use, just as Microsoft and other leading technology companies have started to do  
Steady Wins the Race-DeepSeek Should Reinforce Our Resolve to Be Leaders in AI 
The United States has enjoyed an edge in AI innovation, but DeepSeek recent achievements serve as a reminder that our progress and leadership cannot be taken for granted  It should inspire action and collaboration, not panic or overcorrection that could undermine our strengths 
If there is a lesson to be learned from the DeepSeek-R1 model release, it that AI leadership is not just about who achieves exceptional model performance and accessibility first-it also about who can be reliable and trustworthy while implementing them securely and strategically 
America edge in technology has always stemmed from its robust private sector; world-class research institutions; and open, dynamic innovation ecosystem  Reactionary policies that restrict AI development under the guise of security could ultimately do more harm than good, stifling domestic innovation while failing to curb global AI risks  Instead, the United States should use this moment to be a leader in AI and emerging technologies by investing in AI security research and development, establishing risk-based AI governance frameworks, and expanding data centers and energy grids to support demands and efforts to scale  By leveraging the strengths and strategies that have historically propelled its technological progress, the United States will remain a leader in AI and emerging technologies for decades to come

headline: Defenders of Renewable Energy Say It's Not About a Climate Crisis
text:
The Inflation Reduction Act was once hailed as the biggest climate law in U S  history  But as supporters try to save it, they've stopped talking about the environment altogether 
When it passed in 2022, the Inflation Reduction Act was hailed by Democrats and environmentalists as the most important piece of climate legislation in American history  
 But today, as House Republicans debate whether to repeal the hundreds of billions of dollars that the law provides for solar panels, electric vehicles and other technologies designed to fight global warming, supporters of the law rarely mention the planet 
 Instead, the law defenders argue that the tax credits for battery factories or wind farms are creating manufacturing jobs around the country and will reduce electricity prices and help the United States to compete in an A I  race against China 
 It a sign of how quickly climate has faded from the national agenda under President Trump, who has dismissed the risks of global warming and has rooted out any mention of climate change among federal agencies 
 ''We're no longer talking about the environment,'' said Chad Farrell, the founder of Encore Renewable Energy, based in Vermont  ''We're talking dollars and cents ''
 Mr  Farrell was among the solar industry leaders who met in Washington last week to lobby Congress to preserve many of the law clean energy provisions, saying they were essential for the U S  economy 
 The fate of the clean energy tax credits is being hotly debated on Capitol Hill  The most recent version of the House Republicans' far-reaching domestic policy bill would quickly phase out the biggest incentives for technologies like electric vehicles, batteries, wind turbines, solar panels and nuclear reactors and restrict tax breaks for domestic manufacturing  Some conservative Republicans want to entirely repeal the Inflation Reduction Act 
 On the other side, three dozen Republicans in the House and four in the Senate say they want to preserve at least some incentives, such as those for nuclear power or domestic manufacturing, to protect jobs and bolster U S  energy security 
 ''We must ensure certainty for current and future energy investments to meet the nation growing power demand and protect our constituents from higher energy costs,'' Representative Jen Kiggans, Republican of Virginia, wrote in a recent letter joined by 13 colleagues from her party  Ms  Kiggans has been a vocal supporter of a giant wind farm under construction off the coast of her district 
 In their pitch to lawmakers, many renewable energy companies have cast their industries as essential for achieving national energy dominance, a goal repeatedly mentioned by Mr  Trump 
 One common argument: America demand for electricity is soaring, driven by a boom in A I  data centers, and it difficult to build enough gas-fired power plants to supply all the extra power that the nation needs  Adding more solar, wind and batteries could be the country best hope for averting energy shortages, since they are relatively quick to construct, proponents say 
 ''Maybe before it was tie-dye T-shirts and hugging trees, but today we are a mature energy sector and a critical part of energizing America,'' said Constantino Nicolaou, the chief executive of PanelClaw, a solar company based in Massachusetts that supplies mounting systems for rooftop solar projects 
 ''Yes, we love the environment, but don't look at us like the environmentalists,'' Mr  Nicolaou said 
 Other companies warn that thousands of good-paying manufacturing jobs could disappear if Congress repealed the tax credits  Since the Inflation Reduction Act passed, businesses have announced more than $843 billion in clean energy investments, from wind farms in Wyoming to battery factories in Georgia  More than three-fourths of that spending is expected to occur in Republican-controlled districts 
 If Republicans phase out the tax credits, ''nearly 300 U S  factories -- mostly in red states -- could close or never open,'' said Abigail Ross Hopper, chief executive of the Solar Energy Industries Association, a trade group 
 On Monday, the solar association released a report estimating that 292,000 jobs and $220 billion in local investments might be lost if Congress ended the tax breaks for solar power, while warning of blackouts and higher electricity bills 
 The report does not mention climate change  It a marked contrast with 2022, when the solar association released an analysis showing that additional solar deployment brought on by the Inflation Reduction Act would offset more than 655 million tons of carbon dioxide, the greenhouse gas that is driving global warming 
 Even many environmental groups defending the law now sound like a green-tinged version of the Chamber of Commerce 
 Repealing the tax credits ''means higher gas and electricity costs for struggling families and businesses, tanking the U S  manufacturing resurgence and ceding leadership to China,'' said Sara Chieffo, the vice president of government affairs at the League of Conservation Voters 
 Other companies, including makers of electric vehicles, are trying to persuade lawmakers that the United States could lose a technology race with China if the law were gutted 
 ''We can't lose sight of the fact that the automotive industry is global, and consumers all over the world are increasingly preferring electric vehicles for a variety of reasons,'' said Michael Tubman, director of federal affairs at Lucid Motors, a start-up manufacturing electric cars in Arizona  ''The American auto industry is in fierce competition with foreign competitors, particularly Chinese automakers ''
 The shift away from focusing on climate change is a throwback to an earlier era of clean energy politics, said Alex Trembath, the deputy director of the Breakthrough Institute, an environmental research organization 
 For many years, subsidies for new energy technologies like wind, solar and nuclear power had broad bipartisan support and were often renewed under Republican presidents, in part because they were seen as essential to accelerate technological innovation and improve U S  energy security 
 That changed during the Biden administration, when Democrats made fighting climate change a top political priority and dramatically expanded the tax credits as part of a push to quickly pivot the U S  economy away from fossil fuels 
 ''Once all these tax credits started getting described as once-in-a-generation climate policy, that went a long ways toward turning Republicans away,'' Mr  Trembath said  But in the wake of Mr  Trump victory in the 2024 election, he has written, climate change looks less like a winning issue for Democrats 
 Whether Republicans are swayed by the economic arguments for the Inflation Reduction Act remains to be seen  Conservative critics have pushed back hard, saying that clean energy subsidies could cost taxpayers trillions of dollars 
 ''The Inflation Reduction Act has ballooned into one of the biggest government boondoggles in history,'' said Thomas J  Pyle, president of the American Energy Alliance, a conservative research group focused on energy  ''That real money that could be returned to taxpayers, fueling private-sector innovation, job creation and consumer-driven growth instead of funneling taxpayer dollars into a narrow, government-directed vision of the economy ''
 Some Senate Republicans have suggested phasing out the subsidies for wind and solar -- on the grounds that those industries are mature and don't need further help -- while maintaining tax credits for earlier-stage technologies like advanced nuclear reactors or enhanced geothermal plants 
 That could result in more planet-warming emissions  An analysis by the Rhodium Group, a research firm, found that repealing tax credits for electric cars, wind turbines and solar panels could lead to hundreds of millions of tons of extra carbon dioxide in the atmosphere each year by 2035 
 Yet others say that the broader effect that the tax credits would have on emissions has been overstated  That because many solar and wind projects across the country are currently being held up by delays in local permitting or regulations that make it difficult to build large transmission lines, blunting the law climate impact 
 ''There are a lot of reforms we could make that would have a much bigger impact on decarbonization than the I R A ,'' said Devin Hartman, director of energy and environmental policy at the R Street Institute, a center-right think tank  ''In some ways the fight over subsidies has hijacked that conversation ''
 Whatever happens to the Inflation Reduction Act, many supporters say it was a promising attempt at creating a durable climate policy in the United States 
 Before the law, climate activists often favored restrictions and penalties, such as taxes on emissions from oil, gas and coal  But countries, like Canada and Australia, that enacted such policies later overturned them because they were unpopular 
 The Inflation Reduction Act was designed to be a ''carrot'' rather than a ''stick,'' said Leah Stokes, a professor at the University of California, Santa Barbara, who helped craft parts of the law  It led to investment, and that has made the policies stickier 
 ''Climate policy is hard,'' Ms  Stokes said  ''We're trying to wean ourselves off a product that is represented by the most powerful industry on the planet '' But the tax incentives for clean energy, she added, still have a better shot at surviving ''than anything else ever has ''
 Referring to the law fate in Congress, Ms  Stokes said, ''It not dead yet ''

Graphic

headline: Democrats Have Been at War With Oil for Too Long; Guest Essay
text:
It been a brutal three months for the American oil and gas industry  Oil prices have dropped to their lowest level since 2021, below what most shale companies need for new drilling to be profitable  China recently halted imports of American liquefied natural gas in the latest act of trade retaliation  And as the Trump administration tariffs on Mexico and Canada hit materials and equipment, the cost of producing oil and gas in America is rising 
The future looks grim, too: OPEC, the International Energy Agency and Goldman Sachs have all forecast softer oil demand in anticipation of a global economic downturn set off by President Trump trade war  The chaos has even eclipsed any gains from Mr  Trump's industry-friendly regulatory rollbacks and efforts to fast-track approvals for new energy projects  As one industry veteran put it in a recent Dallas Federal Reserve survey of oil and gas executives, "I have never felt more uncertainty about our business in my entire 40-plus-year career " And this turmoil is no accident; it a direct result of Mr  Trump economic vandalism 
A weakened American oil and gas industry hands more power to OPEC, leaving U S  households vulnerable the next time it cuts production and drives up prices  That means American consumers could eventually feel pain at the pump 
As Democrats sharpen their offensive on Mr  Trump, they would be wise to underscore this uncertainty to voters, who want secure prices and energy  Stable energy prices depend on stable policy - and this moment calls for a Democratic agenda that prioritizes both 
Democrats have missed opportunities to stabilize the oil market in the past  When oil prices crashed during the Covid pandemic, threatening shale companies with bankruptcy, party leaders blocked a plan championed by Texas and Oklahoma Democrats to buy oil for the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, calling it a "bailout for big oil "
By 2021, as oil demand rebounded with the economy, U S  shale producers were ill equipped to ramp up production and counter OPEC production restraint or the looming shock from Russia invasion of Ukraine  Ultimately, U S  consumers paid the price to the tune of $5 a gallon of gasoline  Alongside missteps like these, Democrats' political gestures like fracking bans have alienated an industry that has helped reduce America reliance on oil from countries such as Saudi Arabia and Russia 
Democrats have a tricky line to walk, balancing climate priorities and energy security  But they can chart a new course if they stop treating domestic oil and gas production as a liability 
This doesn't mean adopting a mindless "drill, baby, drill" mentality; it about recognizing that domestic oil and gas companies deliver geopolitical value and economic benefits by keeping the world less reliant on Russia and OPEC  The industry can also help accelerate decarbonization with innovation and financial might 
And admitting this doesn't have to be at odds with Democrats' efforts to enforce strong pollution standards and hold bad oil and gas actors accountable  To unlock production across the energy system, stability is crucial to provide a reliable investment environment and clear rules of the road 
An obvious place for Democrats to pursue this is in a robust defense of the Inflation Reduction Act  The sweeping Biden-era law is diversifying and expanding the American energy system - a necessity as American consumers find themselves competing for electricity with manufacturers, data centers and the global market  Without that additional supply, volatility is inevitable 
Yet Republicans are now pushing to repeal up to $400 billion worth of the law energy tax credits - a move that would stall energy investment and drive consumer prices still higher  That will give Democrats the chance to make the case that diverse energy sources are the best hedge against price spikes in any single fuel 
And while Mr  Trump kneecaps American oil and gas with tariffs and by encouraging OPEC to produce more, Democrats can instead offer a plan to refill the Strategic Petroleum Reserve  That will help U S  producers survive a price crash driven by OPEC oversupply while protecting consumers from future supply shocks 
Achieving stability also means fixing outdated permitting laws that stifle energy development of all kinds and undermine the government ability to invest in it  While Democrats in Congress may not be able to pass legislation without a majority, it is time for them to offer a platform that reduces the uncertainty that paralyzes the infrastructure critical to achieving climate goals and energy reliability 
While much of this could sound like heresy to today Democratic leadership, it would in fact be a course correction: a return to the Obama-era energy playbook that embraced an increase in the production of both American oil and gas and clean energy 
Mr  Trump can wrap chaos in bravado, but his policies have already hurt American producers and undermined our economy  Ultimately they will also hurt consumers 
Democrats can make that plain  They can be the party of reliable energy, robust domestic production and decarbonization - but only if they embrace an agenda that responds to the complexity of the moment with steadiness  The opportunity is there to lead, to grow and to govern in a time when it is so desperately lacking 
Arnab Datta is the managing director of policy implementation at Employ America  He has advised members of Congress from both parties and the Biden White House on energy and economic policy

headline: Developers plan an oasis in a 'restaurant desert'
text:
May 23-In southeast Mesa, Eastmark and Cadence developers have successfully turned cactus fields into thousands of high-end homes 
But many who moved into the new neighborhoods complain they are stuck in a "restaurant desert "
Ask Peter Martens, an Eastmark resident since moving from Gilbert six months ago 
It would be nice to have these types of amenities closer instead of driving into Gilbert or Queen Creek," he said 
When told about plans for the East Gate Plaza, which promises to bring a half-dozen restaurants to Elliot and Ellsworth roads, Martens was thrilled - adding that, it really a no-brainer 
"This area is booming so I'm surprised it taking this long to have these types of developments," Martens said 
For months, every time construction of a new section of homes is announced, or a big company begins the process of opening a new warehouse or data center, southeast Mesa residents have demanded of Councilman Kevin Thompson and others: "What about nice restaurants?"
If you're looking for a job, this area has plenty of big employers: A mammoth Apple data center needs techies, Dignity Health Arizona General Hospital needs nurses and pharmacy techs, Niagara Bottling needs warehouse workers and drivers 
But what about the residents?
The locals have consistently complained that, save for fast food, they have to drive south for date-night and family meals 
Enter East Gate Plaza, a planned production from Diversified Partners - an "A-list" developer with projects on both sides of the Valley 
On the northeast corner of Ellsworth and Elliot roads, across the street from Dignity Health, Diversified Partners says it has "multiple deals in process that include restaurants, retail, convenience store/gas station, coffee, dessert, nail salon and dental/orthodontic users, to name a few "
If that sounds good to you, thank "Team JJ "
Diversified Partners' Julie Harris and Jennifer Hill are the self-described "Team JJ" working on developing this barren corner into a bustling marketplace 
The clever name of East Gate Plaza comes from "marrying Eastmark and the Gateway Airport," Hill noted 
Hill lives in northeast Mesa, making plenty of trips down to the southeast corner of the city to make sure what she saw on Google maps was legit 
"My first phone call was in January 2019; I just called the owner," she recalled  "It wasn't even on the market 
"But I thought, 'Someday, this is going to be the epicenter with the 60 and the 202 and the 24 expansion and what happening at Eastmark and the Elliot Technology Corridor ' So I called the owner, a sweet woman up in Washington, and she put me in touch with her broker     Then I showed it to Walt "
The big man behind the deal is Walt Brown, founder and CEO of Diversified Partners 
He went all-in on this corner, realizing, as he said in a press release last week, "The explosive commercial growth along both Elliot and Ellsworth, proximity to three freeways, the expanding Phoenix-Mesa Gateway airport, new sports complex and thousands of new homes planned and underway "
Though the purchase of the 17 acres to become East Gate Plaza isn't quite official yet, Brown, Hill and company are confident this will be a "go "
"We've got at least six restaurants leased or in (negotiations)," Hill said  "They're national, regional and local "
Though she said she has to be coy about names at this point, when concerns from residents about too much fast food in the area were shared, Hill grinned  "We're not talking McDonald or Jack in the Box," she said 
"There will be patios," she added  "The city got their eye on this as it the doorstep of the tech corridor; they want walkability, they want community "
East Gate Plaza will have as many as eight restaurants, Hill said, as well as space for medical offices ("family doctors, orthopedics, dentists") 
Asked if "restaurant desert" is a fair term for the Eastmark/Cadence at Gateway area, Thompson mulled the question 
"Right now, that a fair representation," the District 6 councilman said  "We're lacking in the amenities you see in other parts of the city, when it comes to nice, sit-down restaurants "
When he meets with developers, Thompson said he encourages them to "step it up  Let not go for the low hanging fruit of McDonald and Jack in the Box "
Thompson thoughts on the planned East Gate Plaza?
"Hopefully, they'll bring in some nice restaurants "
That exactly the plan, say the East Gate developers - even though they don't even own the property at Ellsworth and Elliot yet 
"We're going to close on the land next month," Hill said  "Then we'll start moving dirt "
Infrastructure plans include the installation of a traffic signal on Peterson Avenue, the northern boundary of the property 
In the best-case scenario, restaurants, shops and offices will begin opening a little more than a year from now 
Dane Astle, lead architect of the project, promised a striking project in a press release 
"We are creating a destination community, playing off the tech corridor theme, which means there will be lots of glass, angular lines and rich materials such as masonry, rollup doors and exposed steel elements," Astle vowed 
"We are creating a collaborative environment where people will come to work, take coffee breaks on benches under the trees and stay for the retail and restaurant experiences "

headline: Discharges of fracking wastewater are banned
text:
Environmental advocates on Wednesday celebrated a decision by the New Mexico Water Quality Control Commission to prohibit the discharge of fracking wastewater into the state waterways and groundwater The provision came as part of a set of regulations the commission approved for projects that reuse wastewater produced during hydraulic fracturing to extract oil and natural gas 
The commission had been drafting the rules, a process that began with a hearing last year, and started to finalize the language in April  This week, the commission members tied up loose ends - and made some major changes hailed by environmental groups 
"The proposed rule was different from the final rule, certainly," said Wild-Earth Guardians campaign manager Rebecca Sobel  "I would actually characterize that as a good faith discussion and ruling "
Reusing fracking wastewater, also known as "produced water," has been proposed as a way to reduce competition for one of the state most precious resources: water  Proponents argue treated wastewater from fracking - a process that generates millions of barrels of contaminated water - can be put toward industrial uses like mixing concrete or cooling data centers 
But concerns about the safety of the byproduct of oil and gas production have remained 
For Rachel Conn, deputy director of water conservation group Amigos Bravos, the most significant change to the proposed rules was the ban on discharging produced water - treated or not - outside an oil field 
That prohibition includes pilot projects, which are allowed to handle up to 2,000 barrels of fracking wastewater per day 
Although Amigos Bravos is supportive of pilot projects moving forward, Conn said, she noted there are now no water quality standards to make sure discharge would be safe for the state waterways 
"We don't have enough information to know how to adequately treat, to safely treat, produced water at this point," Conn said  "    Discharging produced water was very much premature at this point "
The commission also responded to concerns produced water should be classified as hazardous waste after it off an oilfield - and needs to be handled as such 
Commissioners decided the wastewater needs to be characterized, or tested for its makeup, before being reused 
They also created a permit requirement for pilot projects, which some advocates say could make it easier to enforce provisions of the rule  Initially, a "notice of intent" process was proposed - but some commissioners said that could make it difficult to hold pilot projects accountable 
"If there a pilot project that isn't testing its waste to see if it hazardous or it mischaracterizing it, we now have a legal mechanism," said Melissa Troutman, climate and health advocate for WildEarth Guardians  "    We have a legal lever to pull  We wouldn't have had that under [a notice of intent] "
The New Mexico Oil and Gas Association was less celebratory  In a statement, President and CEO Missi Currier said the action would "stifle innovation" and set back the state water conservation efforts 
"Unfortunately, the rule passed by the Commission today rejects that reasonable approach and adopts the most severe and limiting regulation for the reuse of treated produced water anywhere in the country," Currier said  "The rule provides no meaningful path forward for the reuse of treated produced water in New Mexico and does not allow for increased conservation efforts "
Currier said the rule could drive economic and research opportunities to neighboring states with less stringent rules  And she criticized the broad ban on discharges 
"In reaching this conclusion, the Commission ignored the work, research, and data collected by New Mexico State University researchers involved in the New Mexico Produced Water Research Consortium," Currier said  "The Commission rule also ignores the position of the New Mexico Environment Department leadership that a ban was unnecessary because the existing regulatory process is protective of human health and the environment "

headline: Discussions about AI don’t focus enough on environmental impacts [letter]
text:
On June 18, I had the privilege of attending the Lancaster Chamber annual dinner, at which the keynote speaker was Zack Kass  The topic of discussion was artificial intelligence and ranged from current day integration to future applications 
Many good points were made, including the need to reinvest in our communities, which is where there was some missing context 
When asked about environmental impacts of AI, Kass responded by asking why AI companies and developers have to address climate issues when almond farmers don't have to 
This deflection refuses to acknowledge AI immense environmental impact  International Energy Agency models predict that data centers will be using 945 terawatt-hours by 2030 (more energy consumption than all of Japan) and these data centers require large amounts of fresh water for cooling systems 
It was disappointing and frustrating to get an answer that fundamentally does not address this question: How can we balance embracing a future in which AI is integrated into our businesses while protecting our environment for generations to come?
Another point Kass made was that we should let the technology get us out of the situation we've put ourselves in  It important to keep in mind that, for many, it is already too late and we are facing a reality of continuous climate disasters and impacts, including the high temperatures we're seeing this summer in Lancaster County 
How do we ensure that unmetered intelligence is introduced equitably and that future generations live to see the communities we can build when we reinvest in them?
Lucas Shumaker
Marietta

headline: DOF to seek US grant for BIR digitalization
text:
Manila, Jan  21 -- The Department of Finance (DOF) is set to enter into negotiations with the United States Trade and Development Agency (USTDA) for a possible grant for the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) digital transformation program 
In a statement, the DOF said President Rodrigo R  Duterte has authorized the department to talk with the USTDA for a possible $809,450, or about P39-million, grant for the Philippines' main tax agency 
A Special Authority was given by the President designating and authorizing Finance Undersecretary Antonette Tionko and Finance Undersecretary Mark Dennis Joven along with BIR Deputy Commissioner Lanee David "to negotiate" with the USTDA on behalf of the Philippines 
Tionko and Joven are the respective undersecretaries for the DOF Revenue Operations Group and International Finance Group, whileDavid is the deputy commissionerfor the BIR'sInformation Systems Group 
The Special Authority covers the negotiations for an agreement on the grant for the BIR'sInformation and Communications Technology (ICT) Modernization Strategy and Data Center Technical Assistance Project 
President Duterte alsodesignated and authorized Finance Secretary Carlos G  Dominguez III or BIR Commissioner CaesarDulay "to conclude, sign, execute and deliver the said Grant Agreement "
The BIR projectaims to modernize the bureau'sinfrastructure and operational environment, the DOF said 
Dominguez has cited the BIR digital transformation effortsas among the factors that led to a dramaticimprovement of its servicesto taxpayers anditsrobust collection performance ahead of the COVID-19 pandemic-induced crisis 
He said the digitally enhanced administrative reforms being undertaken by the BIR are now beginning to pay off by way of the significant improvement in the country tax effort from 13 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) in 2015 to 14 5
percent of GDP in 2019 
The digital switch has also led to the more convenient and efficient electronic filing of tax payments, especially during this coronavirus pandemic, he said 
In February last year, the BIR allowed the use of the PayMaya mobile application as an additional electronic payment channel for tax payments 
On top of PayMaya, these other e-payment tools are GCash, LandBank Linkbiz, DBP PayTax, Union Bank Online and PESONet 
The BIR has also improvedthe tax forms deployed in the e-BIR Forms System to make the filing of tax returns more accessible and convenient to taxpayers 
It began the pilot implementation in April 21last yearof its web-based Internal Revenue Integrated System (IRIS) that will be the central tool and repository to process taxpayers' information 
TheIRIS is targeted to be available nationwide by the end of 2021 
AnElectronic Audited Financial System (eAFS) wasalsolaunched last June 1 to allow business taxpayers to electronically submittheir financial statements to the BIR 
The BIR likewise concluded last June its competition dubbed the 'HACK-A-TAX Challenge,' whichbrought together talented information technology (IT)professionals, start-ups and students across the country to develop innovative digital-based solutions for the bureau 
To enabletaxpayers to continue consulting revenue officials on their tax-related concerns even with the mobility restrictions imposed to curb the spread of COVID-19, the BIR launched its eAppointment Facility onOctober19 
InNovember 2020, the BIR also launched its web-based Procurement, Payment, Inventory and Monitoring System (PPIMS) and its Online Application for Tax Clearance for Bidding Purposes (eTCBP)

headline: DOF working on grant for BIR digitization tack
text:
PRESIDENT Duterte has authorized the Department of Finance (DOF) to enter into talks with the United States Trade and Development Agency (USTDA) in a bid to secure a grant of $809,450 to support the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) digital transformation program 
In a statement on Wednesday, the DOF said the President has approved its request for a Special Authority to designate and authorize any one of its senior officials 'to negotiate and/or facilitate, in accordance with law, for and on behalf of the Government of the Republic of the Philippines, with the authorized representatives of the USTDA '
The Special Authority covers the negotiations for an agreement on the grant of $809,450 00 (approximately P38,850,873 20) by the USTDA for the BIR Information and Communications Technology (ICT) Modernization Strategy and Data Center Technical Assistance Project, the DOF said 
'The project funded by the USTDA grant will ensure an in-depth technical assessment of the BIR current ICT environment, the development of an Enterprise Architecture roadmap/framework and an assessment of the organizational framework of the BIR Information System Group including recommended restructuring and training programs,' the DOF said 
The senior officials recommended by DOF to the President were Undersecretaries Antonette C  Tionko and Mark Dennis Y C  Joven along with BIR Deputy Commissioner Lanee C  David 
Tionko and Joven are the respective undersecretaries for the DOF Revenue Operations Group and International Finance Group while David is the deputy commissioner for the BIR Information Systems Group 
Duterte also designated and authorized Finance Secretary Carlos G  Dominguez III or BIR Commissioner Caesar R  Dulay 'to conclude, sign, execute and deliver the said Grant Agreement '
Dominguez earlier said BIR digital transformation efforts paved the way for the significant improvement of the country tax effort from 13 percent of gross domestic product in 2015 to 14 5 percent of GDP in 2019 
A total of P1 241 trillion or 86 percent of the total P1 45 trillion collected by BIR from January to September last year was made through electronic payment channels, based on preliminary data earlier cited by DOF 
Electronic payment channels tapped by BIR were PayMaya, GCash, LandBank Linkbiz, Development Bank of the Philippines PayTax, UnionBank Online and PESONet 
The number of tax returns filed electronically from January to September last year reached 16 45 million or 94 percent of the total 17 42 million tax returns filed for the period 
To make the filing of tax returns more accessible and convenient, the BIR also improved the tax forms in its e-BIR Forms System 
In April last year, it also began the pilot implementation of its web-based Internal Revenue Integrated System to serve as the central tool and repository to process taxpayers' information  The IRIS is targeted to be available nationwide by end-2021 
Apart from this, the BIR has also launched its e-Appointment Facility, electronic Audited Financial System, web-based Procurement, Payment, Inventory and Monitoring System and its Online Application for Tax Clearance for Bidding Purposes (eTCBP) 
The BIR also concluded in June its competition dubbed the 'HACK-A-TAX Challenge,' which brought together talented information technology professionals, start-ups and students across the country to develop innovative digital-based solutions for the bureau

headline: Don’t Mention Climate: Now, Clean Energy Is All About the Money

Other companies warn that thousands of good-paying manufacturing jobs could disappear if Congress repealed the tax credits  Since the Inflation Reduction Act passed, businesses have announced more than $843 billion in clean energy investments, from wind farms in Wyoming to battery factories in Georgia  More than three-fourths of that spending is expected to occur in Republican-controlled districts

Even many environmental groups defending the law now sound like a green-tinged version of the Chamber of Commerce 
Repealing the tax credits "means higher gas and electricity costs for struggling families and businesses, tanking the U S  manufacturing resurgence and ceding leadership to China," said Sara Chieffo, the vice president of government affairs at the League of Conservation Voters 
Other companies, including makers of electric vehicles, are trying to persuade lawmakers that the United States could lose a technology race with China if the law were gutted 
"We can't lose sight of the fact that the automotive industry is global, and consumers all over the world are increasingly preferring electric vehicles for a variety of reasons," said Michael Tubman, director of federal affairs at Lucid Motors, a start-up manufacturing electric cars in Arizona  "The American auto industry is in fierce competition with foreign competitors, particularly Chinese automakers "
The shift away from focusing on climate change is a throwback to an earlier era of clean energy politics, said Alex Trembath, the deputy director of the Breakthrough Institute, an environmental research organization 
For many years, subsidies for new energy technologies like wind, solar and nuclear power had broad bipartisan support and were often renewed under Republican presidents, in part because they were seen as essential to accelerate technological innovation and improve U S  energy security 
That changed during the Biden administration, when Democrats made fighting climate change a top political priority and dramatically expanded the tax credits as part of a push to quickly pivot the U S  economy away from fossil fuels 
"Once all these tax credits started getting described as once-in-a-generation climate policy, that went a long ways toward turning Republicans away," Mr  Trembath said  But in the wake of Mr  Trump victory in the 2024 election, he has written, climate change looks less like a winning issue for Democrats 
Whether Republicans are swayed by the economic arguments for the Inflation Reduction Act remains to be seen  Conservative critics have pushed back hard, saying that clean energy subsidies could cost taxpayers trillions of dollars 
"The Inflation Reduction Act has ballooned into one of the biggest government boondoggles in history," said Thomas J  Pyle, president of the American Energy Alliance, a conservative research group focused on energy  "That real money that could be returned to taxpayers, fueling private-sector innovation, job creation and consumer-driven growth instead of funneling taxpayer dollars into a narrow, government-directed vision of the economy "
Some Senate Republicans have suggested phasing out the subsidies for wind and solar - on the grounds that those industries are mature and don't need further help - while maintaining tax credits for earlier-stage technologies like advanced nuclear reactors or enhanced geothermal plants 
That could result in more planet-warming emissions  An analysis by the Rhodium Group, a research firm, found that repealing tax credits for electric cars, wind turbines and solar panels could lead to hundreds of millions of tons of extra carbon dioxide in the atmosphere each year by 2035 
Yet others say that the broader effect that the tax credits would have on emissions has been overstated  That because many solar and wind projects across the country are currently being held up by delays in local permitting or regulations that make it difficult to build large transmission lines, blunting the law climate impact 
"There are a lot of reforms we could make that would have a much bigger impact on decarbonization than the I R A ," said Devin Hartman, director of energy and environmental policy at the R Street Institute, a center-right think tank  "In some ways the fight over subsidies has hijacked that conversation "
Whatever happens to the Inflation Reduction Act, many supporters say it was a promising attempt at creating a durable climate policy in the United States 
Before the law, climate activists often favored restrictions and penalties, such as taxes on emissions from oil, gas and coal  But countries, like Canada and Australia, that enacted such policies later overturned them because they were unpopular 
The Inflation Reduction Act was designed to be a "carrot" rather than a "stick," said Leah Stokes, a professor at the University of California, Santa Barbara, who helped craft parts of the law  It led to investment, and that has made the policies stickier 
"Climate policy is hard," Ms  Stokes said  "We're trying to wean ourselves off a product that is represented by the most powerful industry on the planet " But the tax incentives for clean energy, she added, still have a better shot at surviving "than anything else ever has "
Referring to the law fate in Congress, Ms  Stokes said, "It not dead yet "
PHOTOS: Pre-assembly wind turbines destined for the South Fork Wind Farm at a pier in New London, Conn  Right, assembly of an electric vehicle at Lucid Motors, a start-up in Arizona  (PHOTOGRAPHS BY JOE BUGLEWICZ FOR THE NEW YORK TIMES; CAITLIN O'HARA/REUTERS) This article appeared in print on page A9

headline: Dozens of bills from Frederick County state delegates, senators pass in legislative session
text:
Three bills sponsored by a Frederick County state delegate were signed into law on Tuesday  Dozens of others passed the Maryland General Assembly and are awaiting the governor signature 
On Tuesday morning, Gov  Wes Moore, a Democrat, signed the first batch of bills from the 2025 state legislative session 
Among those he specifically praised was HB723, which was sponsored by Del  Ken Kerr, a Democrat from District 3 in Frederick 
In an interview after the bill signing, Kerr said he initially drafted HB723 in an effort to make it easier for dental hygienists certified in other states to practice in Maryland 
"It was just a little bill when it started out," Kerr said 
Eventually, Kerr said he amended the bill with the goal of creating a plan to assist health care workers displaced by federal job cuts across the country in obtaining their licenses to practice here on an expedited basis 
"We are talking about highly qualified, highly skilled workers who have built careers saving lives," Moore said at the bill signing  "This legislation will help ensure essential workers can continue their work to be able to make our state better "
Two other bills sponsored by Kerr, including one related to licensing for social workers and another related to the 3-1-1 nonemergency system, were also signed into law on Tuesday 
Moore will have until May 27 to sign or veto each bill passed by the state legislature during this year session  A bill also can become law if the governor takes no action 
Legislative highlights
Other locally sponsored bills that passed both chambers before the state legislature adjourned on Monday would provide more protections for victims of child abuse, close a loophole in existing law on fuel gas piping and commission a study on data centers, among other changes 
Del  Jesse Pippy (R-4), chair of the state delegation for Frederick County, pointed to a handful of bills he and other Frederick County delegates hope will help child victims testify and build a case against their abusers 
One, HB442, would allow statements made by a child to a professional forensic interviewer to be read in court  Currently, the rule against hearsay prevents forensic interviewers from testifying about statements children made to them 
The primary sponsor of HB442 is Del  Karen Simpson (D-3)  Simpson took up the bill on behalf of Frederick County Executive Jessica Fitzwater, who made the proposal part of her legislative package for 2025 
Also related to testimony in child abuse cases is HB293  If signed, it would allow the testimony of a child abuse victim to be taken outside the courtroom and broadcast inside the courtroom on closed-circuit television 
"That child currently has to come into court and sit in front of their abuser," Pippy said on Tuesday  "What has happened in real life is that, in many cases, those children are terrified to do so "
HB293 was supported by the Maryland State Attorneys' Association and the Frederick County State Attorney Office, among other groups 
Pippy wife, Lindsey Carpenter, is chief of the Special Victims Unit in the Frederick County State Attorney Office 
Another bill sponsored by Pippy and co-sponsored by Kerr would require medical offices to notify patients by either first-class mail or email about the destruction of their records 
Pippy said HB1510 was inspired by the case of Ernesto Cesar Torres, a former Frederick pediatrician who is accused of abusing dozens of children over the course of decades  Last month, Torres was convicted for the first time of child sexual abuse perpetrated against a patient  He has yet to be sentenced 
"When it was first published that he was being charged with several sexual abuse claims, hundreds of people went to obtain their medical records from his office to prove that they were there," Pippy said  "And the records were destroyed "
Both HB293 and HB1510 were introduced by Pippy during the 2024 legislative session, but did not pass as proposed 
Another bill that was introduced in a prior session was SB175  It pertains to corrugated stainless steel tubing (CSST) - a common fuel gas piping material cited as a factor in the lightning-induced fire that killed Frederick County Battalion Chief Josh Laird in 2021 
SB175 was another of Fitzwater legislative priorities for the 2025 session  Initially, the bill set out to ban the sale, transfer or distribution of CSST that has not been proven through testing to withstand at least 36 coulombs of electrical charge - more than the average lightning strike 
Sen  Karen Lewis Young (D-3), the bill sponsor, ultimately amended the bill to ban the sale, transfer or distribution of CSST that does not have conductive jacketing, which reduces the risk of failure due to lightning strikes 
Previously, non-jacketed CSST could not be used in the construction of a new gas piping system, the replacement of an existing gas line or a renovation of more than half a property, but could still be bought and sold in Maryland 
The amended bill also will establish a work group to study the material and what regulations should apply to it 
"For all intents and purposes, we have taken a big safety step even before this work group comes up with a more uniform safety standard," Lewis Young said on Tuesday 
Lewis Young also sponsored SB116, which would require the Maryland Department of the Environment and the University of Maryland School of Business to study the economic, environmental and energy impacts of data centers 
The study would be overseen by the Department of Legislative Services 
Previously, the Maryland Tech Council commissioned a study that found that the Quantum Frederick data center campus would generate approximately $41 million in county tax revenue and 1,700 jobs 
The study required by SB116 would be more comprehensive and objective than the Maryland Tech Council study, Lewis Young said 
"Depending on the results of the study, we could revisit our environmental goals and our incentive structure vis-a-vis data centers," Lewis Young said 
SB116 was co-sponsored by Sen  Justin Ready, a Republican representing District 5 in Carroll and Frederick counties 
Delegation actions
According to Pippy, the Frederick County delegation as an entity did not file as many bills this year as it has in years past 
Among the delegation bills that passed both chambers in 2025 were HB512, which pertains to salon liquor licenses; HB866, which pertains to theater liquor licenses; and HB1026, which pertains to wine and beer tasting permits  All three bills apply only to Frederick County 
A fourth bill, HB492, was not technically a delegation bill, but was supported by the delegation as a whole, Pippy said  It would authorize Frederick County director of human resources to request a state and national criminal records check for a prospective county employee or volunteer 
HB492 was also one of Fitzwater legislative priorities for 2025  It was sponsored by Simpson and co-sponsored by Dels  Kerr, Kris Fair (D-3), April Miller (R-4), April Rose (R-5) and Chris Tomlinson (R-5)  The Senate cross-file, SB995, was sponsored by all four Frederick County senators 
Delegation bills that did not pass in 2025 included a bill to allow volunteer fire and ambulance companies to accept credit cards as payment at gaming events, a bill to allow bow hunters in Frederick and Washington counties to carry handguns and a bill to raise the Frederick County sheriff salary by about 50% 
The 2025 sheriff salary bill, which passed in the state Senate but died in the state House of Delegates, was the delegation fourth failed attempt in recent years to increase the pay for the position 
On the House side, the sheriff salary bill was first referred to the Judiciary Committee before being re-referred to the Environment and Transportation Committee  Pippy said he believed the change was intended to kill the bill 
"The reality is that the Democrats in Annapolis have made it a political football," Pippy said 
Aside from passing legislation, state legislators from Frederick County worked throughout the session to secure funding for local capital projects and amplify constituent concerns about local issues, Pippy said 
Lewis Young said Frederick County did "far better than expected" with securing funds for capital projects this year, given the state budget challenges 
She said she expected to secure funding for only three or four projects, but the delegation was ultimately able to secure funding for eight 
On constituent concerns, Pippy said the Frederick County delegation has been "leading the fight" against the Maryland Piedmont Reliability Project (MPRP) - a proposed high-voltage transmission line that would go through Baltimore, Frederick and Carroll counties 
Several members of the Frederick County delegation this session filed bills taking aim at various aspects of the MPRP, including the seizure of land through eminent domain and the criteria for approving transmission line infrastructure 
The Public Service Commission, a state agency responsible for regulating public utilities, last month declined to start the review process for a certificate to construct the MPRP because the developer application was incomplete 
"The chilling effect of all of us coming together and putting in legislation     really kind of forced the hand of the Public Service Commission to essentially punt on that application," Pippy said  "We feel that we did make a difference "
