headline: Planning Commission votes not to recommend approval of Knapp's data center ordinance
text:
In a split vote, the Frederick County Planning Commission recommended on Wednesday that the County Council not approve the critical digital infrastructure ordinance proposed by Council Member Renee Knapp 
The Planning Commission voted 4-2 against recommending Knapp bill, which would build on a 2022 ordinance that added critical digital infrastructure facilities and substations to the zoning code as distinct uses  Commission Members Joel Rensberger and Tim Davis voted in favor of a motion to recommend approval of Knapp bill 
Commission Members Mark Long, Barbara Nicklas, Carole Sepe and Sam Tressler III were opposed 
Commission Member Craig Hicks was absent 
The County Council is not required to vote in line with the recommendations of the Planning Commission and could still decide to pass the bill 
In lieu of a favorable recommendation, the Planning Commission proposed a number of other provisions that could be amended into the bill or addressed in other legislation 
Chief among them is the creation of a critical digital infrastructure floating zone, which the County Council would have the authority to apply or not apply to any piece of land that has the right underlying zoning for data center development 
Per the 2022 ordinance, critical digital infrastructure can only be developed in the general and limited industrial zones 
The creation of a floating zone was a recommendation of County Executive Jessica Fitzwater data centers work group, which Knapp co-chaired 
The Fitzwater administration proposed, then pulled, a bill that would have established a critical digital infrastructure floating zone after expressing concerns that the zone could pit communities against each other and politicize the approval process for data centers 
Knapp bill does not address the floating zone issue, but states that critical digital infrastructure can only be developed on land that meets certain criteria, including:
o Being within two miles of existing high-voltage electric transmission lines
o Having a designation other than "no planned service" on the county water and sewerage plan
o Not being within a Priority Preservation Area, a Rural Legacy Area or a Treasured Landscape Management Area like the Sugarloaf region
Four of six members of the Planning Commission who were present on Wednesday supported the floating zone recommendation, including Sepe, Davis, Tressler and Nicklas  Rensberger was the only member opposed  Long abstained 
Rensberger said on Wednesday that he opposed the recommendation because businesses need certainty that a floating zone cannot provide  He also echoed Fitzwater concerns about politicization of the approval process for data centers 
"Skipping the County Council keeps it non-political  I think that a good thing," he said 
Nicklas said she appreciates that the bill would strengthen existing regulations on critical digital infrastructure 
However, Nicklas said a floating zone or other special use zone is needed, especially since the bill does not propose to limit the total number of critical digital infrastructure facilities that can be built in the county 
"Predictability is hugely important, however there are ways to deal with that with a floating zone," Nicklas said  "I feel it important for us to stay on top of how much of our limited land is being taken up by data centers "
A separate bill that would create a critical digital infrastructure floating zone has been put forward by County Council Members Steve McKay and Jerry Donald  The council is expected to workshop that bill in May 
Other proposed changes
In addition to the creation of a floating zone, the Planning Commission proposed several other regulations for critical digital infrastructure  They included:
o Requiring that critical digital infrastructure be located within one mile of existing high-voltage electric transmission lines, rather than within two miles
o Requiring that critical digital infrastructure site plans be reviewed by the county Division of Energy and Environment for compliance with sustainability goals
o Clarify what it means for a school, daycare facility, residence, house of worship, fragile ecosystem or watershed to be "in the vicinity of" critical digital infrastructure facilities when it comes to minimizing impacts
o Clarifying that any regulations applied to mechanical equipment also apply to electrical equipment for critical digital infrastructure facilities
o Requiring developers to provide estimates of daily maximum water demand, water storage volumes, total volumes of water returned to the county wastewater treatment plants and the compounds in that discharged water
Most of the recommendations were based on the Planning Commission previous experiences with evaluating site plans for data centers and other associated infrastructure 
"Several of the groups have come to us and said how they're trying to meet sustainability goals      In my view, it was a lot of green-washing," Long said  "I think it behooves us to put as much sustainability teeth in this as possible "
Tressler agreed that the county regulation of data centers needs to go beyond what is contained in Knapp bill 
"We don't know at this point how much energy they're actually going to use," Tressler said  "To this day, I don't know - I haven't had the figures "
The Planning Commission based its water use recommendation on suggestions from members of the public during Wednesday meeting 
Most of those who spoke on Wednesday also participated in the County Council public hearing on Knapp bill last month 
Knapp could not be reached by phone for comment following the vote on Wednesday  During the meeting, she urged the Planning Commission to keep in mind that there would be opportunities for further regulation in the future 
County Council President Brad Young has said that Knapp bill, which he co-sponsored, is expected to come back to the council for a third reading on May 6 
If the bill is amended that day, there will be another public hearing before the final vote

headline: Plug Power plans $84 million project in Camden County
text:
Jun  10-ATLANTA - Gov  Brian Kemp has announced that Plug Power, a leading global provider of hydrogen solutions, will invest $84 million in opening a green hydrogen fuel production plant in Camden County  Plug Power has deployed more than 40,000 fuel cell systems for e-mobility, delivering efficiency gains, fast fueling, and lower operational costs to its customers, and delivers 40 tons per day of hydrogen fuel to those customers 
"I'm glad to welcome Plug Power to the No  1 state for business," Kemp said in a news release  "As the home to several of Plug Power great Georgia-based customers, including Southern Company and The Home Depot, I'm confident they will find continued success and innovative partnerships in the Peach State "
Plug Power vertically integrated GenKey solution ties together critical elements to power and fuel and provides services to customers including Amazon, BMW, Southern Company, The Home Depot, Group Carrefour and Walmart  The company is continuing to expand to markets including zero-emission on-road vehicles, robotics and data centers  With close proximity to I-95 and strategically on the Eastern Seaboard, this new zero-emission fuel production plant will help the company better serve its industrial customers across the Southeastern U S 
"With this hydrogen production plant, we are expanding our green hydrogen network to provide zero-emissions fuel to customers in Georgia and across the Southeast," Andy Marsh, CEO of Plug Power, said  "Investing in Camden County is the right choice to support Plug Power continued growth "
Located at the Camden County Industrial Park in Kingsland, the company hydrogen fuel production facility will create 24 jobs in the county  As soon as summer 2022, Plug Power will hire for careers in operations, engineering, maintenance and logistics  Individuals interested in opportunities with the company are encouraged to visit plugpower com for additional information 
"We look forward to working with Plug Power and our state and local economic development partners on this exciting project that clearly demonstrates that Kingsland is open for business," Kingsland Mayor C  Grayson Day said 
Assistant Director Alyce Thornhill represented the Georgia Department of Economic Development Global Commerce division on this project in partnership with the Camden County Joint Development Authority, Georgia Ports Authority, Georgia Power, and Okefenokee EMC 
"Having Plug Power expand its footprint to Georgia is exciting news for this growing industry," Georgia Ports Authority Executive Director Griff Lynch said  "The Ports of Savannah and Brunswick provide the capacity needed for auto part imports and auto exports, making the I-95 corridor perfectly suited for this type of development "
"Georgia is a great place to do business, and because of that, we continue to attract the jobs of the future," GDEcD Commissioner Pat Wilson said  "Many thanks to Plug Power for choosing to invest in Georgia, and for helping bolster our green technology ecosystem  Congratulations to our partners in Camden County, and thank you for your efforts to help bring jobs to Georgia that positively impact our economy and our environment "

headline: POLITICAL NEWS & NOTES: Michigan House sues Secretary of State Benson; Coalition aims to boost school funding
text:
Jun  6-Michigan Political News & Notes is a weekly look at what happening in Lansing and around the state 
Here is a compilation of some of the week biggest headlines:
Michigan House sues Secretary of State Benson over election training records
KALAMAZOO - Michigan Republicans are suing the battleground state top elections executive over access to election training materials 
The lawsuit filed Thursday is the latest escalation in a brewing dispute that began when the GOP took majority control of the state House of Representatives last year, the Associated Press reports 
Since winning control of the chamber in the 2024 election, statehouse Republicans have repeatedly scrutinized the state election processes and Secretary of State Jocelyn Benson, a Democrat who is running for governor in 2026  The conflict comes as some state Republicans echo past false claims of election fraud in Michigan, which was a prime target of President Donald Trump and his backers after his 2020 election loss 
Republicans on the chamber Oversight Committee subpoenaed Benson in April, seeking access to training materials for local clerks and staff who administer elections, including access to the Bureau of Elections' online learning portal 
Benson office released some requested materials in response to the subpoena, but not all, citing cybersecurity and physical security concerns related to administering elections and the voting process  The office has said it needs to review the online portal for "sensitive information" and make redactions 
"Since the beginning of this saga, Secretary Benson has asked lawmakers to let a court review their request for sensitive election information that, in the wrong hands, would compromise the security of our election machines, ballots and officials," Michigan Department of State spokesperson Cheri Hardmon said in a statement Thursday 
House Republicans say the goal of reviewing the material is to ensure clerks are trained in accordance with Michigan law  The House voted along party lines in May to hold Benson in contempt for not completely complying with the subpoena 
The request for training materials originally came from GOP state Rep  Rachelle Smit, who has pushed false claims that the 2020 election was stolen  Smit is the chair of the House elections committee, which was renamed to the Elections Integrity Committee with the new Republican majority 
"Secretary Benson has proven she is unwilling to comply with our subpoena and Michigan law," Rep  Smit said in a statement Thursday  "She skirted the rules and done whatever she could to avoid public scrutiny  It become overwhelmingly clear that she will never release the training materials we're looking for without direction from a court "
The lawsuit asks the Michigan Court of Claims to intervene and compel Benson to comply with the subpoena 
"The public interest is best served if the constitutional order of the State of Michigan is preserved and the Legislature can properly perform its duty to regulate the manner of elections in the state and, if deemed necessary, enact election laws for the benefit of Michigan residents," the lawsuit says 
Benson gained national attention for defending the results of the 2020 election in the face of Trump attempts to undercut the outcome nationwide and in Michigan  Multiple audits - including one conducted by the then-Republican-controlled Michigan Senate - concluded former President Joe Biden won the state in 2020 and that there was no widespread or systemic fraud 
Benson has remained a subject of GOP scrutiny this year 
A Republican state representative introduced three articles of impeachment against Benson on Tuesday, and several of the accusations continue to cast doubts on the results of the 2020 election  With Democrats in control of the state Senate, it unlikely the impeachment articles will result in a conviction 
Secret Donors funneled millions through nonprofit backing Michigan GOP, court records reveal
Wealthy donors and corporations quietly funneled millions of dollars into a nonprofit aligned with Michigan Senate Republicans during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, newly released court records show - raising alarm among prosecutors and advocates for political transparency 
According to an investigation by the Detroit News, the nonprofit, Michigan Citizens for Fiscal Responsibility, took in $8 6 million between 2014 and 2020, all while operating under a 501(c)(4) tax designation that shields donor identities  According to records obtained through an ongoing criminal investigation, the group became a key conduit for undisclosed contributions to GOP-led initiatives, including the 2020 Unlock Michigan campaign - a successful ballot drive to repeal Gov  Gretchen Whitmer emergency powers 
Then-Senate Majority Leader Mike Shirkey, R-Clark Lake, and GOP strategist Heather Lombardini promoted the nonprofit as a discreet option for donors 
"No one will know," Lombardini wrote to one potential contributor in an email submitted as evidence 
Contributions came from GOP megadonor Dick DeVos ($50,000), Las Vegas-based Switch data center ($50,000), charter school executive J C  Huizenga ($25,000) and construction magnate Edward Levy ($30,000) 
From mid-Michigan, Garber Management Group of Saginaw contributed $20,000  Other regional donations included $3,334 from Bay Aggregates of Bay City and $3,333 each from Central Concrete Products based in Mount Pleasant, Alma and Clare and Fisher Sand and Gravel of Midland, and $2,000 from Vantage Plastics of Standish 
Health Alliance Plan (HAP) gave $20,000 through the nonprofit just weeks after parent company Henry Ford Health furloughed nearly 3,000 workers amid the pandemic  Internal emails show HAP worried about the optics, preferring to avoid public disclosure 
In all, $1 8 million flowed from the nonprofit to Unlock Michigan - money that would have required disclosure if donated directly to the ballot committee  Prosecutors argue the arrangement violated state campaign finance law 
Lombardini has been charged with forgery and perjury for allegedly lying under oath about the money intended use 
Attorney General Dana Nessel office says the case highlights how "dark money" can distort democracy 
With over $1 2 million in previously unknown contributions revealed, the case is expected to shape future efforts to tighten Michigan campaign finance laws ahead of the 2026 elections 
Whitmer warns SNAP cuts could cost Michigan $900 million per year
Proposed federal changes to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) could cost Michigan nearly $900 million a year, according to Gov  Gretchen Whitmer administration 
A report released June 4 by the State Budget Office estimates the shift would force Michigan to either cover the federal shortfall or cut other programs like education, Medicaid or public safety, according to the Detroit Free Press 
The proposal is part of a sweeping federal spending bill championed by former President Donald Trump and passed by the U S  House on May 22 by a single vote 
"In Michigan, we will fight to make sure our kids and families are fed," Whitmer said  "But we need Republicans in our congressional delegation to step up for their own constituents who need SNAP and Medicaid to survive "
Currently, the federal government pays the full cost of SNAP benefits and half of administrative expenses  Under the bill, states would have to fund at least 5% of benefits and see their share of administrative costs rise from 50% to 75%  States with higher error rates in payments could be forced to pay even more-up to 25% of benefit costs  Michigan error rate in 2023 was over 10%, per USDA data 
In total, Michigan could owe up to $800 million in benefits and $90 million in administrative costs each year 
Republicans say the changes promote work and reduce fraud  The bill expands work requirements for SNAP recipients ages 55 to 64 who don't care for young children and limits state waivers tied to unemployment 
"If these cuts are signed into law, more Michiganders will go to bed with a pit in their stomach," she said  "That unacceptable "
The Senate is expected to consider the bill before July 4 
MSU urges International Students to stay in U S  amid visa restrictions
EAST LANSING - Michigan State University is urging its international students not to leave the United States this summer, citing new federal restrictions on student visas and growing uncertainty about reentry into the country, according to a report from Michigan Advance 
The warning came in an email sent Tuesday by Krista McCallum, director of MSU Office for International Students and Scholars (OISS), who cited recent federal actions affecting both current international students and those still seeking to enter the country  McCallum "strongly" advised students already in the U S  to avoid international travel until further notice 
The message followed a cable signed by U S  Secretary of State Marco Rubio stating that, under President Trump direction, the government would halt new visa interviews and aggressively revoke visas held by Chinese nationals connected to the Chinese Communist Party or studying in sensitive fields 
McCallum email sparked concern among MSU international community, where students say recent policy changes have made travel feel risky 
"It no longer just about paperwork," Diego Granados, a Mexican PhD student in biochemistry, told Michigan Advance  "Crossing the border now comes with real risk "
MSU spokesperson Amber McCann said the university remains committed to its international students and is "closely monitoring" federal developments 
Some students say the university response feels inadequate 
"They just want to play it safe," said Granados  "But now we feel trapped - like we might not be able to come back if we leave "
Hall, Schuette push for new regulatory committee
House Speaker Matt Hall, R-Richland Township, announced plans to replace the Joint Committee on Administrative Rules (JCAR) with a new, more aggressive regulatory oversight panel aimed at curbing what he describes as excessive red tape imposed by the Whitmer administration 
According to MIRS, Hall said he directing House Rules Committee Chair Bill G  Schuette, R-Midland, to deliver a regulatory reform report in response to Gov  Gretchen Whitmer 2025 State of the State call to reduce regulatory burdens 
"Do you remember when Gretchen Whitmer got up at the State of the State and said we need regulatory reform?" Hall said  "I couldn't stand up out of my chair faster  I said, 'Bill G  Schuette, you better run through that window before it closes '"
Hall promised the new committee would have more power than JCAR and would be able to block or amend proposed rules much earlier in the process  JCAR, currently composed of five Democrats and five Republicans, cannot stop new rules without bipartisan agreement - a limitation Hall aims to bypass 
Schuette report states that Michigan regulatory environment is stifling business 
"It can take up to three times as long to get a permit in Michigan as it does in Ohio or Indiana," he said, adding that complex rules are worsening issues like the childcare shortage and rising housing costs 
The new committee is expected to roll out within the next 30 days  Hall said he hopes the Senate will follow the House lead in reclaiming legislative authority over regulations 
Snyder eyes ballot proposal, says Michigan schools 'In dire shape'
LANSING - Former Michigan Gov  Rick Snyder says he preparing to reenter the political arena - not for himself, but "for the kids "
Speaking to MIRS News and The Detroit News, Snyder said he likely to lead a 2026 statewide ballot initiative focused on overhauling Michigan struggling K-12 education system, which he described as falling behind even historically lower-performing states like Mississippi 
"We are at the early formation stages of a concerted campaign," Snyder said 
The former Republican governor is calling for a broad coalition - labor, business, legislators and educators - to help draft a plan  One of his top priorities is improving third grade reading scores, long seen as a key predictor of student success 
While no formal ballot language has been finalized, Snyder said a statewide proposal is "likely in 2026 " He hinted at a $20 - 30 million campaign effort, with plans for a statewide bus tour to engage voters  Whether the changes will ultimately be placed before voters or pursued legislatively will be decided in early 2026 
Snyder approach echoes past reform efforts like Launch Michigan and the Growing Michigan Together Council - both of which called for collaboration across sectors, but whose recommendations have seen little action 
The campaign would emerge alongside a competing school funding proposal from the Michigan Education Justice Coalition, the AFT, and others, who are backing a ballot measure to raise taxes on the wealthy and direct an estimated $4 billion in new revenue to public schools 
Snyder, by contrast, said new revenue isn't the starting point  "We will not lead with that," he told MIRS, though he acknowledged funding "would be on the table "
Describing the effort as nonpartisan, Snyder emphasized urgency: "This is for the kids "
He cited calls of support following remarks he made at the Detroit Regional Chamber Mackinac Policy Conference 
"Our performance is terrible," Snyder said  "We're in the 40th percentile  That unacceptable "
Report: DOJ Pardon Attorney Weighs Clemency for Michigan GOP false electors
A top U S  Department of Justice official is reportedly considering federal clemency for a wide group of individuals connected to former President Donald Trump - including Michigan 2020 false electors and two men convicted of plotting to kidnap Gov  Gretchen Whitmer 
According to a Mother Jones report published Wednesday, DOJ pardon attorney Ed Martin Jr  is crafting a framework that would grant pardons to Trump allies facing criminal charges related to efforts to overturn the 2020 election, as well as those tied to the violent extremism surrounding it 
Among those under consideration: the 15 Michigan Republicans charged by Attorney General Dana Nessel office for submitting false electoral certificates in 2020  The group includes former Michigan GOP co-chair Meshawn Maddock and former RNC member Kathleen Berden  Charges range from forgery to conspiracy to commit election fraud 
A source familiar with Martin efforts said the Michigan electors are "next in line" for federal clemency consideration, according to Michigan Advance 
Martin also confirmed that Barry Croft and Adam Fox - convicted in federal court for orchestrating the failed 2020 plot to kidnap Whitmer from her northern Michigan vacation home - are on his clemency list 
"We'll be a little bit more outward-facing in terms of talking about what happening," Martin said during a May press conference outlining his plans  "The law can be taken up, but the starting point is to make sure that the public sees that we're doing it "
The proposed clemency campaign appears designed to recast Trump post-election conduct in a more favorable light, with Martin describing it as a bid to "wipe away" the memory of the former president attempts to stay in power after losing to Joe Biden 
The pardon considerations are expected to escalate partisan tensions in Michigan, where trials are pending and public opinion remains sharply divided on Trump legacy and ongoing influence 
Coalition pushes ballot measure to tax wealthy, boost school funding
A statewide coalition is pushing a 2026 ballot initiative to increase taxes on Michigan wealthiest residents and use the revenue to better fund the state under-resourced public schools, according to Michigan Advance 
The group, Invest in MI Kids, announced Thursday that it plans to place a constitutional amendment on the November 2026 ballot that would raise income taxes on individuals earning more than $500,000 and couples making more than $1 million annually  The proposal would add a 5% income tax surcharge to those earners, amending Michigan current flat 4 25% income tax rate 
The plan would generate an estimated $1 7 billion annually, directing the funds to Michigan School Aid Fund to address longstanding education inequities, reduce class sizes, expand career and technical education, and help attract and retain teachers - especially in underserved areas 
"It time for the wealthiest to pay their fair share," said Molly Sweeney, organizing director of 482 Forward  "Michigan flat tax was designed to favor the rich, not support working families or public education "
Supporters say Michigan schools are underfunded by $4 billion to $5 billion annually  Backers will need to collect about 600,000 signatures to qualify the measure for the 2026 ballot 
Organizers say the measure reflects growing frustration with lawmakers' failure to adequately fund education 
"Most people agree the system broken," said Rachelle Crow-Hercher of the Michigan Education Justice Coalition  "This ballot initiative addresses the funding part - and we're listening to people across the state about what policy changes they want too "
The campaign has support from the American Federation of Teachers Michigan, dozens of grassroots groups, and student advocates like Christina Yarn, a senior at Heritage High School in Saginaw 
"We're suffering in schools that are falling apart," Yarn said  "No buses, unsafe buildings, poor water and air  We've been forgotten  This can change that "
(c) 2025 the Midland Daily News (Midland, Mich )  Visit www ourmidland com  Distributed by Tribune Content Agency, LLC

headline: Portaltic.-Kingston Announces Availability of DC1500M for Mixed-Use Workloads in Data Centers
text:
MADRID, 28 (Portaltic/EP)
 Kingston announced on Monday the availability of the DC1500M storage unit, designed to support mixed-use workloads in data centers, where it offers constant read/write latencies 
 DC1500M is a U 2 NVMe SSD hard drive offering increased resiliency with constant latency and IOPS performance, thanks to a high-performance Gen 3 0 x4 PCIe NVMe design, as highlighted by the company in a statement 
 The DC1500M supports multiple storage drives, virtualization and web hosting environments, and its broad application: "from high-performance cloud services, file capture and movement to big data applications," said Tony Hollingsbee, SSD Business Manager at Kingston EMEA 
 The DC1500M features the U 2 (universal, 2 5-inch) form factor and allows Gen 3 0 and Gen 4 0 servers and storage arrays to use existing PCIe slots and U 2 motherboards, while maintaining system serviceability without rebooting 
 Kingston has designed the new drive from the "success" of U 2 NVMe SSDs, enabling the DC1500M to feature advanced corporate-level features that improve long-term performance in high-demand environments such as end-to-end data path protection, multi-drive storage drive management , power loss protection (PLP), and telemetry to increase data center reliability 
 The tech company has reported that DC1500M is available in capacities of 960GB, 1 92TB, 3 84TB and 7 68TB

headline: Power companies hit brakes on renewables citing oversupply
text:
Power companies in Brazil are reassessing their investment strategies in generation and redirecting funds to more conservative, predictable sectors due to an oversupply in the country  Other factors playing a role include significant price volatility, which undermines the viability of new renewable projects, and generation cuts imposed by the Operator of the National Electricity System (ONS) due to low demand 
This market instability, exacerbated by subsidies and inefficient fund allocation, has prompted companies to be more cautious  For instance, the French company Engie is no longer interested in investing in greenfield renewable energy generation projects, citing low electricity market prices deemed insufficient to justify starting projects from scratch 
CEO Eduardo Sattamini said Engie has increased its stake in other sectors, such as energy transmission, to achieve a balanced revenue stream  This segment is the safest in the industry, being fully regulated  The auction winner secures a 30-year contract indexed to the Extended Consumer Price Index (IPCA), eliminating default risks  In this context, Engie is likely to pursue opportunities including acquiring contracted projects offering lower risk and better revenue predictability 
"To ensure long-term growth, it is key to efficiently manage the portfolio, maintaining a healthy balance between revenue sources and mitigating risks, especially in a volatile environment," he states  Mr  Sattamini explained that Engie energy generation portfolio of 9 6 GW of 100% renewable installed capacity is primarily made up of hydropower and supplementary sources, such as wind and solar  "Additionally, we have transmission lines that support the distribution of energy from production centers to consumer hubs," Mr  Sattamini said 
Portugal-based EDP has also indicated a change in direction  In December, CEO Jo~ao Marques da Cruz told journalists that the company does not plan to invest in new energy generation assets in the coming years due to insufficient demand and low prices 
EDP has invested in a hydrogen plant but admitted it is not a profitable venture without subsidies to develop the technology in Brazil  Now, the focus shifts to distribution and transmission segments, with planned investments of R$11 billion over the next five years  EDP declined to provide further details when contacted 
Auren decided to develop the Caju'ina 3 project, part of the Caju'ina complex (1 and 2)  However, due to both structural and situational factors, the expansion of the Tucano 2 wind farm will remain on hold  Additionally, the main focus is on deleveraging the company after acquiring AES 
"We haven't progressed initially [in new projects], the company focus is now on Caju'ina 3  From 2027, we'll reevaluate  We've stopped pursuing Tucano 2," said CEO Fabio Zanfelice 
The abundant energy supply, driven by subsidies and lacking proper planning, has become a challenge for the country, which now struggles to absorb this surplus  Data from the Brazilian Electricity Regulatory Agency (ANEEL) show that the electrical grid increased by 10 9 GW in 2024, the largest expansion since official records began  Of this total, over 90% of the installed capacity came from wind and solar sources  A similar pace is expected for 2025 
This substantial growth in energy generation capacity in Brazil in recent years-far exceeding demand growth-renders new projects unfeasible  Galp has abandoned plans to develop solar generation projects previously outlined in its portfolio in the country 
In this context, consumer tariffs will continue to rise due to subsidies and sector charges, energy contracting beyond needs, and costs with thermal plants to stabilize the system, among other expenses 
"Who will invest when there no certainty of selling the energy? Distributed generation in consumer centers and homes produces so much energy that it displaces these enterprises, which have contracts but can't fulfill them  They can't sell because they're cut off," explains Professor Nivalde Jos'e de Castro, coordinator of the Electricity Sector Study Group (GESEL) at UFRJ 
In this case, the issue is the national grid operator curtailment of renewable generation  This happens when energy production exceeds transmission or consumption capacity  "The origin of the crisis in the electric sector is micro and mini-distributed generation, which creates a 'gold rush' for subsidies," Mr  Nivalde argues 
Canada-based asset manager Brookfield invests in the Brazilian electric sector but has been reassessing its strategy  Elera, Brookfield energy generation arm, has struggled from ONS cuts  The situation causes losses for various companies, impacting their market value and hindering their ability to raise funds  The asset manager is considering new investment routes such as sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), reforestation, and biogas  Brookfield declined to comment 
Elbia Gannoum, president of ABEE'olica-an association representing wind energy companies-pointed out that the excess of subsidies and the decline in contracting new projects since 2022 have led to losses in the sector  "2024 was our first low year  In 2023, we broke installation records with 5 GW  Last year, we installed 3 3 GW of wind energy  In 2025, it will be about 2 8 GW "
Ms  Gannoum suggests that new segments could absorb this energy supply, such as data centers and the production of green fertilizer and hydrogen 
The withdrawal of companies from new renewable energy projects has triggered a ripple effect leading to deindustrialization in the Brazilian wind power sector  GE Renewable Energy announced a suspension of new turbine production in Brazil  Siemens Gamesa and WEG have halted operations to adjust their production structures, while LM Wind Power shut down a wind blade factory 
Petrobras was seen as a driving force for the sector recovery in Brazil and had partnered with WEG to support the wind sector production chain  The company mentioned studying the acquisition of operational renewable projects (brownfield), but nothing has materialized so far  Asked about the trend of oil companies divesting from renewables, Petrobras stated it has increased low-carbon investments in its business plan, even though most investments are still directed toward oil and gas 
Internationally, major oil companies have been redirecting investments to more predictable markets or segments with higher profitability  Shell has halted new investments in offshore wind projects as part of a new global strategy focused on cost reduction  Equinor revised its renewable generation targets through 2030 and cut renewable and low-carbon solutions investments by 50% between 2025 and 2027  Chevron is reducing investments in low-carbon energies by 25%, and BP has dropped its 2020 goal to cut oil and gas production by 40% by 2030 
These cases can be partially explained by the policy shift under the Trump administration, which cut all renewable energy projects and aims to boost fossil fuel production, where the U S  is already a global leader 
While it early to declare a trend, the U S  retreat from the energy transition may lead to a redirection of investment flows 
The GESEL coordinator points out that investments in renewables are expected to continue in other countries  The ABEE'olica leader believes the U S  context could benefit the Brazilian wind sector  "Trump rollback is advantageous for us  Brazil is a renewable energy powerhouse and could export that to other countries," she predicts 
(Essa publicac~ao 'e parte integrante do jornal Valor Econ^omico e se sujeita aos termos de uso do site )

headline: Power Companies Reach $16.4 Billion Deal as A.I. Drives Energy Use Up
text:
Constellation Energy deal to buy Calpine is being driven by fast-rising demand for electricity in part by the technology industry investments in artificial intelligence 
Constellation Energy, the nation largest nuclear power plant operator, has agreed to buy another electricity producer, Calpine, for $16 4 billion, a deal that shows how fast-rising demand for power, partly a result of the data centers being built for artificial intelligence, is having far-reaching effects on the economy 
The cash-and-stock deal, announced Friday, ranks among the power sector biggest, and indicates that natural gas is likely to play a larger role than many expected a few years ago in meeting the nation electricity needs  That could undermine efforts to address climate change unless companies quickly figure out how to capture and store emissions from gas power plants 
The tie-up would broaden Constellation portfolio as companies like Microsoft, Google and Amazon are scrambling to secure energy for data centers used to run artificial intelligence and other services  Electricity demand is also increasing because of the building of new factories in the United States and greater use of electric vehicles and heat pumps  The growth is reshaping a traditionally sleepy industry that has not been accustomed to turbocharged growth 
"Lots of people who were not paying any attention to electricity a year ago are now trying to figure out how to participate in meeting what seems to be inevitable growth in demand," said Daniel Yergin, the vice chairman of S&P Global, who won a Pulitzer Prize for his book "The Prize: The Epic Quest For Oil, Money and Power "
Calpine, which is based in Houston and privately held, operates a large fleet of natural gas power plants in several states as well as the Geysers geothermal energy complex in California 
Constellation, which is based in Baltimore, said in a statement that it expected Calpine natural gas assets to help ensure the reliability of the electric grid  The combination also would broaden the company presence in Texas, where power demand is growing quickly, and add more renewable energy to its portfolio 
"We believe that natural gas and geothermal, along with nuclear, will be critically important for the nation," Joseph Dominguez, chief executive of Constellation, said on a call with investors and analysts on Friday morning 
He added that it was important to ensure that energy resources were not only sustainable, but reliable as well  "We believe that natural gas and clean energy, blended together, will be very attractive to customers," Mr  Dominguez said 
Constellation stock price soared more than 20 percent in early trading on Friday and closed the day up 25 percent, an unusually large jump for an acquiring company  Its shares had already more than doubled over the past year as expectations for U S  power demand growth rose 
Constellation would pay $4 5 billion in cash and assume roughly $12 7 billion of Calpine debt as part of the deal 
Nuclear power plants, which can operate around the clock without releasing planet-warming emissions, have been among the early beneficiaries of booming investment in artificial intelligence  Constellation agreed last year to spend $1 6 billion to restart a nuclear reactor at Three Mile Island near Harrisburg, Pa  - a project for which Microsoft is effectively footing the bill 
But there are only a few mothballed nuclear plants that can be restarted  Some companies are also betting on new, smaller reactors, but those are not expected to begin producing meaningful amounts of power for at least several years if all goes well 
As a result of those challenges, many energy and tech companies are increasingly looking to natural gas, even though its use releases carbon dioxide and methane, two leading greenhouse gases that are warming the planet 
"It going to be hard for the utilities to provide the power that these data centers need without gas," said Andrew Gillick, an energy strategist for the analytics firm Enverus 
Power demand from data centers is poised to increase 15 percent a year on average through the end of the decade, Goldman Sachs estimated last year 
Andrew Novotny, chief executive of Calpine, said the combined company would be able to invest in new power generation  "Together, we will be better positioned to bring accelerated investment in everything from zero-emission nuclear to battery storage that will power our economy in a way that puts people and our environment first," he said in a statement 
A diverse group of power plants could let the new company be more effective in how it manages its resources, depending on how electricity needs change  Adding more natural gas to its portfolio would, however, expose Constellation to more risk related to fluctuating commodity prices, Enverus said 
The deal with Constellation is the culmination of a big turnaround for Calpine, which had come under pressure in recent years as California and other states sought to move away from fossil fuels  A group of investors including Energy Capital Partners took Calpine private several years ago in a deal valued at $5 6 billion, not including debt 
The companies said they expected the transaction to close within a year, subject to regulatory approvals  Constellation would address any potential concerns raised by antitrust officials about its market power by selling assets, Mr  Dominguez said 
Ivan Penn contributed reporting 
Ivan Penn contributed reporting  
PHOTO: Calpine natural gas power plants, like this one in Texas, are increasingly valuable because of the rising demand for electricity from data centers  (PHOTOGRAPH BY TAMIR KALIFA FOR THE NEW YORK TIMES) This article appeared in print on page B4

headline: POWER NEEDS ARE STAGGERING; GOVERNMENT, UTILITIES GRAPPLE WITH GROWTH; MINNESOTA | MEGA DATA CENTERS
text:
Facebook parent company is building Minnesota first mega data center in Rosemount to house its fast-growing need for computing muscle 
Amazon and Microsoft bought land for large data centers near Xcel Energy soon-retiring coal plant in Becker  A Colorado company called Tract has advanced a project in Farmington and is eyeing colossal sites in Rosemount and Cannon Falls  Other companies want to build data centers in Chaska, Faribault, North Mankato and Hampton 
If built, this crop of data centers could demand as much electricity as every home in Minnesota 
State and local officials as well as electric utilities are grappling with how to manage this explosive growth while keeping the lights on and complying with laws for a transition to clean power 
Lagging power supply on the 15-state regional grid has spurred warnings of blackouts starting this summer  The data centers are already raising concerns about whether they will prolong the burning of fossil fuels for electricity even as Minnesota requires a carbon-free grid by 2040 
"There so many different utilities and industries and businesses who have done a lot of work on decarbonization in Minnesota," said Amelia Vohs, climate program director at the Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy  "It feels a little bit like this one industry has a real possibility to significantly erode that "
Still, many state and local officials welcome the projects, which could shower Minnesota with new carbon-free power infrastructure, construction jobs, tax revenue and, potentially, lower electric bills for everyone 
The server farms are critical to services such as medical records, financial transactions, streaming media, navigation apps, email and remote work  The average household has 21 devices connected to the internet, from watches to bird feeders  The rise of artificial intelligence products has accelerated the construction of these centers, massive buildings that house banks of computers using enough electricity to power cities 
"All of that is generating and consuming more and more data than ever before," said Aaron Tinjum, vice president of energy for the industry trade group Data Center Coalition  "It an industry that has really become, effectively, the lifeblood of our modern economy "
Interviews with two dozen people representing data center companies, local officials, environmental nonprofits and electric utilities, as well as public records, shed new light on the size and scope of these projects in a planning process that developers often make intentionally opaque 
New horizon
Minnesota was not a large data center market in the past  Meta Platforms' project in Rosemount is the farthest along in what could be a surge of large-scale projects  At least nine other facilities are in development 
Some cite lower cooling costs from Minnesota weather, access to fiber infrastructure, the Twin Cities' strong workforce and proximity to the large metro area  Xcel is one of many to argue the state carbon-free law is a boon because tech companies want to meet their own climate goals 
Helping to draw these data centers are state tax breaks on energy sales, equipment and software enacted in 2011  It started as a relatively small program worth about $7 3 million a decade ago  It has since grown to often more than $100 million annually - before the arrival of colossal data centers 
About three dozen states have tax breaks for equipment and are "table stakes" to attract big data centers, said Tract chief investment officer Graham Williams 
Williams said data centers are popping up in Minnesota after becoming too concentrated in places like Chicago and Virginia, where companies are fighting for real estate and resources like space on congested power lines 
"What they realized is they have to have some outlets for that, for future growth," Williams said 
Minnesota may be a secondary market for data centers, but the centers' power needs would still be massive 
2 3 million homes
Xcel anticipates supplying data centers with 1,300 megawatts in Minnesota and the Dakotas over the next seven years  Great River Energy, a Maple Grove-based nonprofit, is planning 1,000 megawatts of new demand in a similar time frame 
That amount of power demand between Xcel and Great River would be roughly equivalent to Minnesota 2 3 million households 
Xcel is building one of the nation largest solar farms in Sherburne County, a project seen as a landmark for carbon-free progress in Minnesota  That can generate only enough electricity for 150,000 homes 
Even before the data center boom, utilities were preparing for higher demand as people switch to electric vehicles and home heating systems 
Minnesota in 2024 made it faster to permit and build clean energy  Still, it can take several years to build wind and solar projects and as much as a decade for large transmission lines to carry electricity 
"My analysts are skeptical about rapidly delivering power of any sort, much less clean power, in the size and time frames that the data centers are likely to request," Pete Wyckoff, a deputy commissioner at the Minnesota Department of Commerce, told utility regulators in October 
High-need focus
In February, Xcel announced plans to add more than 3,000 megawatts of new wind energy by the end of the decade, plus a major amount of solar and batteries  Now the company says it might need more  But Xcel said it can still follow through with closing its two big coal plants and comply with Minnesota 2040 carbon-free deadline 
Xcel long-range plans include a new natural gas "peaking" plant in Lyon County which would kick in during hot summer days or other high-need times  The new data centers make Xcel more likely to extend some contracts to buy electricity from third-party gas plants into the 2030s, said Ryan Long, Xcel president in Minnesota 
In March, the PUC approved Great River plan to shift from having about a third of its power from carbon-free sources last year to 90% by 2035  The data center demands mean that plan is already outdated, according to Zac Ruzycki, director of resource planning for Great River  Great River will have to build or buy extra power that might include gas despite a surplus of electricity on its system now, Ruzycki said 
Ruzycki noted Minnesota 2040 law allows utilities to burn fossil fuels to maintain a reliable system and account for those emissions by purchasing renewable energy credits for clean power elsewhere 
Reducing carbon
Still, some believe data centers - and the deep pockets of their owners - will ultimately help the switch to carbon-free power 
Google told the PUC in October it buying the power from a geothermal plant in Nevada, and the tech industry has purchased a tremendous amount of renewable electricity across the country 
Beyond wind and solar, some data center companies are paying to revive nuclear plants such as Three Mile Island in Pennsylvania or investing in emerging technology for smaller reactors 
The data center companies most interested in Minnesota "don't want to show up and have somebody build a gas plant to serve them," said Audrey Partridge, director of policy for the nonprofit Twin Cities-based Center for Energy and Environment  "That would be a problem for them at the corporate level "
No tech companies have publicly committed to building major clean power projects with local utilities so far in Minnesota 
Vohs, with the MCEA, said it would be a "huge gift to our electric system" if data center owners did that, but it not required 
Meta says it will buy enough renewable energy to account for any fossil fuels burned for its Rosemount project  But that might not directly help Xcel, its climate goals or its customers 
Xcel spokesman Theo Keith said Meta plans to purchase renewable energy credits  Their electric contract does not require Meta to build anything specifically for the power company 
Graphic
 
Ren'ee Jones Schneider, Minnesota Star Tribune Facebook parent company, Meta Platforms Inc , is building Minnesota first mega data center in Rosemount, an estimated $800 million project

headline: Power Struggle: Residents worry about environmental impacts, losing their homes if MPRP is built
text:
This story is part of an occasional series about the Maryland Piedmont Reliability Project (MPRP), a proposed 500,000-volt transmission line to cross three counties, and its potential impacts on people living and working in Frederick County  The project is being developed by the Public Service Enterprise Group (PSEG) 
Jay and Alicia Weinrich live in a log house off Black Ankle Road in Mount Airy, nestled in trees and tranquility, at the end of a narrow, steep driveway on top of a hill 
Jay is originally from Howard County, and Alicia is from Carroll County  The couple bought their property in Frederick County in 2003 with the intention of building the home they'd live in for the rest of their lives 
With the help of family and friends for more than a year and a half, the couple constructed their house from the ground up, touching all the logs that make the home and staining everything in the basement 
The Weinrichs officially moved into the house in 2005  It where they raised their son, who has moved away 
Alicia said they chose this property because it "kind of in the middle of nowhere "
The stars come out at night because the sky is clear, and the view is beautiful, filled with trees and, in the summer, hay when farmers are working the land 
She said deer run across their property and cross over the driveway, drinking from a nearby stream filled with trout  The couple also sees and hears bald eagles frequently 
"This was really our dream - he wanted a log cabin, and I just wanted to have some peace and quiet," she said 
The MPRP could shatter that dream entirely 
MPRP- Jay and Alicia Weinrich
Jay, left, and Alicia Weinrich stand for a portrait at their home in Mount Airy on April 30  In the early 2000s, the couple constructed their log house with the help of their family and friends  Now, the final route of the Maryland Piedmont Reliability Project (MPRP) would cut through their property  If the MPRP is built, the Weinrichs don't know what they'll do, and they're hoping the project doesn't move forward 
Residents like the Weinrichs, as well as farmers and other property owners, are fighting a staunch battle against the project  More than 170 petitions to intervene were submitted to Maryland for an application PSEG submitted for a certificate to build the project 
A petition to intervene is a request to become a formal party in a case and be able to actively participate in the legal process and influence the outcome of a project 
Additionally, a nonprofit called Stop MPRP formed after the project was announced  Its mission is to advocate for people impacted by the project and stop it entirely, and it a resource many opponents of the MPRP turn to 
The Weinrichs submitted a petition to intervene on Feb  10  They've never had anyone approach them asking for a right-of-way to build something through the property 
If the MPRP is built, Alicia said, it'll be placed right over the Weinrichs' fence next to their home, cross over their chicken coop and the driveway, and hang over the trout stream at the bottom of the hill the house is on 
What is the MPRP?
The MPRP is slated to be nearly 70 miles long and would go through northern Baltimore County, the middle of Carroll County and parts of Frederick County, ending at the Doubs substation 
The project is meant to help meet anticipated power demands on the regional power grid operated by PJM Interconnection, which coordinates the movement of electricity in 13 states - including all of Maryland - and Washington, D C 
As part of developing the project, PSEG has applied to the state for a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity, which would allow the company to build the transmission line should its application be approved 
In addition to the hundreds of properties in the route path, there are 58 parcels that PSEG added to its application as possible places for access roads, which the company would use while constructing the MPRP 
PSEG spokesperson Bill Smith said that it possible for the MPRP route to be adjusted or changed during the application process 
Smith said that PSEG has "engaged with residents and property owners to address their concerns regarding agriculture, safety and property values" since the MPRP was announced and will continue to do so 
He also said PSEG does not have eminent domain rights - a governmental power to seize private property for public use with just compensation - without its certificate application being approved 
On April 15, PSEG filed a request for a court order to gain temporary access to properties on or along the MPRP route to conduct environmental and field studies as part of its certificate application 
MPRP route and county outlines
Alicia said she estimates 50% of the trees on her property will be cut down for the project  The stream will get too hot, killing all the trout 
"I'll be able to stand there at my kitchen sink and see the top post holding wires," she said  "And then, to top it off, that beautiful view that I have - this will all be gone because they're taking out all of my trees "
The couple said when PSEG first proposed multiple routes for the MPRP, there were three in the general area of their home  Only one of those routes - the one that was ultimately picked - cut through the Weinrichs' property 
The couple attended one of PSEG public information sessions it held after the final MPRP route was announced  Jay said he also sent letters to many Maryland elected officials sharing the couple story 
Jay said he feels like all the discussion about the project is about the farms people are losing, but not about anyone else 
MPRP- Jay and Alicia Weinrich
Alicia, left, and Jay Weinrich pose together outside at their home in Mount Airy on March 20  The Weinrichs moved into the log house in 2005 after building it with the help of family and friends  Now, they are worried how the MPRP will affect their property and the environment around them 
"There was very little discussion about those of us that just have a home on five acres  It was all about farmland," he said  "I felt like we were kind of the forgotten ones "
For Alicia, the MPRP poses a unique health risk  She has a spinal cord stimulator, a medical device that sends low-level electric pulses into her spinal cord to relieve pain 
When she goes to a doctor office and cautery tools are used to stop bleeding, she has to turn the stimulator off because otherwise, it'll start shocking her  It could also turn off improperly and may not come back on 
If one tool can cause that, she wonders what would happen if she were living under a 500,000-volt transmission line 
"What that going to do to my back? Is it going to shut my spinal cord stimulator off completely? I just don't know," Alicia said 
The Weinrichs don't know what they'll do if the MPRP is built  Right now, they're just hoping it doesn't move forward at all - they've told people who asked to enter the property to evaluate it to not come 
Just the thought of having the transmission line on the property, Alicia said, "makes me want to just run "
MPRP- Jay and Alicia Weinrich
Shown through a window, Alicia and Jay Weinrich stand for a portrait with their dogs at their home in Mount Airy on April 30  In the early 2000s, the couple constructed their log house with the help of their family and friends  Now, the final route of the MPRP would cut through their property  If the MPRP is built, the Weinrichs don't know what they'll do, and they're hoping the project doesn't move forward 
MPRP- Jay and Alicia Weinrich
Jay, left, and Alicia Weinrich pose together with DeDe inside their home in Mount Airy on March 20  The Weinrichs moved into the log house in 2005 after building it with the help of family and friends  Now, they are worried how the MPRP will affect their property and the environment around them 
Margie and Steve Schroeder
Margie and Steve Schroeder live on and own a 25-acre parcel of land off Ijamsville Road  Their home has a room with windows for walls where the sunlight spills in and the couple can look out at a sprawling field 
MPRP- Steve and Margie Schroeder
Margie, left, and Steve Schroeder pose together in the sun room of their home on March 27  They built the house sun room as an add-on to the home to get a better view of the outdoors  This year marks their 50th year living in the home  All of the routes PSEG proposed for the MPRP cut through the Schroeders' property 
The property was originally a 220-acre parcel where the first settler of Ijamsville, Plummer Ijams, built a plantation, according to the Schroeders 
Over time, the parcel has been divided up, and several of the Schroeders' family members - like their daughter, nephew and grandchildren - live on other pieces of the land 
Margie said her father bought the entire property in 1950  He was a lawyer working in Washington, D C , but he always had wanted to run a farm 
When she was a child, she did chores on her family dairy farm, and the family also owned horses and hunted foxes 
Margie met Steve while attending college in North Carolina, and when the couple moved to Frederick County after Steve finished dental school, Margie father gave them the land where their house now stands 
This year marks their 50th year living there 
The couple nephew, Josh Nicodemus, owns the family farm to the west of their home, and their daughter and grandchildren live in another house built on a giant parcel  Margie sister also lives in a tenant house on the land 
Since the family lives so close to each other, it common for the grandchildren to walk over to see the Schroeders  They love to sled on the field when it blanketed in snow in the winter 
If the MPRP gets built, the route would put the line over the Schroeders' pool and into the woods, requiring trees to be removed for the right-of-way 
MPRP- Steve and Margie Schroeder
Cousins James Duncan, 14, left, and Sawyer Smith, 11, play in the pool at Margie and Steve Schroeder home on April 30  If the MPRP is built, the line would go over the Schroeders' pool and into the woods 
All of the routes PSEG presented for the MPRP would've gone through the Schroeders' property 
Margie said the couple worst case scenario is that they could lose their house  Even if the power line is put farther out toward the field next to the home, it would divide the acreage and separate the Schroeders from the rest of their family 
The consequences of the MPRP on the family range in severity  Margie said her daughter won't feel comfortable walking over to the Schroeders' house if she and her grandchildren will go under a power line 
Nicodemus may not want to farm anymore  Margie said the MPRP would cut the field in half and prevent him from using a chunk of the land 
Margie said the MPRP wouldn't devastate their livelihood the way it could hurt people with farms along the MPRP route, but it would still notably impact her and her husband 
Over the past several decades, many developers have approached the Schroeders to buy the field by the house 
Margie said the couple would never sell the land unless there was a "catastrophic event" where they needed those funds 
She doesn't think they would be able to move, because their property value would tank and nobody would want to buy it 
Margie and Steve built the house sun room as an add-on to the original building specifically so they could see the nature that they've enjoyed for so long 
They are concerned there could be a point in the future when eminent domain rights are used to obtain their land 
"We wish that they would actively pursue other ways to power the data centers and think about the impact on the residents," Margie said 
"    I know other people have had horrible things done for progress and for the public good and eminent domain  I know it been used other places, but it when it affects you, you think, 'Oh my God, how can I live in the United States of America?'"
MPRP- Steve and Margie Schroeder
Margie Schroeder, left, sits next to her sister Victoria Nicodemus at the Schroeders' household on April 30  The Schroeders' 25-acre parcel of land is part of a larger parcel of land that is divided among several of the Schroeders' family members  Victoria lives across the field on the parcel  Since the family members live close together, it is common for them to walk across the field to the Schroeders' home  But, if the MPRP is built, Margie said family members wouldn't feel comfortable walking under the power lines 
MPRP- Steve and Margie Schroeder
Cousins Sawyer Smith, 11, and James Duncan, 14, drive through a field behind Margie and Steve Schroeder home on April 30  Several of the Schroeders' family members live by the field and own parts of the parcel of land  Margie said that if the MPRP is put farther out in the field, it would divide the acreage and separate the Schroeders from the family 
Betsy and Mike McFarland
Betsy and Mike McFarland house in Adamstown overlooks Bennett Creek, a tributary to the Monocacy River, and they can hear the frequent chitter and songs of wildlife outside their windows 
Betsy McFarland can list many different animals she seen since she and her husband moved to Frederick County almost 20 years ago from Rockville 
Those animals include a black bear, bald eagles, reptiles, frogs, a snapping turtle and wild turkeys  She remembers watching the snapping turtle eggs hatch, and the newborns headed down to Bennett Creek 
McFarland said the couple wanted to be somewhere rural, since they both love animals and nature  The house is tucked away at the very end of a road, and in the summer time, "you feel like you're in the middle of the jungle "
"As we settled in, I was like 'This is the house I'll die in ' I'm so happy to be here  I love this place," she said  "Now, of course, it all threatened "
MPRP- Betsy and Mike McFarland
Shown through the reflection of a mirror, Betsy McFarland looks out of a window where a Stop MPRP sign stands outside at the McFarlands' home in Adamstown on April 7  The Maryland Piedmont Reliability Project (MPRP) would cut through the middle of Betsy and Mike McFarland property  If the project is built, Betsy doesn't think she and her husband would want to stay in their home 
The MPRP would cut through the middle of the McFarlands' property  They own nearly three acres of land, and a quarter acre of what they own is on the other side of Bennett Creek 
McFarland said a power line tower would be built west of their property, and power lines would go past the couple back patio and over the creek 
Like other residences in wooded areas along the project route, trees would have to be cut down so the power lines can be built  For the McFarlands, the right-of-way is supposed to go between their house and Bennett Creek 
If the project is built, McFarland doesn't think she and her husband would want to stay in their home 
"It really thrown our plans into a loop  We were planning to stay here through retirement," she said  "Now, we're really wondering what going to happen, and we've put everything on hold "
The McFarlands have ignored attempts from PSEG to contact them, including an offer of $1,000 from the company if the couple agreed to give PSEG permission to survey the property 
MPRP- Betsy and Mike McFarland
A document sent to Betsy and Mike McFarland is shown at the McFarlands' home in Adamstown on April 7  The MPRP would cut through the middle of their property  This document from the Public Service Enterprise Group (PSEG) offers $1,000 for a temporary right-of-entry to the property 
McFarland said she wanted to live somewhere that was safe from development  Since part of this property is designated as a floodplain by the U S  Federal Emergency Management Agency, she thought it was safe 
She worried about how the MPRP will impact the environment and the rich wildlife  It "seems insane" that a company could come in, take down trees and build power lines through an environmentally sensitive area, she said 
Additionally, she said, the county government discussions about how to regulate the data center industry and development are intertwined with the MPRP 
She said she recognizes the need for data centers and creating support for that industry - but there are other solutions available to power those facilities that isn't building a transmission line across Maryland 
"Anybody who says this is just for, 'Oh, we gotta stop rolling blackouts,' whatever, I don't buy it for a second," she said  "    There a lot of different ways this could be done  They're just pushing their way because it best for that company, and that also makes me livid "
McFarland is the chair of Stop MPRP advocacy and outreach committee and shared the potential consequences to her home in the nonprofit short documentary, "Unyielding Ground: The Fight Against the MPRP "
She also submitted a petition to intervene and has attended public meetings in all three counties 
"There only so many mountains you'll die on, but for me, this is one of them, and I'm going to fight this as much as I have to because I care about it," McFarland said  "I care about Frederick County, not just my own house "
MPRP- Betsy and Mike McFarland
Betsy and Mike McFarland stand outside at their home in Adamstown, which overlooks Bennett Creek, on April 7  The MPRP would cut through the middle of the McFarlands' property  Betsy is worried how the MPRP will impact the environment and the wildlife

headline: Preventing Cyberattacks and the Risk of Data Breaches to Critical Infrastructure
text:
MCLEAN, Va , May 11, 2021 /PRNewswire/ -- Onclave Networks, a global cybersecurity leader specializing in securing OT/IoT devices and systems, views the adoption of Zero Trust guidelines as essential for protecting critical infrastructure 
"Attackers are more sophisticated than ever," said Don Stroberg, CEO of Onclave  "Operational technology is too complex and diverse to protect with a passive approach or a continued reliance on IT security solutions  It also means a near-infinite number of attack vectors to exploit  Our platform is purpose-built to secure OT/IoT systems and is based on Zero Trust principles  Our solution greatly reduces the number of potential attack surfaces, and is the ideal choice for mitigating the risk of breaches to OT/IoT networks "
Critical infrastructure and healthcare facilities are particularly vulnerable targets  Power plants, refineries, and hospitals can have tens of thousands of non-IT devices as an example  A complex network of devices means a potentially innumerable number of vulnerable endpoints that can be exploited, increasing the risk and cost when it comes to ensuring the protection and integrity of the network 
Onclave recommends that businesses across industries should adopt a Zero Trust framework: the idea that trust is verified at each endpoint before access is granted to any device, system or user  "This is the core principle of the Onclave TrustedPlatform(TM)  Our unique solution continuously reassesses trust to ensure the integrity of your network," Stroberg said 
According to industry data:
Cyberattacks are up 92% and the average data breach now costs $3 86M to resolve (though some businesses report costs in the tens of millions) 
Since COVID-19, the FBI reports on average 4,000 cyberattacks a day (A 400% increase)  
*Interpol reports "an alarming rate of cyberattacks aimed at major corporations, governments, and critical infrastructure "
*Ransomware attacks are up 800% 
The National Security Agency recently released the Cybersecurity Advisory, "Stop Malicious Cyber Activity Against Connected Operational Technology", for National Security System , Department of Defense (DoD), and Defense Industrial Base operational technology owners and operators  In the memo, the NSA stated, "While there are very real needs for connectivity and automating processes, operational technologies and control systems are inherently at risk when connected to enterprise IT systems  Seriously consider the risk, benefits, and cost before connecting (or continuing to connect) enterprise IT and OT networks "
The National Institute of Standards and Technology recently published its Zero Trust Architecture (SP 800 - 207) for organizations to adopt the Zero Trust principles  It requires strong authentication and continuous monitoring for any anomalous behavior before access is given  This includes visibility as well as controlled communications between remote users, devices, applications, workloads, data centers and public cloud environments 
Onclave aligns with both NIST and NSA recommended guidelines  The Onclave TrustedPlatform(TM) creates cryptographically separate OT networks while still allowing them to share the IT infrastructure  Onclave also supports Zero Trust principles by moving away from "trust, then verify" to "never trust, verify first", providing continuous monitoring and offering the capability to isolate and contain threats  "We are pioneers in developing a proven solution that immediately protects trusted OT/IoT systems and devices - significantly improving your enterprise overall network security and making it most resilient," Stroberg said 
About Onclave Networks, Inc 
Based in the Washington, D C , area, Onclave Networks, Inc  is a global cybersecurity leader that specializes in securing operational technology (OT/IoT) through private networks  Onclave provides the first true, secure communications platform based on the Zero Trust framework  Our solution protects both legacy and new operational technologies from cyberattacks and other unauthorized access  Onclave makes trusted secure communications a standard for all by providing the fastest path to a more secure, simplified, and cost-effective alternative to today solutions  For more information, contact info@onclavenetworks com or visit onclavenetworks com

Alexis Quintal
alexis@newswire com
