headline: What are the world's richest people doing to combat climate change?
text:
The world richest people have become richer and, as a result, their carbon footprint has expanded  Some of the most millionaires are actively involved in the fight against climate change, while others are not so much 
A recent UN report found that the richest 1% of the world population accounts for 15% of emissions  "The rich have the greatest responsibility in this area," the report found, referring to changes in consumer behavior that prioritize low-carbon consumption  "This elite will have to reduce its footprint by a factor of 30 to stay in line with the objectives of the Paris Agreement," the UN study added 
So what are the richest of the rich the planets doing, that is, the 10 richest people in the world, either through their personal or corporate influence to help the world avoid the worst consequences of climate change?
Their companies are more likely to make public promises of sustainability than they have personally expressed  Several have joined the Science Based Targets (SBTi) initiative,a coalition of international organizations such as United Nations Global Compass that independently supports and evaluates business commitments to set net zero carbon targets  Meeting these goals would prevent an increase in global temperature to more than 1 5 degrees Celsius, experts predict 
SBTi has set two deadlines: first, halve greenhouse gas emissions by 2030,followed by reaching net zero carbon emissions by 2050  Companies have two years to present their goals once they commit to SBTi 
The other investments, philanthropic initiatives and personal opinions of these billionaires complete the image of what their commitments to sustainability and a carbon-neutral future look like  Here, a look at the 10 richest people in the world from this perspective  (Net values are April 21, 2021) 
#1 Jeff Bezos
Net worth: $194 4 billion
Source of wealth: Amazon
SBTi commitment? Yes, but their targets haven't been sent yet  Amazon launched its own initiative, The Climate Pledge
The richest person on the planet has not shortage of commitments to address climate change in recent years, be it Amazon Climate Pledge, co-founded with the Global Optimism group, or its own Bezos Earth Fund, a Climate Commitment, which has been signed by 104 other corporations so far and aims to achieve net zero carbon emissions by 2040 
Amazon also established a $2 billion Climate Fund to invest in new technologies needed to build a zero-carbon economy  The company claims to be the world largest buyer of renewable energy and set domestic targets such as deploying 100,000 electric vehicles for delivery by 2030 and supporting carbon reduction technologies in cloud computing, but its sustainability goals have not been presented to other umbrella organizations  An example is the SBTi, which he committed to in May 2020 
Amazon latest sustainability report, dated June 2020, provides an idea of the size of the carbon challenge facing the e-commerce giant: as the company continued to grow in 2019 (revenue grew by 22%), so did its carbon footprint (at 15%)  Amazon chooses a different metric to define your footprint, called "carbon intensity," that measures the equivalent carbon dioxide associated with every dollar of gross merchandise sales  Amazon says its carbon intensity metric in 2019 was reduced by 5% compared to 2018 
Follow business information in our specialized section
#2 Bernard Arnault
Net worth: $179 9 billion
Source of wealth: LVMH
SBTi commitment? Yes, February 2020, no targets have been set 
Arnault is the king of luxury, having built an empire of 75 brands in LVMH, from Louis Vuitton to Givenchy, which encompasses jewelry, fashion, beverages, cosmetics and even a yacht manufacturer, all sectors facing complex sustainability challenges 
LVMH often speaks with a united voice on the sustainability front through its LVMH Initiatives for the Environment (LIFE) programme, which was renamed "LIFE 360" in December when it unveiled goals such as achieving 100% renewable energy use and eliminating plastic or packaging use by 2026  LVMH committed in 2017 to achieving a 25% reduction in its emissions by 2020, a target that says it not only achieved but exceeded, reporting a 37% reduction 
LVMH declined to join the Fashion Pact, led by rival Kering, which commits its signatories to implementing the principles of the United Nations Fashion Charter, achieving 25% supply of low-impact materials by 2025, 50% renewable energy by 2025 and 100% by 2030 in its own operations  Arnault said these goals made no sense for LVMH business and added that we "prefer action to pacts "
Arnault also has investments in the French fashion brand Herm'es and the Carrefour supermarket chain, both signatories to the Kering Fashion Pact  Herm'es also committed to SBTi in December, but has yet to specify its objectives  Carrefour got engaged and set goals with SBTi 
Arnault personal contributions and commitment to sustainability are unknown  A look at Europe richest man attitude towards sustainability was revealed in 2019, when Arnault criticized teen climate activist Greta Thunberg, whom he felt was "giving up entirely to cadastrophism," before adding, "I prefer positive solutions that will allow us to reach a more optimistic position "
3 Elon Musk
Elon Musk, Tesla CEO
Net value: $175 5 billion
Source of wealth: Tesla, SpaceX
SBTi commitment? none
Elon Musk first achieved multimillion-dollar status in 2012, thanks to its stakes in electric car manufacturer Tesla and solar firm SolarCity, two companies offering an alternative to fossil fuels  Musk has also used his influence to advocate for a carbon tax (which the United States does not have) and to denounce climate denialism, leaving his role as an adviser to the White House after President Trump decided to withdraw the United States from the Paris Agreement on Climate Change 
However, carbon elimination is in Musk mind  In February, he pledged $100 million to the nonprofit XPrize for a four-year competition, which will open for registrations on April 22, dedicated to finding solutions to "collectively achieve the carbon elimination target of 10 gigatonnes per year by 2050 "
Part of Musk philanthropy was well publicized  In 2018, Musk defended a $38,900 contribution to Republican politicians by claiming it was "0 5%" of what he donated to the Sierra Club environmental group
4 Bill Gates
Bill Gates, founder of Microsoft
Net worth: $130 4 billion
Source of wealth: Microsoft, investments
SBTi commitment? Yes, Microsoft set targets in September 2019 to obtain 100% renewable electricity by 2030 and reduce the intensity of greenhouse gas emissions per unit of income by 30% by 2030 from 2017 levels  Microsoft is also a signatory to Amazon Climate Commitment 
The company that made Gates a billionaire, Microsoft, has matched its co-founder sustainability efforts, setting one of the most ambitious climate goals in the tech world: becoming negative carbon by 2030, which means removing more carbon from the atmosphere the company creates  If that wasn't enough, by 2050 Microsoft aims to have erased its historical carbon footprint by "eliminating all the carbon that the company has emitted directly by electricity consumption since its founding in1975"  (Gates resigned from Microsoft board of directors in March 2020) 
5 Mark Zuckerberg
Mark Zuckerberg, CEO and Founder of Facebook
Net worth: $110 3 billion
Source of wealth: Facebook
SBTi commitment? Yes, Facebook got engaged in September 2020 but hasn't set targets 
Just for Earth Day, Facebook and its CEO, Zuckerberg, announced that it reached the zero emissions target and the energy it buys and was working toward a net zero emissions target indirectly created by Facebook activities, such as business travel, by 2030  Facebook also said it recognizes Goals Based initiatives ?? in science, to which he committed himself in September, although he has yet to present objectives 
A great focus of Facebook sustainability efforts is on renewable energy  The company boasted of being the largest corporate buyer of renewable energy in 2019, a title to which it was later defeated, first by Google and then by Amazon, the holder of the current record, but says it plans to invest in "carbon elimination projects and solutions based on supporting "nature" 
Facebook recent efforts to suppress the misinformation shared on the platform, a U-turn by Zuckerberg, who had previously rejected the role of fact-checker, will now include the issue of climate change through a new section dedicated to discrediting the myths of climate change 
On a personal level, Zuckerberg said in 2015 that he backed Gates' Breakthrough Energy Coalition, but it is unclear how much he has contributed to it  That same year, he and his wife Priscilla Chan created The Chan Zuckerberg Initiative, a limited liability company that has charitable and defense purposes, to which they have committed 99% of their Facebook shares 
The Chan Zuckerberg Initiative has so far committed just over $2 billion to various projects  None of the foundation focus areas deal specifically with climate change, but sustainability is part of some of the priorities of grant recipients  The California TransForm initiative, which focuses on fostering sustainable communities that do not rely on motor vehicles for transportation, received $725,000 from CZI between 2018 and 2021 
#6 Warren Buffett
Warren Buffet
Net worth: $104 6 billion
Source of wealth: Berkshire Hathaway
SBTi commitment? No
For someone known as "the Omaha Oracle," Buffett 2015 statement in his letter to Berkshire Shareholders Hathaway about the possibility that climate catastrophes could drive insurers' profits has not aged well, and reinsurers face a $100 billion blow by a series alone  catastrophic wildfires, hurricanes and floods 
In the six years since, Buffett attitude towards the risk of climate change and reporting has not changed; opposes a new proposal from shareholders to report on climate change  Berkshire annual presentation of powers states that the board of directors "unanimously favours a vote against the proposal", and Buffett 2015 letter is cited as one of the reasons for the opposition 
#7 Larry Ellison
Larry Ellison, founder of Oracle
Net worth: $101 3 billion
Source of wealth: Oracle
SBTi commitment? No
Ellison software company has a clear position on sustainability: "Oracle recognizes that a sustainable business is a good business," proudly states its sustainability website, as well as showing the company sustainability goals, which have not been presented to SBTi, and its progress so far 
Ellison resigned as CEO in 2014, being president and chief technology officer  Some of his afitions, such as navigation, offer information about his sustainability spirit  Ellison launched the SailGP catamaran race in 2018, which currently has an estimated value of $200 million and adopted the "nature-driven" slogan, to underscore its commitment to being 100% powered by renewable energy sources by 2025  But Ellison most ambitious project is to turn the Hawaiian island of Lanai, which he bought in 2012 for $300 million, into a utopia of clean energy and well-being  In Lanai, he founded the wellness company Sensei, which has so far launched a $3,000-a-night spa called Sensei Retreat and solar-powered hydroponic greenhouses called Sensei Farms  The farms are off-grid, fully powered by solar energy, something Ellison aspires to apply to the island as a whole 
8, 9 Larry Page and Sergey Brin
Google co-founders
Net worth: $98 9 billion and $95 9 billion
Source of wealth: Google
SBTi commitment? No
Founded with the slogan "don't be evil," Google was one of the first major companies to commit to carbon neutrality, and in 2009 announced that 2007 emissions and some of the 2008 emissions had been neutralized by purchasing "high-quality carbon offsets " "Since then, Google has created a new parent company, Alphabet  Since 2017, Alphabet says it has obtained its energy from renewable sources  The last goal the company has set itself is to operate its data centers and campuses with carbon-free energy 24 hours a day, 7 days a week by 2030 
Google co-founders have been frank about their commitments to sustainability even when it caused controversy, as in 2014 when they left the American Legislative Exchange Council free market pressure group for their ties to the denial of climate change  However, Page and Brin received criticism in 2019 for their secret three-day retreat in Italy, which supposedly cost them $29 million, which many of their A-list guests, people who were just going to discuss climate change, arrived on private planes 
Outside of Alphabet, co-founders have similar but separate business interests in innovative technologies  Page has invested in the recently dismantled asteroid mining company Planetary Resources and flying car companies Kitty Hawk and Opener  Brin has reportedly been busy for the past four years by investing millions of dollars in the secret start-up of LTA Research, whose mission is to build a hydrogen and helium-powered aircraft for deployment in disaster zones 
10 Francoise Bettencourt Meyers
Francoise Bettencourt Meyers
Net value: $81 6 billion
Source of wealth: L'Or'eal
SBTi commitment? Yes, in April 2018, L'Or'eal committed to reducing its greenhouse gas emissions by 25% by 2030, from 2016 levels, specifically reducing absolute emissions at its operated sites by 100% by 2025 
The world richest woman is also among the shyest with publicity, perhaps that to be expected after spending years involved in a very public scandal, dubbed the Bettencourt case, driven by her lawsuit against a photographer who received nearly $1 billion in gifts from her mother over the years 
The company, of which it holds a 33% stake, also called the cosmetics colossus: L'Or'eal, has publicly committed itself to reducing emissions  In 2018, it committed to reducing its absolute greenhouse gas emissions by 25% from its 2016 levels  By 2030, it set the same deadline to derive 95% of its ingredients from "renewable plant sources, abundant minerals or circular processes" instead of oil  -based ?? in addition to ensuring that 100% of formulas are "respectful of the aquatic environment"  Bettencourt Meyers presumably gave the green light to this effort as he is a board member of L'Or'eal strategy and sustainable development committee 
Betterncourt Meyers also performsas president of her family foundation, which is not focused on sustainability or climate change, but focuses on "the arts, life sciences and an inclusive society " His family investment art, Tethys, also shares a focus on health and life sciences; so far no investment in sustainability or climate change projects has been revealed 
BY: Sofia Lotto Persio and David Dawkins

headline: What Trump, and C.E.O.s, Actually Got in Riyadh; DealBook Newsletter
text:
Executives including Elon Musk and Nvidia Jensen Huang, were in Saudi Arabia for new investments  That, not tariffs, is what they wanted from Trump 
"A lot of checks"
Business leaders who had high hopes for the second Trump administration hadn't signed up for the turmoil of his trade war  President Trump deals-focused visit to Saudi Arabia is more of what they had in mind 
Leaving aside questions of how many of the multibillion-dollar agreements the administration announced on Tuesday were actually new, the first leg of Trump trip to the Middle East underscores that his transactional instincts are what corporate leaders have been betting on 
Here are highlights of what was announced on Tuesday:
A nearly $142 billion defense agreement involving over a dozen American companies and covering air force and space abilities, border security, communications and more
A deal for Nvidia to sell about 18,000 of its latest chips to the Saudi artificial intelligence start-up Humain to power new data centers, and for AMD to sell about $10 billion worth of processors to Humain as well - with shares of both chipmakers soaring on Tuesday
About $80 billion worth of investments by Google, Oracle, Uber and others in "cutting-edge transformative technologies in both countries"
About $19 billion worth in total of gas turbine by GE Vernova and plane exports by Boeing
A commitment by DataVolt, a Saudi company, to invest $20 billion in artificial intelligence data centers and energy infrastructure in the U S 
Saudi Arabia approval of Starlink use in the region, according to Elon Musk
The deal flow is why top corporate leaders were in Riyadh on Tuesday  Consider who was at a U S -Saudi investment lunch: Musk; Jensen Huang of Nvidia (whom Trump gave a shout-out to, while noting that Tim Cook of Apple wasn't present); Sam Altman of OpenAI, who is raising billions to build data centers as well as selling A I  services; Larry Fink of BlackRock, who has assiduously courted Saudi wealth; Andy Jassy of Amazon; Dara Khosrowshahi of Uber; Alex Karp of the data consulting firm Palantir; Kelly Ortberg of Boeing; and more 
It also an environment - d'ecor-wise, sure, but also focused on deals - that Trump appeared to enjoy  "We have the biggest business leaders in the world here," he said  "They're going to walk away with a lot of checks "
Never mind that the announced deals are falling far short of Trump goal  The White House said it had secured $600 billion worth of investment commitments from Saudi Arabia  But the announced total so far is about $283 billion, and organizers of the investment forum said that 145 deals were signed for a total of a bit over $300 billion  And several of the deals have been in the works before Trump second term 
The president has pressed Saudi officials to commit to more than $1 trillion (and bring down the price of oil, to boot)  But that figure exceeds the assets held by the Saudi sovereign wealth fund, the Public Investment Fund - which has already said it wants to invest less abroad and instead focus on the domestic economy 
All of this is a reminder that deals are the way to Trump heart  Promises of big investments were how Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, Saudi Arabia de facto ruler, forged ties with the president during his first term 
And it a lesson that Qatar and the United Arab Emirates have also learned, almost certainly meaning that billions more in commitments will be announced when Trump heads there next 
Speaking of Qatar, even Republicans are wary of the country potential gift of a Boeing 747 for use as an Air Force One: Senator Ted Cruz of Texas told CNBC that "the plane poses significant espionage and surveillance problems "
HERE WHAT HAPPENING 
A trade court hears a lawsuit over President Trump tariffs  A three-judge panel for the U S  Court of International Trade in Manhattan listened to arguments from a group of small businesses arguing for blocking the levies and to a defense from administration lawyers  Separately, The Washington Post reports that electoral concerns helped persuade the White House to ease up on its Chinese tariffs 
Tesla board is said to weigh a new pay package for Elon Musk  A committee of two directors - Robyn Denholm, the carmaker chair, and Kathleen Wilson-Thompson, an independent board member - has been formed to consider ways to compensate Musk, especially if Delaware Supreme Court upholds the overturning of a multibillion-dollar payday, The Financial Times reports  Separately, Denholm sold $198 million worth of Tesla stock over the past six months via a prearranged trading plan 
Musk DOGE no longer claims credit for cutting more federal contracts  The Department of Government Efficiency withdrew claims from its website that it had canceled dozens of contracts, after The Times reported that they had already been reinstated  The move underscores bigger questions about what the Musk-formed group has actually achieved, and whether it is losing influence on Capitol Hill 
Microsoft again swings the ax on its work force  The tech giant said that it planned to lay off about 3 percent of its employees, or roughly 6,000 people, as it aims to reduce layers of management  The move - coming despite better-than-expected quarterly earnings - is the latest reminder that tech companies still see benefits from cutting expenses, with Amazon and CrowdStrike among those having announced layoffs this year 
Back to square one 
Calm has returned to the bond market, and S&P 500 futures are pointing upward on Wednesday as investors grow bullish that President Trump trade war will be replaced by trade deals 
The S&P 500 has pulled back from the brink of a bear market to positive territory for the year 
The speed of the market bounce is reminiscent of the Covid-era V-shape market recovery in April 2020, according to Deutsche Bank data  That surge bolstered investor portfolios and led to a deals boom, but there were plenty of hiccups along the way 
This one could be choppy, too  Investors on Tuesday cheered the latest Consumer Price Index report, which showed the annualized headline inflation rate inching up 2 3 percent, its lowest level in more than four years  But economists expect to see the effects of tariffs on price increases in future reports  A string of companies have warned that the levies have upended their supply chains, forcing them to consider passing those costs on to consumers 
Here how things might play out:
The bull case: There have now been three consecutive tame C P I  reports; even egg prices are falling  If it weren't for tariff uncertainty, the narrative hanging over markets today might be about the Fed having largely stamped out the inflation menace, analysts say 
Could a string of trade deals keep the inflation genie in the bottle? Would that be enough to persuade the Fed to resume cutting interest rates, which could spur borrowing and motivate investors to buy stocks?
On social media Tuesday, Trump again beseeched the central bank and its chair, Jay Powell, to act: "THE FED must lower the RATE, like Europe and China have done  What is wrong with Too Late Powell? Not fair to America, which is ready to blossom? Just let it all happen, it will be a beautiful thing!"
(That said, futures traders on Wednesday actually lowered their odds slightly for just two rate cuts this year )
The bear case: Tuesday C P I  report, was a mirage  "Higher tariffs threaten to derail this trend, and our expectation is that prices for goods including vehicles and apparel will rise in the coming months," Sarah House, a senior economist at Wells Fargo, wrote in a research note on Tuesday  Other economists flagged the rising prices for household furnishings, toys and audio equipment as a sign that tariffs may still squeeze consumers 
The question is whether tariffs result in entrenched inflation, which could sap consumer buying power and erode economic growth  That could put more pressure on the Fed to intervene - but that could take months  "It will be a quiet summer for the Fed," Becky Qin, a multi-asset portfolio manager for Fidelity International, told Bloomberg TV this morning 
We'd like to know how the tariffs are affecting your business  Have you changed suppliers? Negotiated lower prices? Paused investments or hiring? Made plans to move manufacturing to the U S ? Or have the tariffs helped your business? Please let us know what you're doing 
"He needs to leave " 
- Eric Holder, the former U S  attorney general who led an inquiry into the World Economic Forum founder Klaus Schwab over whistle-blower accusations of financial impropriety and discrimination, to trustees of the organization, according to The Wall Street Journal 
How donors missed Biden warning signs 
The fallout from the presidential election has set off soul-searching within the Democratic Party and its backers - especially before next year midterms  A new book is poised to force them to reflect on what went wrong 
"Original Sin: President Biden Decline, Its Cover-Up, and His Disastrous Choice to Run Again," by Jake Tapper of CNN and Alex Thompson of Axios, dives into whether President Joe Biden decision to remain atop the Democratic ticket for so long doomed the party chances in November 
A main focus of the book is how Democratic power players and major donors missed the signs that Biden mental acuity was apparently slipping even as they poured huge sums into his re-election campaign  Were these just bad days, or the sign of entrenched decline? Were aides and party leaders in denial, the book suggests, or were they willfully deceiving others about Biden mental state and capabilities?
Tapper and Thompson detail what went down backstage at a major Los Angeles fund-raiser, organized by Jeffrey Katzenberg, the Hollywood mogul who was a chair of Biden campaign, with assistance from the actor George Clooney, two weeks before the fateful debate that spelled the end of Biden run  From an excerpt in The New Yorker:
It seemed clear that the President had not recognized Clooney 
"It was not O K ," recalled the Hollywood V I P  who had witnessed this moment      "It was uncomfortable "
"George Clooney," the aide clarified for the President 
"Oh, yeah!" Biden said  "Hi, George!"
Clooney was shaken to his core  The President hadn't recognized him, a man he had known for years  Clooney had expressed concern about Biden health before - a White House aide had told him a few months before that they were working on getting the President to take longer steps when he walked - but obviously the problem went far beyond his gait  This was much graver 
This was the President of the United States?
Clooney was certainly not the only one concerned  Other high-dollar attendees who posed for photographs with Obama and Biden described Biden as slow and almost catatonic  Though they saw pockets of clarity while watching him on television, and onstage later that night, there were obvious brain freezes and clear signs of a mental slide  It was, to some of them, terrifying 
THE SPEED READ 
Deals
The estate of Paul Allen has put the N B A  Portland Trail Blazers up for sale; the team has been valued at about $3 6 billion  (Bloomberg)
"OpenAI shake-up plan gets SoftBank nod - all eyes now on Microsoft" (CNBC)
GSK agreed to buy a liver-disease treatment from Boston Pharmaceuticals for up to $2 billion  (WSJ)
Politics, policy and regulation
A bid by Robert F  Kennedy Jr , the health secretary, to label pesticides a potential health risk has drawn opposition from other Trump administration officials who fear it could roil the food supply chain  (WSJ)
House Republicans are pushing a bill that would repeal state laws regulating artificial intelligence, and bar states from passing new ones for a decade  (WaPo)
"Real estate titans 'hold their nose' and get behind Cuomo with big money" (Politico)
Best of the rest
CNN will debut a streaming service this fall, just three years after the CNN+ debacle, and ESPN new streaming app will cost $29 99 per month  (NYT, CNBC)
Airbnb is rebranding to be for not just rentals, but "anything " (WSJ)
Pete Rose is among a group of deceased players whose eligibility bans have been lifted by Major League Baseball, giving them a shot at the Hall of Fame  (The Athletic)
We'd like your feedback! Please email thoughts and suggestions to dealbook@nytimes com 
PHOTO: Business leaders, including Tesla Elon Musk, BlackRock Larry Fink and Blackstone Steve Schwarzman, were in Saudi Arabia yesterday, during President Trump first major overseas stop  (PHOTOGRAPH BY Doug Mills/The New York Times FOR THE NEW YORK TIMES)

headline: What’s Really Behind Corporate Promises on Climate Change?
text:
For the past several years, BlackRock, the giant investment firm, has cast itself as a champion of the transition to clean energy 
Last month, Laurence D  Fink, BlackRock chief executive, wrote that the coronavirus pandemic had "driven us to confront the global threat of climate change more forcefully," and the company said it wants businesses it invests in to remove as much carbon dioxide from the environment as they emit by 2050 at the latest 
But crucial details were missing from that widely read pledge, including what proportion of the companies BlackRock invests in will be zero-emission businesses in 2050  Setting such a goal and earlier targets would demonstrate the seriousness of the company commitment and could force all sorts of industries to step up their efforts  On Saturday, in response to questions from The New York Times, a BlackRock spokesman said for the first time that the company "ambition" was to have "net zero emissions across our entire assets under management by 2050 "
As the biggest companies strive to trumpet their environmental activism, the need to match words with deeds is becoming increasingly important 
Household names like Costco and Netflix have not provided emissions reduction targets despite saying they want to reduce their impact on climate change  Others, like the agricultural giant Cargill and the clothing company Levi Strauss, have made commitments but have struggled to cut emissions  Technology companies like Google and Microsoft, which run power-hungry data centers, have slashed emissions, but even they are finding that the technology often doesn't yet exist to carry out their "moonshot" objectives 
"You can look at a company website and see their sustainability report and it will look great," said Alberto Carrillo Pineda, a founder of Science Based Targets initiative, a global effort to assess corporate plans to reduce emissions  "But then when you look at what is behind it, you'll see there is not a lot of substance behind those commitments or the commitments are not comprehensive enough "
President Biden is also placing a big emphasis on climate change and has rejoined the Paris agreement  But determining how hard companies are really trying can be very difficult when there are no regulatory standards that require uniform disclosures of important information like emissions 
Institutional Shareholder Services, a firm that advises investors on how to vote on board elections and other corporate matters, uses company data and its own analysis to assess what corporations are doing to reduce emissions  Just over a third of the 500 companies in the S&P 500 stock index have set ambitious targets, it found, while 215 had no target at all  The rest had weak targets 
"To realize the necessary emission reductions, more ambitious targets urgently need to be set," said Viola Lutz, deputy head of ISS ESG Climate Solutions, an arm of Institutional Shareholder Services  "Otherwise, we project emissions for S&P 500 companies will end up being triple of what they should be in 2050 "
There has been some progress by companies that have rigorous targets  In a report last month, Science Based Targets, which was started by the environmental groups and hundreds of businesses brought together by the United Nations, said the 338 large companies around the world for which it had sufficient emissions data collectively reduced their emissions by 25 percent between 2015 and 2019 
Often large companies in the same industry have very different records 
For example, Walmart discloses its targets for emissions reductions and the progress it has made to the CDP, including a goal for emissions from its suppliers, and its plan has been vetted by Science Based Targets  But Costco doesn't expect to have commitments to reduce emissionsuntil the end of next year  Costco executives declined to comment 
Netflix is often compared to technology giants like Google and Microsoft  But Netflix has yet to set a target for reducing the emissions caused by its offices, production activities and the computer servers it uses  "Climate action is important, and we'll announce our plans in the spring, which will include targets based on climate science," the company said in a statement 
Slashing emissions is difficult  Businesses must reliably measure how much carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases they are responsible for  Then companies have to find cleaner energy sources without hurting their operations  Where they can't find cleaner substitutes, businesses often pay others to reduce emissions or remove carbon from the atmosphere 
The task gets even harder when companies begin the process of reducing so-called Scope 3 emissions - pollution caused by suppliers and customers  At oil companies, for example, Scope 3 would include emissions from cars that use gasoline 
BlackRock, with $8 7 trillion of assets under management, including stakes in many companies, clearly faces a daunting task  The company doesn't directly own most of the shares or bonds it buys - it manages them for pension funds, other corporations and individual investors - limiting how much climate activism it can pursue  In addition, most of its investment products track indexes like the S&P 500, so it inevitably ends up managing stocks of fossil fuel companies 
Many Wall Street firms have made pledges to get to net zero emissions from their lending and other financial activities but have not made clear whether that goal applies to the stocks and bonds they manage for customers  BlackRock decision to include all the assets it manages could pressure other financial giants to make similar commitments, but it could rankle fossil fuel industries and their political supporters in Congress 
Later this year, BlackRock is going to announce an interim target for how many of its investments will have achieved, or be on their way to, zero emissions in 2030 
Still, BlackRock is careful about the language it uses when describing what it will do to push businesses in its portfolios to reduce emissions - for which it has been criticized by people who want the firm to take a more activist stand  In a recent letter, the company said it was intent on "increasing the role of votes on shareholder proposals in our stewardship efforts around sustainability "
"This could mean a lot of things and - as always - the proof is in the pudding," Ms  Lutz of ISS said 
Ed Sweeney, a company spokesman, said BlackRock had recently voted for a significantly higher number of shareholder proposals aimed at making companies greener compared with previous years  And in a client memo sent Wednesday, the firm said it might vote against directors and management at companies that it determines do not have clear climate disclosures and credible environmental plans 
"While we recognize that the transition to net zero is at different stages based on industry and region, through a combination of engagement with management teams and boards of directors and holding companies accountable for insufficient progress through our voting on director elections and shareholder proposals, BlackRock will continue to focus on this important issue," Mr  Sweeney said in an email 
Other companies that have pledged to cut emissions face different challenges, including coordinating with suppliers and partners 
Consider the apparel industry  Much of its contribution to climate change comes from its supply chain  The clothes that Levi Strauss and others put their labels on are often made in factories in places like China, Pakistan and India that remain reliant on coal-fired power plants  The clothes are transported on ships and planes that burn diesel and jet fuel 
Even so, when Levi Strauss rolled out its 2025 climate action strategy three years ago, its chief executive, Chip Bergh, said, "We believe it time for businesses to start playing a larger role in fighting the world most pressing problems, like climate change "
The company set a Scope 3 emissions target  But Science Based Targets said in January that emissions from Levi supply chain were not falling and had grown by 13 percent between 2016 and 2019 
Jeffrey Hogue, Levi Strauss chief sustainability officer, said that calculation was incomplete because the company could not yet take credit for many interventions and investments it has made with suppliers  He said that would happen once the apparel industry decides on a method for calculating the advances suppliers have made in cutting emissions, particularly for factories that sell to multiple companies 
"We believe we are way better than 13 percent," Mr  Hogue said  But he said he could not confidently give his own estimate, adding, "We are working with the industry to create more accuracy and precision in the number "
Gary Cook, the global climate campaigns director of Stand earth, an environmental group, was skeptical  "They set an aggressive target and they are struggling with that," he said 
Cargill, one of the largest privately owned American companies and a major middleman that works with farmers and food companies around the world, has attempted to become a strong voice on climate change but has struggled to meet its goals 
The company is a big purchaser of Brazilian soybeans, which are often grown on land that was previously forested  In 2010, Cargill promised to meet a "net zero" deforestation goal by 2020, but the company did not succeed and has extended its target to 2030  "Our commitment on deforestation has not wavered," said Jill Kolling, Cargill vice president for global sustainability 
The company plans show how emissions could go up over all even when a business has set a goal to cut them  Cargill wants to reduce its emissions in its global supply chains by 30 percent per ton of production by 2030, a target it made no progress on at the time of measurement in 2019, according to Science Based Targets  But overall emissions in its supply chains may not fall by that amount because of increases in production  "It depends on how our business grows, and that hard to predict," Ms  Kolling said 
By contrast, deep-pocketed tech firms have probably made the most progress  Now they are setting even more ambitious targets 
Google wants all its operations to be consistently powered by energy sources that do not release greenhouse gases by 2030, but that could be difficult to achieve because the output of wind and solar farms is still small in some countries  Microsoft wants to be "carbon negative" by 2030 even including Scope 3 emissions  That goal will almost certainly require extracting carbon dioxide from the atmosphere  Those technologies are nascent and could be very expensive 
And for all these ambitious targets, even some executives argue that the current voluntary approach won't ensure the required reduction in emissions 
"If we are going to achieve a net-zero carbon economy for real, we will need everyone to act," said Lucas Joppa, Microsoft chief environmental officer  "And that means action can't be voluntary  We need requirements and standards that everyone is expected to meet "
PHOTO: Walmart is transparent about its climate goals and progress; Costco is not  (PHOTOGRAPH BY GABBY JONES FOR THE NEW YORK TIMES) (B5)

headline: Which companies are looking for students and graduates for their first job opportunity
text:
In the context of a depressed labour market, calls for internship programs and young professionals are good news for students and recent graduates who are trying to get their first job 
After an irregular year, in which companies postponed or reduced calls to adapt to the pandemic situation, in 2021 most of the proposals were adapted to the home office modality 
The sector where the most job offers appear is technology  This month, for example, Mercado Libre and Globant made announcements of massive additions in Argentina 
But in the case of internships and young professionals, the programs are also maintained in large companies with varying fields,such as finance or mass consumption 
Open calls
Azul Nuss B'ertora is 24 years old, has a degree in UBA Management and entered SAP in 2019 as an intern  Today he is an analyst at SAP South Region Talent Attraction and who explains what that company internship program is all about 
"We have permanent open vacancies  Right now, there are 12 active searches,"he says  The company has an internship agreement with 14 universities, public and private, including the UBA, UTN, UADE and UCA 
After going through a selection process, students join the company under the conditions set by the Internship Law  They work 20 hours a week to continue their studies  The first contract is for 6 months, and can be extended up to 18 months 
"Trainees are provided with training, career development possibilities and training  Within the company they have the specific tasks of the area to which they belong and the possibility to participate in projects of their interest", describes Nuss B'ertora 
Last year, as a pandemic, "contractions were reduced overall," he says  However, "we hired 67 passers-by over the years, and made 68% effective "
Positions that are now open are "mostly for the Finance area today and some also for Support," the analyst reports 
Internships are being performed remotely, and those entering the company receive all the tools needed to work from the hogar 
To apply, you must log in to the SAP page or do so via LinkedIn 
In Lumen there is also a permanent call for staff  "Four years ago, we started our first internship plan by incorporating 12 interns as a pilot," says Mart'in Tommasi, director of HR 
Of those who started in 2020, 46% were already effective and the rest continue as an intern  "Each rotation is covered with a new intern we always keep the quota of 12," he explains 
Students are incorporated into the "core" areas of the business: data center, security, IT areas, infrastructure  That why the search is geared towards technology and engineering profiles 
Passers-in enter with a stimulus allocation of 27,000 pesos and the same corporate benefits as the rest of the employees 
The contract is for 20 hours a week, "but we give you the possibility to choose how to fulfill them  We are flexible because we know that the priority is the faculty, and this is an environment where they will develop," says Tommasi 
Nominations are made through the nine universities with which Lumen has internship agreements 
Nestl'e will open the call for its internship program in May  "This program includes an accompaniment in onboarding, training and integration activities, among other things", says Mar'ia Fernanda Amado, director of RR HH  for Nestl'e Regi'on Plata 
This time they will incorporate 25 students into the areas of Supply Chain, Environment, Engineering, Regulatory Affairs, Production, Maintenance, Quality, Information Technology, Trade Marketing, Marketing, Legal, Communications and HR 
"60% of those who entered in 2019 were effective and those who did so in 2020 are still within the internship period," says Amado  Nominations will be available on the Talent Cia platform 
At Unilever, the internship program has been in place for 25 years, and was not interrupted by the pandemic, although it did delay its start in 2020 and that litter of interns, from 17 different universities, will conclude in May  30% have already been effective 
"When a position arises, you look at the development of the intern of the area  If you have potential and want to continue, you are effectively incorporated," says Agustina Iino Cotado, Manager of Employer Brand and Learning for South Latin America 
On the profiles sought, Iino Cotado says that "the focus is on soft skills, especially in the desire to learn  Only in very specific positions, such as in laboratory R&D, does it require more technical training "
As for this year call, it closed this week and will incorporate 55 young people in the last two years of their career and up to 28 years old, with residence in AMBA or Gualeguaych'u, where a vacancy is available 
The Young High Flight Takeoff program has some different characteristics, as it is not part of the Internship Act 
The search is aimed at high school graduates withsome knowledge in programming, "who may have acquired it in a self-taught way, in the technical school or in the early years of a career", specifies Carlos Alvarez, CTO of the company 
The program is already 10 years old, but by 2020 it was not done for the pandemic  This year the mode will be totally virtual 
The company will select 25 young people and hire them for three months during which they will be trained by experts from the company itself  This edition has a focus on the backend 
During the training "we strengthen their programming base with a more formal and more reliability-oriented vision and expose them to problems that we have already solved in Takeoff", describes Alvarez 
 "Kids come out with notions of programming more structured than they had when they came in and with an understanding of the programming environment and technology that allows them to solve problems that don't exist in theory but do in practice  Most stay in the company," he says 
The call opened on April 19 and closes on May 2  Applications are made through LinkedIn and in the "Join" link of the company 
In May BGH Tech Partner will open the call for its Training and Certification Program in Tierra del Fuego and San Juan, a training on Cloud computing topics aimed at university students 
The first edition of this program was held in Tierra del Fuego from January 2021 to April 18 last  12 candidates were selected for training  "Of the 12, 8 have certified and are part of the company payroll,"says Marcelo Girotti, CEO of the BGH Group 
The marketplace company Avenida+ convenes the first edition of its program "a+ Next"  The proposal has "the logic of bootcamp, where you learn by making and transmitting the culture of the company", describes Santiago Lorenzo, chief of staff 
The program will last 6 months during which participants will receive "a market salary" and have a technical mentor and coordinator 
"They will be assigned real tasks according to their preferences (payment, database, front-end, etc ) and challenged to solve them with the necessary accompaniment and structure," Lorenzo says 
After the program,"we hope you can at least enter 50%,"Lorenzo says  For now, they covered 3 out of 5 vacancies and those interested in applying can write to: hr@avenida com  
At another technology company, Planexware,they started with 3 passers-by last year  "Two of them finish renewing their internship mid-year  The idea is for them to move to the permanent plant as programmers," says Alex Doring, HR manager 
Two other passers-by entered in April and a third-party search is still open  What they are looking for are system and engineering profiles,and they have agreements with UTN, UBA and UNSAM 
Facebook offers 40 vacancies for Latin American students in the United States and the United Kingdom, as part of its international internship program  The positions are for software engineers and front-end engineers 
The requirements are: to have a year or more of experience with Perl, Java, Php, Python or C++, to be students in the process of obtaining a bachelor degreeor master degree in computer science or related science and be able to communicate in English 
The call will be open until the vacancies are completed  "Every year it happens between June and July" report from the company 
The links to apply are, for the United States: www facebook com/careers/v2/jobs/654496918442526/ and for the United Kingdom: www facebook com/careers/v2/jobs/654496918442526/
Young professionals
These programs? are aimed at young recent graduates who are hired on an effective condition and for whom a specific development plan is organized 
Banco Galicia,for example, makes annual calls for its young professionals program 
Last year "we did the design of the program, we launched it and we came across the pandemic context, so it was stand by for a while, we redesigned it to make it virtual and incorporated 24 people", says Victoria Romero, advisor in the People area 
This year will also be virtual and there will be 30 vacancies open from May  The requirements to apply are: be a newly graduated professional or owe up to 3 finals and be up to 27 years old 
"We are looking for talent that is oriented to challenges, in a collaborative spirit, analytical gaze, oriented to work with data, with business vision and transformative capacity," romero lists 
The careers they prioritize are economics, industrial engineering, systems, communication, marketing, marketing and design  Nominations will be available on LinkedIn, galicia Instagram and the 
Another company that has such a program is the petrochemical Unipar,but for recently graduated engineers  In its last edition, in September 2020, 3 were entered  This year they are looking at whether sanitary conditions allow for a new edition 
However, they did start their internship program, which added up to 9 students in April in the areas of Human Resources, Maintenance Planning, IT, Technical Assistance, Taxes, Services and Facilities, and different areas of Maintenance 
Look too

headline: WHO NEEDS TO KNOW WHEN A HURRICANE'S COMING?; EVEN TED CRUZ IS WORRIED ABOUT NOAA
text:
Sunday is the first day of hurricane season, and we trust you'll note the occasion accordingly  I usually drive to the Dairy Queen, then cry softly in the car over an Oreo blizzard, but wait - being told that more a matter for Mental Health Month 
Anyway, hurricane season is now officially longer than football season and potentially a lot more destructive according to the people at NOAA who survived DOGE season, in which Elon Musk blew through the government infrastructure like a Category 5 
Remaining job holders at the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, the weather forecasting agency and environment monitoring unit established by radical left lunatic Richard Nixon 55 years ago, have managed to maintain a web site that predicts we'll see 6 to 10 hurricanes between today and the end of November, 3 to 5 of them "major "
To be clear, there is often very little material advantage to being in the path of a minor hurricane 
Hurricane season chaos
But as the hurricane season begins, some preseason personnel moves by the biospheric geniuses at the top of the second Trump administration have made things a world more difficult, so it little short of a miracle that NOAA can still talk a good game 
"NOAA and the National Weather Service are using the most advanced weather models and cutting-edge hurricane tracking systems to provide Americans with real-time storm forecasts and warnings," said Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on the government web site  "With these models and forecasting tools, we have never been more prepared for hurricane season "
Most external analysis puts that assertion as not in the same ocean as the truth 
Hundreds of people were fired at NOAA in February, and Trump has moved to slash FEMA grants by more than $600 million on top of DOGE damage that potentially left states ill-equipped to deal with 6 to 10 major hurricanes  When FEMA director Cameron Hamilton told a Congressional committee three weeks ago that he didn't think it was in the best interests of the American people to eliminate the agency, he was promptly fired 
Karoline Leavitt, the White House press secretary, confirmed the simplest explanation available as to Hamilton failing, which was merely stating "something that was contrary to what the president believes "
The problem for the administration is that even incorrigible Trump sycophants like Texas Senator Ted Cruz fear that slowdowns at NOAA, where Lutnick now insists on reviewing any government contract in excess of $100,000, could bring plenty of hurricane season chaos 
Project 2025 against NOAA
"NOAA alone has 5,700 contracts set to expire this year," Cruz said at a Senate hearing in May  "These include everything from post-hurricane flood assessment to janitorial services " One backlogged contract apparently forced the temporary shutdown of a data center at Texas A&M, which Cruz said "depriv(ed) Texas emergency and water managers of critical drought forecasts that help them manage reservoirs and track storm surge data and hurricane forecasts in real time "
If things get much worse, he'll barely know when to head for Cancun 
Ted concerns are ominously well-placed, with Texas currently ranked second only to Florida in the record book under Most Times Walloped By Hurricanes, a book the government started writing in 1851  North Carolina is third, followed by Louisiana, South Carolina, Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi  In those eight states, 51 to 65% of the voters went for Trump in 2024 
They can claim to have been duped (though probably won't), yet detailed plans to cut government agencies were all available in Project 2025 long before Election Day  The project authors describe NOAA as part of "the climate change alarm industry" that should be "broken up and downsized " FEMA was more centered in its crosshairs, where disasters are seen as a state problem that states best know how to handle, even if the main thing states know is that they need federal assistance 
When Hurricane Helene ruined parts of western North Carolina last October, Trump leaped into action, sending his MAGA surrogates onto Fox News to savage the FEMA response, claiming the agency was ignoring Republican areas and channeling disaster funds to undocumented immigrants  The truth was that FEMA, under Joe Biden, committed to covering 100% of debris removal costs for 180 days, and when North Carolina Governor Josh Stein asked the Trump administration to extend the same commitment last week, it was denied 
Funny how that works 
An inconvenient statement
As hurricane season advances, forecasters that still have jobs are particularly concerned with a notorious storm trigger called the West African Monsoon  If they detect heightened atmospheric activity related to that, it can mean an increased number of major storms tracking across the Atlantic, or is it the Ocean of America now?
Unfortunately, there another inconvenient statement that might be classified as contrary to what the president believes, and it about hurricanes 
You can't nuke 'em 
Gene Collier is a columnist for the Post-Gazette: gcollier@post-gazette com and on Bluesky: @genecollier bsky social 
Graphic
