PHOTO: Mike Stewart/Associated Press: The aftermath of Hurricane Helene in Asheville, N C

headline: Why a Plane-Size Machine Could Foil a Race to Build Gas Power Plants
text:
To hear Trump administration officials and many energy executives tell it, the United States is on the precipice of a new golden age for natural gas that will be driven in large part by the voracious power needs of data centers 
But turning natural gas into electricity requires giant metal turbines that are increasingly difficult to secure  Companies that haven't already reserved this equipment, which can weigh as much as a large airplane and cost hundreds of millions of dollars, are facing waits of three or four years, about twice as long as just a year earlier 
The cost of building gas power plants has also soared - so much so that in some parts of the country, solar panels and batteries are likely to be cheaper, energy executives and consultants said  By some estimates, it now costs two or three times as much to build a gas-fired power plant as it did a few years ago 
The challenge of securing enough gas turbines is one of the clearest examples of how booming investment in artificial intelligence is reshaping the electric power industry, overwhelming suppliers and upending longstanding notions of what makes sense financially 
It also a reminder of the gap that often exists between the plans and goals of politicians and executives and the reality on the ground 
U S  gas demand is clearly rising, all the more so because of the data centers needed to train and use chatbots and other forms of A I  But there are limits to how much more gas the country can use - limits that elected officials and energy tycoons cannot easily wish away 
GE Vernova, the biggest manufacturer of large gas turbines in the world, is among those betting that the recent flurry of interest in gas power will last  The company, formed last year in the breakup of General Electric, is spending more than $160 million to overhaul its gas turbine plant on the edge of Greenville, S C 
By the end of next year, the 1 5-million-square-foot factory is expected to churn out about 35 percent more gas turbines  The building is a whirring, beeping expanse of partly automated assembly lines interspersed with metal turbine components 
"More electrons are going to be created from gas," Scott Strazik, chief executive of GE Vernova, said in a recent interview  "Appetite is very real "
About this time last year, interest in natural gas to power data centers picked up, catching much of the energy industry off guard 
Tech giants like Microsoft and Google pledged years ago to lower their emissions  But as it has become clearer how much and how quickly their energy needs will grow, companies have turned to gas  When burned, natural gas produces carbon dioxide, the leading cause of climate change  But gas plants can be built faster than nuclear power plants and operate all day, unlike wind and solar energy 
As sales of turbines climbed, so did wait times and prices  It takes about four months for GE Vernova to assemble the turbines used in power plants  But that clock starts only after the company has received all the components, like the dense metal fins that catch hot air inside the turbine, causing a rotor to spin 
These days, the backlog is so severe as to be reminiscent of the snarled supply chains of the pandemic, which constrained production of cars, medical devices and much more 
Between those delays and the time it takes to build a power plant, a company starting from scratch today would probably not have a new gas plant running before 2030  Other critical electrical equipment like transformers is also harder to get 
By comparison, a large solar project that includes batteries to store energy for use in the evening could reasonably be completed in three years, said Jesse Noffsinger, a partner at the consulting firm McKinsey & Company 
Chris Wright, the energy secretary, said the Trump administration was encouraging power equipment suppliers to increase U S  manufacturing capacity  In an interview, he also floated the possibility of invoking the Defense Production Act, which authorizes the president to extend loans and take other steps to encourage companies to produce critical equipment  President Trump used the law, enacted in 1950, to boost production of things like ventilators during the pandemic 
Mr  Wright, who previously led an oil and gas company, said he expected natural gas to soon meet about half the country electricity needs, up from 43 percent last year  As for wind and solar, "they're going to continue to play some role," Mr  Wright said  "But are they going to be backbones of an electricity grid? Never "
Mr  Wright is much more optimistic than other energy experts were about gas  Consulting firms like McKinsey and Rystad Energy expect gas power share of the U S  electricity market to remain relatively steady as renewables grow more quickly 
It is hard to compare the cost of gas power with that of solar panels or wind turbines and batteries  That is because it is not always sunny or windy, meaning other power sources are sometimes needed to complement renewables  Gas costs also add up over time and can spike during crises, as they did after Russia 2022 invasion of Ukraine  By contrast, solar and wind farms cost relatively little to operate once they are built 
Generally speaking, building a gas power plant can now be about as expensive as installing solar panels paired with batteries, according to Rystad, when including tax credits that apply to renewable energy and storage  One big factor is that gas turbines now cost about 50 percent more than they did just 10 months ago, according to the investment bank Jefferies 
"We're in this weird no man land where it very profitable to run a plant and it clear we're going to need more electricity," Mr  Noffsinger of McKinsey said of gas plants  But in some markets, he added, it is unclear whether building new ones will make financial sense 
Lawmakers in Texas, which gets about 30 percent of its electricity from renewable energy, have sought to ensure gas plants get built anyway  The state Senate recently passed a bill aimed at ensuring that half of any new generation capacity comes from sources other than wind, solar and batteries  The House has not yet taken up the bill 
"My biggest concern is: How big? How long?" Bill Newsom, chief executive of another gas turbine manufacturer, Mitsubishi Power Americas, said of the current flurry of interest in gas  "I lose sleep over it every night "
This year, 93 percent of the electricity capacity added to U S  grids will be renewable energy and battery storage, according to the Energy Information Administration  Gas will account for just 7 percent 
S&P Global Commodity Insights recently estimated that by 2040, the United States would need to add at least nine times as much renewable energy and batteries as gas generation capacity to meet new electricity demand  That is partly because many customers prefer renewable energy, and various bottlenecks are slowing the construction of gas-fired power plants 
But forecasts vary widely, even over just the next few years  Complicating matters is that utilities have often overstated power needs  From 2012 to 2023, utility planners overestimated electricity demand by 23 percent, on average, in their 10-year forecasts, according to RMI, a nonprofit research organization that aims to reduce emissions 
Joseph Dominguez, who runs the country largest nuclear power plant operator, is among those who question how big the gas power boom will ultimately be  His company, Constellation Energy, struck a $16 4 billion deal in January to buy Calpine, which owns many gas power plants 
"But that a very different thing than saying that I would invest to replicate that fleet today," Mr  Dominguez said  He pulled up a chart on his tablet showing how much scientists expect global temperatures to rise in the coming decades  Last year was the hottest on record 
"This world portends to be quite ugly for its inhabitants and will drive political outcomes that are radically different than those which we're discussing today," Mr  Dominguez said 
PHOTOS: Gas turbines can weigh as much as a large airplane and cost hundreds of millions of dollars  Above right, the blades of a gargantuan metal turbine, which is used to turn natural gas into electricity  (B1); A 7HA gas turbine being assembled at the GE Vernova factory in Greenville, S C , in March  Companies that haven't already reserved this equipment are facing waits of three or four years, about twice as long as a year earlier  At right, a testing stand that was under construction at the factory  (PHOTOGRAPHS BY WILL CROOKS FOR THE NEW YORK TIMES) (B3) This article appeared in print on page B1, B3

headline: Why Factories Are Having Trouble Filling Nearly 400,000 Open Jobs
text:
President Trump pledge to revive American manufacturing is running into the stubborn obstacle of demographic reality 
The pool of blue-collar workers who are able and willing to perform tasks on a factory floor in the United States is shrinking  As baby boomers retire, few young people are lining up to take their place  About 400,000 manufacturing jobs are currently unfilled, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics - a shortfall that will surely grow if companies are forced to rely less on manufacturing overseas and build more factories in the United States, experts say 
Difficulty attracting and retaining a quality work force has been consistently cited as a "top primary challenge" by American manufacturers since 2017, said Victoria Bloom, the chief economist at the National Association of Manufacturers, which produces a quarterly survey  Only recently has the issue slipped down on the list of challenges, superseded by trade-related uncertainty and increased raw material costs due to tariffs, Ms  Bloom said 
But the scarcity of skilled blue-collar workers remains a long-term problem, according to Ron Hetrick, an economist with Lightcast, a company that provides labor data to universities and industry 
"We spent three generations telling everybody that if they didn't go to college, they are a loser," he said  "Now we are paying for it  We still need people to use their hands "
The hiring challenges faced by American factories are multifaceted 
The president crackdown on immigration, which includes attempts to revoke deportation protections for migrants from troubled countries, may eliminate workers who could have filled those jobs 
Many Americans aren't interested in factory jobs because they often do not pay enough to lure workers away from service jobs that may have more flexible schedules or more comfortable working environments 
For some companies, remaining globally competitive involves the use of sophisticated equipment that requires employees to have extensive training and familiarity with software  And employers cannot simply hire people right out of high school without providing specialized training programs to bring them up to speed  That wasn't the case in the heyday of American manufacturing 
Attracting motivated young people to manufacturing careers is also a challenge when high school guidance counselors are still judged by how many students go on to college 
College graduates, on the other hand, often do not have the right skills to be successful on a factory floor  
The country is flooded with college graduates who can't find jobs that match their education, Mr  Hetrick said, and there are not enough skilled blue-collar workers to fill the positions that currently exist, let alone the jobs that will be created if more factories are built in the United States 
The Business Roundtable, a lobbying group whose members are chief executives of companies, has started an initiative in which executives collaborate on strategies to attract and train a new generation of workers in skilled trades  At an event last week in Washington, executives commiserated about how hard it was to find qualified people and swapped tips onstage for overcoming the gap 
Their ideas included combing through existing company job descriptions to prioritize relevant experience over college degrees and recruiting high school students as young as sophomores for experiences that could draw their interest in manufacturing careers 
"For every 20 job postings that we have, there is one qualified applicant right now," said David Gitlin, the chairman and chief executive of Carrier Global, which produces air-conditioners and furnaces and services heating and cooling equipment 
With the rise of artificial intelligence, Mr  Gitlin said, demand has exploded for technicians to service data centers, which are built with cooling systems called chillers  He estimated that each data center would require four technicians to maintain a single chiller 
"We have 425,000 technicians today," he said, referring to the heating and air-conditioning industry as a whole  "We are going to need to hire another four to five hundred thousand over the next 10 years " But the number of young people going to vocational schools and community colleges, he added, is dropping, not growing 
At the Business Roundtable event, executives praised Mr  Trump efforts to revive the country industrial base  But some executives acknowledged that the president immigration policies posed challenges to any effort to fill the factories that he has pledged to bring back  
Peter J  Davoren, the chairman and chief executive of Turner Construction Company, said that he would like to see "a clear path for citizenship" for immigrants in the construction industry and the food industry 
The Trump administration aggressive cuts to training programs for blue-collar workers have also hurt efforts to train a new generation of factory workers  The administration has taken steps to eliminate the Job Corps, a 60-year-old program that provides at-risk youths from 16 to 24 with a path to a career in the trades  Huntington Ingalls Industries, the country largest shipbuilder, hired 68 Job Corps graduates in December in its bid to beef up its work force 
"The gap between available skills and needed skills in the work force is widening," Chris Kastner, the president and chief executive of HII, said  "Technology is evolving fast but education and training systems too often lag behind "
The Trump administration has rolled out an initiative called Make America Skilled Again, which consolidates existing work force training programs into one initiative that would give states grants if the states met certain criteria  At least 10 percent of the new Make America Skilled Again funding must be spent on apprenticeships 
In April, Mr  Trump signed an executive order that directed the secretary of labor, the secretary of commerce and the secretary of education to submit a plan to create one million registered apprenticeships  But it is unclear if that ambitious target can be achieved with the funding in Mr  Trump budget bill, which trims $1 6 billion from work force training 
In April, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent drew the ire of many federal employees when he suggested that American factories could get the labor they needed from the ranks of laid-off government workers  "We are shedding excess labor in the federal government," he told Tucker Carlson, the former Fox News host  "That will give us the labor we need for the new manufacturing "
But recruiting laid-off federal employees never came up at the Roundtable event  Instead, participants spoke of efforts to train high school students and veterans 
Blake Moret, the chairman and chief executive of Rockwell Automation, a Milwaukee-based process-automation specialist, said his company had created an academy of advanced manufacturing that trained returning service members for 12 weeks 
Sara Armbruster, the chief executive of Steelcase, a company in Grand Rapids, Mich , that designs furniture, said businesses must begin recruiting in high school to educate students and their parents about how rewarding a career in manufacturing could be 
Students often change their minds about a career in manufacturing when they visit the company shop floor and see that a modern factory is clean, high-tech and "cool," she added 
"When they have that moment, it really changes everything in terms of opening up possibilities for them in their career," she said 
PHOTOS: At Charlotte Pipe and Foundry factory in Oakboro, N C , an employee ground off imperfections on goods as they came off the production line  (PHOTOGRAPH BY TRAVIS DOVE FOR THE NEW YORK TIMES) (B1); John Wren, chief executive of Omnicom Group, said he was "delighted" that his company $13 5 billion agreement with Interpublic was moving forward  (PHOTOGRAPH BY BALINT PORNECZI/BLOOMBERG) (B3) This article appeared in print on page B1, B3

headline: Why Greenland is in the spotlight
text:
Remote, icy and mostly pristine, Greenland plays an outsize role in the daily weather experienced by billions of people and in the climate changes taking shape all over the planet  Greenland is where climate change, scarce resources, tense geopolitics and new trade patterns all intersect, said Ohio University security and environment professor Geoff Dabelko  The world largest island is now "central to the geopolitical, geoeconomic competition in many ways," partly because of climate change, Dabelko said  Since his first term in office, President-elect Donald Trump has expressed interest in acquiring Greenland, which is a semiautonomous territory of Denmark, a longtime U S  ally and a founding member of NATO  
It is also home to a large U S  military base  Why it coveted? Think of Greenland as an open refrigerator door or thermostat for a warming world, and it in a region that is warming four times faster than the rest of the globe, said New York University climate scientist David Holland  Locked inside are valuable rare earth minerals needed for telecommunications, as well as uranium, billions of untapped barrels of oil and a vast supply of natural gas that used to be inaccessible but is becoming less so  Many of the same minerals are currently being supplied mostly by China, so other countries such as the United States are interested, Dabelko said  Three years ago, the Denmark government suspended oil development offshore from the territory of 57,000 people  But more than the oil, gas or minerals, there ice " a "ridiculous" amount, said climate scientist Eric Rignot of the University of California, Irvine  If that ice melts, it would reshape coastlines across the globe and potentially shift weather patterns in such a dramatic manner that the threat was the basis of a Hollywood disaster movie  Greenland holds enough ice that if it all melts, the world seas would rise by 24 feet    Greenland will be "a key focus point" through the 21st century because of the effect its melting ice sheet will have on sea levels, said Mark Serreze, director of the National Snow and Ice Data Center in Boulder, Colorado  "It will likely become a bigger contributor in the future " Other climate factors? Greenland also serves as the engine and on/off switch for a key ocean current that influences Earth climate in many ways, including hurricane and winter storm activity  It called the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation, or AMOC, and it slowing down because more fresh water is being dumped into the ocean by melting ice in Greenland, Serreze said  A shutdown of the AMOC conveyor belt is a much-feared climate tipping point that could plunge Europe and parts of North America into prolonged freezes, a scenario depicted in the 2004 movie "The Day After Tomorrow " Because of Greenland mountains of ice, it also changes patterns in the jet stream, which brings storms across the globe and dictates daily weather  Often, especially in winter, a blocking system of high pressure off Greenland causes Arctic air to plunge to the west and east, smacking North America and Europe, Cohen said  Its location is important Because it straddles the Arctic Circle between the United States, Russia and Europe, Greenland is a geopolitical prize that the U S  and others have eyed for more than 150 years  "I see it as insanely beautiful  It eye-watering to be there," said Holland, who has conducted research on the ice more than 30 times since 2007  "Pieces of ice the size of the Empire State Building are just crumbling off cliffs and crashing into the ocean  And also, the beautiful wildlife, all the seals and the killer whales  It just breathtaking "
Graphic
 
AP/Aug  15, 2019 A boat navigates large icebergs in 2015 near the town of Kulusuk, Greenland

headline: Why is america's return to the Paris climate agreement so important?
text:
The United States helped the world participate in the Paris climate agreement in the innovative global agreement reached in 2015 to reduce greenhouse gas emissions in an effort to curb climate change  With Donald Trump, the United States became the only country to retire  Now, America is coming back  
On his first day as president, Joe Biden formally began the 30-day process to join the Paris Agreement, starting February 19  
We asked five academics to describe what America participation in the Paris Agreement means for the nation and the rest of the world, and for the future food security, protection and warming of the planet 
Which means this country of America is back in the Paris pact
Morgan Bazilian, Public Policy Professor and Director, Payne Institute, Colorado School of Mines 
Amanda Gorman, the nationally laureate youth poet, said for Biden inauguration: "When the day comes, we come out of the shadows " That a good articulation of why the United States is now joining the Paris Agreement  
In the short term, the benefits are mainly diplomatic  It is not a small thing to try to rebuild the international position of a country that helped the world accede to the Paris Agreement and then abruptly abandoned it  Humility, recognizing the nation recent lousy record, and reconsidering both the need for a U S -organized climate summit and the habit of "naming and shaming" other countries could be helpful  
The Paris Agreement took years to design and develop  It allows considerable flexibility and goes intrinsically "from bottom to top": each country sets its own objectives and determines how it will meet them  I was a negotiator in the climate talks for several years  
The deal was never a threat  His dismissal by the Trump administration was theater, or bragging  There will be a temptation to listen to Obama-era policies and approaches again: many of Biden political appointees come from that shared experience  Reinstatement should primarily be used as a boost for much stronger, more stable, sustainable and thoughtful national policy and regulation  That the least glamorous thing: unfinished work  
Why US participation is important for other countries 
Edward Carr, Professor and Director of International Development, Community and Environment, Clark University 
Just returning to the Paris Agreement, as signed in 2015, will not meet the world climate needs or restore the United States to a global climate change leadership position  
To measure the seriousness of the Biden administration, other countries will be watching two things  
First, will the United States strengthen its commitments to decarbonize the economy?
While the Trump administration worked to undermine global action on climate change, several statesthey adopted aggressive goals, including Washington, California and even states with Republican governors like Massachusetts  With far more ambitious goals than those agreed by the United States in the 2015 Paris Agreement, these states make it clear that the United States can be much more aggressive in its national commitments without losing its global competitiveness  
Second, will the Biden administration invest heavily in adaptation at home and abroad?
A growing body of research shows that the worst impacts of climate change are endured by the poorest and most vulnerable people  Moreover, these impacts tend to exacerbate existing inequalities  The new management has the tools  The world will have to see if its focus on justice and equality extends to the impacts of climate change  
Returning to Paris is a good first step  But without further steps, it will be seen as empty and could further erode America credibility 
We recommend:
Biden signs first decrees: reinstates US to Paris Agreement and halies arctic oil plan 
'One more scratch on the tiger   ': 2020 was the warmest year in Europe since records existed 
What the Paris food security agreement means 
Kristie Ebi, Professor of Global Health and environment, University of Washington 
Food security will be a theme of this century, largely due to the increase in carbon dioxide concentrations and our changing climate  
Globally, nearly 9% of the population suffers from food insecurity, and numbers have increased in recent years  Approximately 45 percent of childhood deaths worldwide are attributed to insufficient calories or nutrients  
As the planet continues to warm, climate change threatens to worsen these situations 
Crop damage as global temperatures rise isn't just about heat, floods and droughts  While carbon dioxide is necessary for plant growth, research shows that increasing CO2 concentrations will reduce the nutrient density of the world two most important crops, wheat and rice, as well as other food sources  These nutritional losses can have serious health effects, such as impaired cognitive development and metabolism, obesity and diabetes  
In addition, climate changes resulting from increased CO2 are reducing crop yields and food supply stability  
The United States is the second largest CO2 emitter after China, and the largest historically  The Biden administration new commitment to reducing greenhouse gas emissions under the Paris climate agreement and advancing research and solution development can help protect the health and well-being of families and future generations  
Why the Paris agreement is important forvulnerable communities 
Deb Niemeier, Professor of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Maryland 
In a valley that was once home to the Maidu tribes, local legend says a man stood on a hill in the 1860s, escaping the heat of Central California Valley  Enjoying the fresh air, I was sure I had found paradise  Over the next 50 years, that mountain range saw mining give way to sawmills and, finally, the planting of fruit orchards  A small but robust village grew, along the way resisting many fires  
Over time, Paradise, California, became home to farmers, retirees and others who were just looking for a quieter life  The air was fresh and the views extraordinary  The 2018 Camp Fire destroyed most of the city and devastated many of its farms  I was there later  The culprit was the aging infrastructure and extremely dry conditions that have become more common as the planet warms 
The future of humanity has always been intertwined with that of the natural world  Today, however, people have a huge influence that comes in part from years of burning fossil fuels and other climate-influencing activities  
Coastal communities face more frequent flooding as sea level rises  Western wildfire seasons are lasting longer  The National Climate Assessment has shown how extreme storms and heat waves that harm health and crops will become more common as global temperatures rise  
It is no longer enough to repair faulty power lines that can cause wildfires  The Paris Agreement motivates countries to begin the hard work of reducing greenhouse gas emissions to reduce the underlying risk  
Paris and the problem of an Arctic that heats up quickly 
Walt Meier, Senior Research Scientist, National Ice and Snow Data Center, University of Colorado 
The importance of the Paris climate agreement is particularly evident in the Arctic, where sea ice is declining and permafrost is melting  If you think about the implications for the whole planet, your imagination must expand by three  That because the Arctic is warming almost three times faster than the global average  
A large part of the Arctic climate lies on the edge of a razor between freezing and melting  Even a small change can have big consequences  An increase of 2 degrees Fahrenheit at the mid-latitudes, say 70 F to 72 F, is not easily noticeable  But in the Arctic Ocean, a 2-degree change, from 31 F to 33 F, is the difference between ice skating and swimming 
That change from ice to ocean and snow to bare ground is profound  Ice and snow are white, meaning they reflect most of the sun energy and keep the Arctic cool  The loss of ice and snow means that more sunlight is absorbed, further warming the Earth and further causing melting  
So, every greenhouse gas emittedhas three times the strength in the Arctic than at lower latitudes  This means that every greenhouse gas that the Paris climate agreement can help countries avoid emitting saves three times in the Arctic  
Our choices influence the fate of the Arctic     and the world 
You can find the original post at the following link:

The Conversation is an independent, non-profit source of news, analysis and commentary from academic experts

headline: Why the ‘redheaded stepchild’ of renewable energy is poised to rise under Trump
text:
AUSTIN, Texas - Even as President Trump slashes support for wind and solar energy, another renewable energy source is finding unexpected favor under his second administration 
In early February, newly confirmed Secretary of Energy Chris Wright named geothermal energy, which uses underground heat to generate clean heat and electricity, as one of the prime areas for department research and development  
And geothermal advocates see Trump own pledges to "drill, baby, drill" - even if primarily intended to benefit the oil and gas industry - as offering both rhetorical and practical support for the nascent enhanced geothermal industry, which uses technologies like fracking to extract the heat from the earth  
The sector is still "10 to 15 years" behind where wind and solar are, and raising capital is tough, Cindy Taff, chief executive of geothermal company Sage Geosystems, told The Hill 
But now, as momentum grows and money begins to pour in, she said, "it going to be the decade of geothermal "
With bipartisan political support behind its efforts and skyrocketing energy demand from data centers bolstering the potential market for its offerings, the geothermal industry is getting excited  
Geothermal proponents interviewed by The Hill argue it could soon offer heating, cooling and electric power in a form that combines the cheap, pollution-free aspects of wind and solar power combined with the on-demand security of fossil fuels  
Those advantages, they argue, make the industry particularly well placed to help meet the demand of the new data centers, which require massive quantities of electricity to run and cool off their servers: A November McKinsey study found that data center electricity demand would more than quadruple by the end of the decade - requiring additional electricity about equal to all the power the state of Texas used in 2024 
For one thing, they noted, geothermal energy is nearly as plentiful as solar  Last year, the International Energy Agency (IEA) found that there is enough accessible heat within the Earth to increase global electricity demand by a factor of 140  
It can also be produced on a more consistent basis  Geothermal plants on average ran 75 percent of the time in 2023, according to the IEA - far more than solar  
Unlike fossil fuels, the main contributor to global emissions, geothermal energy also offers on-demand heating, cooling and electricity without heating the planet or releasing toxins into the air  
The Trump administration embrace of geothermal is a rare silver lining in an era of record heat, Jamie Beard of the geothermal advocacy group Project InnerSpace argued  If the climate movement "wants to hand-wring about what we've lost" as Trump turns against the Biden administration climate goals, he said, then geothermal acceptance among Republicans "is something you can grab onto "
Over the short term, Beard foresaw a rough ride for the geothermal industry: It could lose access to Inflation Reduction Act tax credits if Republican efforts to cut them - in whole or in part - go through  And most federal geothermal programs are housed in the Department of Energy Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, a potential target of a staunchly pro-fossil fuel administration 
But over the medium term, Beard said she was "cautiously optimistic" that "geothermal is going to shine under Trump" - an attitude widely shared by geothermal entrepreneurs 
Many were tentatively excited about Trump pick of Wright, pointing to his deep experience - as head of oilfield services company Liberty Energy - with oil and gas drilling, which has significant overlap with geothermal technology  While generally focused on oil and gas, Wright company, Liberty Energy, invested $10 million in Houston-based geothermal startup Fervo Energy  
Wright "wants to unleash base load power, and we think geothermal really fits the bill," Charlotte "Coco" Wallace, who leads policy and business development for geothermal startup Quaise, told The Hill 
Others saw hope in Trump announcement of a new "National Energy Council," to be headed by Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, which would encircle the whole energy supply chain - from permitting to production to distribution - under one hub  
"For geothermal, that is potentially HUGE," Beard wrote to The Hill  Most of the hottest, most accessible rock in the country is in the West, nearly half of which is federally owned  In those regions, "red tape" has made geothermal projects a "slog," she said  
Much of the energy in geothermal is currently based in Houston, Texas, site of the nation biggest oil and gas industry workforce and home to billions in energy capital  Many oil giants now have geothermal divisions, and Texas leads in startups 
There so much going on in the state, even industry insiders struggle to keep up with what happening, said Matt Welch of the Texas Geothermal Energy Alliance, the local industry trade group  
In December, for example, Fervo - the company that Liberty Energy invested in - announced it had raised more than a quarter-billion dollars in a single funding round, bringing its capital to well more than half a billion dollars  
Once considered too expensive and geographically limited - dependent on rare underground geyser systems of naturally heated water - geothermal is now being reimagined using drilling techniques pioneered in the oilfields like the Eagle Ford Shale of South Texas and the Dallas-area Barnett Shale, two of the original proving grounds of the country oil and gas boom 
Houston-based companies like Fervo, Sage Geosystems and Quaise are testing new methods to exploit the western U S  underground heat, securing record-breaking funding rounds and striking deals with tech giants like Google and Meta  
Fervo, the first company to apply fracking to geothermal, has a deal with Google to supply 115 megawatts of clean power to the Nevada grid by 2026 
"We don't think the world is going to be solely powered by geothermal because, you know, the world has never been solely powered by one energy resource," Fervo chief executive Tim Latimer told The Hill last year 
But he thinks that by midcentury, the grid could be 80 percent solar - with the remaining 20 percent offered by geothermal, serving as that "base load backbone to a flexible, functioning type of grid "
Sage, meanwhile, raised $35 million in December and just finished construction on a prototype pressure battery in South Texas - a new form of energy storage which uses underground cavities to store energy generated by solar farms or windmills 
Sage has also signed a deal with tech company Meta to provide 150 megawatts of geothermal electricity to a company data center by 2027 - the largest such deal inked for a project east of the geologically active Rocky Mountains 
Then there is Quaise, another Houston-based company that has raised nearly $100 million  Its founder wants to use microwave beams to vaporize rock that - at 500 degrees Celsius (about 1000 degrees Fahrenheit) - would melt conventional drills  
Because such technology would also significantly cut the time, and therefore cost, of drilling - much of which is consumed with laboriously pulling drill bits out of the hole every time they wear out - it could potentially bring geothermal to the densely populated Northeast, where the kind of heat usable for electricity sits inaccessible beneath miles of old, cold rock 
In a January demo for journalists and investors, Quaise engineers shined a flashlight-like beam on a shoebox-sized block of basalt and melted a two-inch hole in it  The company says the technology can ultimately be loaded onto standard drilling rigs  
Support from the administration helps get geothermal past the point where it theoretically abundant but "too expensive," Taff told The Hill 
"We need to drive that cost down," she said  Republican support, particularly in a sector dependent on capital and know-how from the fossil fuel industry, helps "get the industry to the point where we can be cost-competitive with other forms of electricity generation "
Beard worries that geothermal risks becoming partisan and ultimately being dropped or facing significant regulatory scrutiny under a future administration  In an interview with The Hill last year, she warned that "the redheaded stepchild of renewables'" place as a neutral ground in American energy politics - the site of a kind of refreshing comity that is increasingly rare - would be key to its success 
But it was also, she said, an asset that could easily be squandered  
"Here my fear: We go full bore the next two years with a Republican Congress and a Republican president, geothermal becomes a Republican flag - right alongside natural gas and oil, right? And then as soon as there a change in Congress or the administration, geothermal gets dumped," she said 
In an effort to head off such a possibility, Beard group is co-running a series of gatherings that bring together representatives of the left and right, Big Oil and the climate movement, in an effort to secure the technology future by "bringing everyone along "
Taff offered a rosier view  "Electricity is the great equalizer, right?" she said  
"Everyone needs cheap electricity  And if geothermal can bring that, I just can't see why it not going to be supported by both sides of the aisle "
For the latest news, weather, sports, and streaming video, head to The Hill

headline: Why the boards?
text:
Special for Infobae of The New York Times 
Humanity has spent millennia confined within the earthly limbo of the joints 
The ancient Egyptians, to mention a subset of humanity, used various hieroglyphs to communicate the concept of "counsel " From this detail we can infer that at least some of them spent a lot of time gathered in groups in charge of consultation tasks, since a council without meetings would be like a detour without bulls: just a lot of clowns sitting there 
Four thousand years later, the board of directors has evolved through infinite permutations: the "standing", the "general", the "advancing" and the "analysis of results", to name just a few  Moreover, when a global pandemic made it impossible to hold less essential in-person gatherings, humans invented new methods of gathering, as unable to resist its charms as waves subjected to the gravity of the moon are unable to resist advancing toward the coast and breaking on its shore 
Those meetings were digital and few passed without delays  But, despite the problems, they seemed sufficient, and perhaps even set the country on the path to the glory of a collective five percent increase in productivity 
However, some found the new rules to be flawed  It not enough to see or listen to colleagues, these people (most of the management class) say  Some closeness is necessary to be able to touch them, in theory (but in a few cases in reality, if anything)  Only when workers have repopulated their desks will the wild magic of meetings (those chills that trigger some unexpected learning, lasting friendships, and/or litigation) shine again in the workplace 
Right now, businesses are celebrating the waning severity of the pandemic by strongly incentivizing employees to return to the office  In a retroactive analysis, the conclusion was that remote interactions have been considered unsyventable for companies' businesses 
What do we miss when we don't meet in person, besides the quality that office enthusiasts identify as "something missing"?
Do you think you'll come back to that later? Of course, we would love to; nothing gives as much relief as the promise to return to the starting point later  The problem is that the circle ends here  In this document, which will be made available to everyone to read on their own, we will try to understand why they are done together in the workplace and identify what we are supposed to have missed 
'We were undermined on a daily basis in the Cabinet'
There was a time, centuries ago, when small gatherings in the workplace kept time, not just filled it  It was in the year 1791  President George Washington had been in power for two and a half years, and the city of Philadelphia had been even longer without a violent attack like that of a mob that lunged at an elderly woman in the street because she was supposedly a witch (four years had passed) 
Back then, the presidential house was in Philadelphia, which was the temporary capital, and there (just a few blocks from where the old woman neighbors had sliced her head after accusing her of sorcery), despite years of effort to prevent it, Washington finally summoned a group of four employees at the same time 
Lindsay Chervinsky, a presidential historian and author of the book "The Cabinet: George Washington and the Creation of an American Institution," hypothesizes that Washington had shunned meetings early in his presidency because he worried that there would be "comparisons with the British cabinet, because Americans hated the British cabinet "
As Chervinsky explains, the Americans blamed the British cabinet for causing the War of Independence  The Constitutional Convention expressed a resounding rejection of proposals to create a similar body in the U S  government  The president was free to consult advisers, as long as they were individual consultations 
For more than two years, Washington sought specific advice from the heads of executive departments in individual conversations or through letters, then follow-up letters and more letters to follow up on follow-up letters 
Convening meetings to discuss matters "that could have been resolved with an email" is a popular torment among the laments of modern office workers  The tradition of the Presidential Cabinet, by contrast, originated because of all the letters that should have been meetings 
"It exactly why the Cabinet was created," explained Alexis Coe, a presidential historian and author of a biography of Washington titled "You Never Forget Your First " "Washington was not in a position to respond to everyone  He went crazy " Every letter the president sent had to be handwritten and then copied for preservation in presidential records  If one of your interlocutors made any new enquiried, you might well require yet another letter to an adviser you had already consulted  "It was just too much," Coe said 
The solution Washington came up with was to gather everyone to deliberate in his studio: a room 4 5 x 6 4 meters, full of furniture and in which a sweltering heat was concentrated in the warm months 
Under the president quiet leadership, the meetings provided his advisers with a space for discussion  "Jefferson and Hamilton are supposed to be willing to destroy each other," Coe said  "They're supposed to kill themselves  What Washington thought was precisely that that confrontation would be productive, because he would be there and allow that to happen "
Although they were usually contentious and frustrating for the few participants, the meetings really accelerated the task of establishing a new government and, unlike the recurring and meaningless meetings that office calendars are now full of, at least they were imbued with the sense of urgency of tailor-made planning 
Who needs the board?
It is clear how it might benefit a group of individuals dedicated to erecting the literal and figurative architecture of a new government to meet in person to debate, at a time when there were neither phones nor computers  But why is everyone else calendar full with thousands of meetings?
Caitlin Rosenthal, a historian at the University of California, Berkeley campus, found that the rapidly spreading fire of modern corporate boards was ignited by two sparks of the Industrial Revolution era: the growth of big business and a late-development theory that employees are people 
"This is a period that many might describe as the shift from workshops to factories," said Rosenthal, who previously worked as a consultant at McKinsey & Co  where she said she attended "many" meetings 
A shoemaker shop with a few employees doesn't need much internal communication, Rosenthal said  Instead, a shoe factory preparing to implement technology in its machinery in order to increase production volume may have to divide work among hundreds of workers and operate through large distribution channels, such as railroads, complicating the logistics of deliveries 
"All of a sudden, it turns out that you need formal structures for reporting, and people share information through formal channels  All of that will require together," Rosenthal concluded 
In the same period, many of the largest companies began to modify their participation structure  There were "more partnerships," Rosenthal said, "to be more specific, more partnerships  And those types of companies require shareholders' meetings and board meetings " The annual meetings were review forums, and they often provided an opportunity to plan for growth 
Unfortunately, the fact that the business is growing does not generally mean that everyone is satisfied in the workplace  In some New England factories, employees resigned at such a rapid pace that there was a risk of having to stop production altogether (for example, if a company could not get enough workers to operate a machine)  "He fled" was the explanation that was sometimes recorded in the accounting books of companies for these departures 
Frequent staff turnover was costly; companies needed some tool to ensure that the workers in whose training they had invested time and effort remained in the job (large southern companiesthey turned the problem around with the exploitation of enslaved day laborers who couldn't give up) )
Then came the area of human resources 
The meaning of these two words together is often not well understood  The intention was never for any element of that phrase to describe resources made available to human beings by the employer  Rather, the humans referred to are the resources of the respective organization, or at least some of them (human resources) 
In Rosenthal view, the development of these departments represents "the evolution of managerial thinking " While industrialization allowed companies to extract more value and faster from their employees' work, managers regularly evaluated employees almost "as if they were machines, with an interest only in inputs and outputs," he said  Treating human beings like machines is likely to create setbacks  It is something that happened in the past and it also happens in the present  Managers needed to design a method that would allow them to readjust the relationship with their subordinates 
The idea they had: to remember everyone birthday 
"Hr usually loves birthdays," Rosenthal said  A 1951 document he found while researching the history of human resources departments advised giving employees a "birthday lunch" in an effort to "heal any discomfort with the organization "
Another thing that human beings love, besides their birthday: that other human beings recognize them  Meetings are opportunities to consult employees with the rest of the team, to explicitly request their presence, and to invite meetings 
What to do if you get through a board?
Of course, not all boards are designed to manipulate people into believing they are valued members of a work community  Sometimes, you simply have to decide what the theme of the party will be, communicate to staff the changed storage policies, resolve scheduling issues between spring sports, and it turns out that someone decided, perhaps even quite rightly, that an in-person dialogue is the most effective way to achieve this 
Nor is it that all verbal communication about work between colleagues is a meeting; some other forms of interaction are spontaneous conversations, prepared speeches and disagreements that turn into full-lunged discussions in front of the entire office 
Unfortunately, some of the worst meetings have their origin in the best intentions  In his exceptional 1976 treatise on effective communication in the workplace, entitled "How to Run a Meeting", British writer Antony Jay warns of dangers such as refusing to exclude someone from a debate or waiting for everyone to arrive to start the meeting ("There is only one way to make sure a meeting starts on time - start on time," Jay wrote 
In excessive detail, Jay describes what seems to the reader to be about 500,000 possible permutations of meeting type, objectives, leadership tactics, debate structures, seat allocation, and many other elements of the architecture of hypothetical boards (which always, by any direction, undoubtedly lead us to productivity), in a scene increasingly resembling a motif taken from an Escher lithograph  However, Jay is not in favor of default meetings  As he writes, holding a meeting is only justified if the consequences of not doing so are of sufficient severity 
Tsedal Neeley, a professor of Business Administration at Harvard Business School, blamed the modern saturation of meetings on the assumption that verbal communication is the best way to communicate 
"All these together," he said, "I bet you, I assure you: you could eliminate 50 percent if people had the courage to eliminate them "
To select a communication format, Neeley recommends considering two criteria: first, should all participants be present at the same time and in the same space to exchange information? Secondly, will a better understanding of information be achieved through "terse means" (which are done through text) or "enriched media" (which include nonverbal context)?
Instant messaging apps, Neeley explained, are "synchronous" (designed for simultaneous engagement) and "terse" (primarily text-based), making them ideal when only coordination is needed  Whether they're done in person or via video chat, he said, the meetings are synchronous and enriched, and they're also ideal for tasks that involve complex coordination and negotiation actions 
In short, a joint can be good, but only if it needs to be a joint 
Consultation meetings should be reduced, Neeley said, with a maximum of six participants, to reduce the risk of "social reduction of effort," a phenomenon that involves people attending but not participating in the meeting 
As for the people you need to invite: "You need a combination of introverts and extroverts," he said  "Introversion and extroversion are not defined just by the fact that people talk more or less  They refer to the way people process information and ideas "
Technology for meetings in technology companies
In mid-February 2020, engineers working on Google video calling app, Google Meet, began receiving alerts that servers in Asia were experiencing unusually high levels of traffic from their product  The volume was so high that there was a risk of overcapacity if no appropriate action was taken  A short time later, that pattern was repeated, but in that case traffic had risen in Italy  To resolve a situation that already constituted a "global capacity risk," Google integrated an incident response team with two groups of leaders in different time zones, to ensure that there was always someone working 
"Video calls became an essential service overnight," said Sanaz Ahari, senior product director for Google communications products on Workspace and Android 
Google employees juggled: they worked non-stop to avoid outages while increasing the load capacity of servers in the company 23 international data centers  Google gave access to Google Meet (which used to be a paid service) to everyone who had a Gmail account (over 1500 million users) 
There was yet another obstacle to actions to support video conferencing designed to help Google users work from home: "It all happened with the same team members who were thinking, 'Oh my God, I have to work from home and deal with my kids now that we're all home together!'" said Ahari 
According to the company, between January and March, meet peak daily usage multiplied by a factor of 30  Between March 2020 and March 2021, people participated in more than 6000 million meetings through Google Meet  During a summer peak, users spent a total of 7500 million minutes on Meet every day 
Although the sudden proliferation of remote video calls came to try to continue business as usual, the change had unpredictable effects on people lives 
Coe, the presidential biographer, was already working from home before the pandemic began  He mentioned that, in 2020, many meetings that the previous year would have been called were replaced with video chats 
Rosenthal, the history professor, realized that, with the complication of child care during the pandemic, she was turning down more meetings  She was forced to do so  "And most of them weren't necessary "
Ahari said that at Google ("I've heard it from our customers, but I've also observed it personally on Google") there a phenomenon that designated widespread "organic etiquette adoption" in meetings, particularly as it relates to the raise-up feature on Google Meet, available for participants to indicate they want to take the floor 
"That the beauty of us all being a box," Ahari said  "Everyone participates and there is a way to contribute, regardless of whether you are in your living room or in your office  A box is a box, regardless of your time zone or location  And that a really positive aspect from a fair participation perspective " (It should be noted that if you master the number of participants who only have the audioactivated, the screen with video boxes may look like a toothless smile) 
Now that the design team is focused on refining the app for the post-pandemic work environment, Ahari said, they hope to preserve many of the elements that made Google Meet feel different from an in-person meeting in the office, such as "raise your hand," "polls," and the in-session messaging feature 
"No one will be able to hold meetings again as they were before the pandemic, that what we've realized," he said 
It would be tantamount to gathering like farm animals 
Missing or absent?
Kristin Arnold, who describes her calling as a "major board facilitator" (her website identifies Raytheon Technologies and General Mills among her clients), has heard complaints for years that meetings "are a waste of time" and that many attendees "don't know why they're there " If no one likes bad meetings, why are there still bad meetings?
The obvious answer, according to Arnold, is that many people don't know how to create a meeting that isn't bad 
He said the mere fact that you've had a lot of meetings "doesn't mean you know how to run a meeting "
Being specific improves meetings, Arnold said  Transform the leaders from disoriented chaperones to precise guides through the desert  He noted that instead of inviting people to a meeting to talk about back-to-office policies, "you should say, 'Let talk about how we can make sure those who still work from home feel connected to the office  Bring your ideas prepared '"
In Rosenthal experience, not in all cases meetings are traps to lure workers into complacency and allow managers to appear to be too busy  In his words, a meeting can be useful and even good if it meets these three criteria: you know what you're going to do in it, you do what you have to do, and in the end, someone says, "Understood, this is what we're all going to do from now on "
If you need to have a meeting, follow those guidelines, and with as few participants as possible

headline: Will advanced nuclear technology be in Eagle Mountain's future?
text:
Isaac Hale, Deseret News
Traffic flows along Eagle Mountain Boulevard in Eagle Mountain on Tuesday, Jan  14, 2025 
1
City leaders in Eagle Mountain are considering revisions to an ordinance that would allow a diverse set of energy options as well as new transmission to meet growing electricity demand in the fast-growing community 
Among potential developments that would be allowed under the modifications is the addition of small modular reactors - or advanced nuclear technologies 
"There are no immediate plans to have a nuclear facility in Eagle Mountain  I don't think that even the state of Utah has the capability of taking in a nuclear power plant anytime soon, just because there not really all the right pieces to bring one in - to kind of keep it simple  So we're not expecting to see a nuclear power plant, whether an SMR, small modular reator, or otherwise, anytime soon, probably several years from now," said Evan Berrett, the city economic development director 
Berrett stressed that the city wants to be able to use that energy source should it become viable and financially a good fit 
"If there anything that leads us to believe that they aren't going to be appropriate for our community, if there any reason to believe that they are not going to be safe or good for our environment, and we do take all those things, of course, super seriously, then it will be no "
Isaac Hale, Deseret News
EM energy_IH_0551 jpg
1
Why the potential change?
Enyo, a clean energy company, wants to put in battery storage and solar panels in Eagle Mountain  It requested a revision of the ordinance to make the project possible  Enyo developed an 80-megawatt solar farm in Tooele County and has three other solar projects under development in Utah 
Berrett said aside from the existing Facebook facility and Enyo request, three other high tech companies have expressed a strong desire to power up data or AI centers in Eagle Mountain, including Google, which has already purchased land at the south end of the city 
"Because of our growth, because of all the demands here, we're trying to create a local economy, and that difficult when you're just sort of on the end of the line," said Eagle Mountain Mayor Tom Westmoreland  "We're not a drive-through city  We don't have an I-15 or anything like that  So creating a strong local economy is a real challenge for us "
The city, for example, has grown from 250 residents when it was founded 28 years ago to a population of 71,000, Westmoreland said  "We're still adding quite a few people  I think last year we added somewhere around 5,500 people, so it is growing super fast "
Isaac Hale, Deseret News
EM energy_IH_0487 jpg
1
City officials say the revisions align with "Operation Gigawatt," announced last year, in which the state aims to double its energy production within a decade 
Revisions contemplate more zoning flexibility for not only solar and battery storage, but natural gas plants, electrical substations and the small modular reactor option 
That component has a fueled a group of opponents fearful about the dangers of nuclear-powered energy, the risks it imposes to the community, water consumption, exposure to radiation and more 
"Nuclear reactors, while efficient energy sources, carry numerous potential risks, regardless of size," stated Joy Rasmussen, who launched a petition to gather opponents to potential nuclear deployment in Eagle Mountain  She cited the possibility of earthquakes, fires, floods and potential terrorist attacks that could expose residents to undue risks of leaked radiation 
"Given these known risks, allowing a nuclear reactor in Eagle Mountain would be a dangerous proposition, putting our community health at jeopardy," she wrote in her petition 
"We urge you to recognize the gravity of this situation - the threat it presents to millions of lives, and how it can potentially increase cancer risks in our community "
Both Berrett and Westmoreland stressed there is not a immediate plan to site a small modular reactor in the community and before that happens - if it does - it will be properly vetted 
Westmoreland added that even without the demand posed by data centers, the city will be unable to meet energy needs of its residents without another resource and diversification 
City officials toured the Lakeside Natural Gas plant last week and also met virtually with representatives from the Idaho National Laboratory, the nation premier U S  Department of Energy laboratory researching nuclear technologies 
"You know, electricity really is at the core of modern life, and I have not seen an alternative to that yet," Westmoreland said  " So we've had hydroelectric power and coal power, but, of course, those take lots of water  We can possibly do natural gas, but even there, you still still have exhaust and with our high air quality standards, and everybody wants clean air, right?"
Westmoreland said the main concern for residents should be the ability to keep the lights on 
"Especially in United States, we've come to take electricity for granted  I mean, we flip on the switch and it comes on but if the day comes that it not reliable, that going to severely impact us in a negative way, and that truly does frighten me," he said  "It frightens me from a national security level  It frightens me from a quality of life level "
The city planning commission is expected to take up the ordinance revisions Tuesday night and possibly vote to move them on for the consideration of the city council

headline: Will the Shift to Clean Power Continue Under Trump?
text:
Experts say that President Trump may be able to slow the transition away from fossil fuels, but he won't be able to stop it completely 
Earlier this week, Lisa Friedman, Brad Plumer and I reported on the head-spinning moves by the Trump administration that are undermining the government ability to combat climate change 
Trump policies, which are promoting fossil fuels and reducing support for clean energy investments, are expected to increase emissions of planet-warming greenhouse gases 
The changes comes as the energy transition - the broad shift away from fossil fuels and toward clean power - had been racing ahead  Spending on clean energy in the United States topped $280 billion in 2023, up from about $200 billion in 2020, according to the International Energy Agency 
Many of these investments were encouraged by laws passed during the Biden administration  But much of the activity is simply a function of market dynamics as the price of clean energy comes down and demand for solar, wind and batteries grows 
Put differently: not even the White House can fully control the pace of the energy transition 
Over the past month or so, I've asked a wide range of policymakers, executives and climate activists what they think will happen to renewable energy projects, electric vehicles and other initiatives now that Trump is in power 
None disputed that the administration actions were having an effect  But most asserted that while some of Trump moves may slow the pace of change, they are unlikely to stop it completely 
Canceled projects
Manish Bapna, the president of the Natural Resources Defense Council, said that it was already possible to see the impact of Trump policies in the form of canceled or delayed projects 
"Their policies are undercutting a thriving, clean energy industry that creating jobs, cutting costs for consumers and making the country more energy secure," Bapna said  "Trump actions have put those gains at risk, and cast a pall of uncertainty over our clean energy future  Jobs and investments have been thrown into limbo as new factories are put on hold or canceled outright "
In recent years, the construction of new clean energy projects has vastly outpaced new fossil fuel projects  But Abigail Dillen, the president of Earthjustice, an environmental law firm, said that Trump fast-tracking of approvals for new natural gas plants and moves to slow construction of new wind farms risked blunting that momentum 
"That entire trajectory is in jeopardy," she said 
Danielle Franz, the chief executive of the American Conservation Coalition, a conservative environmental group, said she supported Trump efforts to expand fossil fuel production at a moment of growing energy demand 
"The Trump administration is right to cut red tape that is holding back energy production," she said, adding that she was "cautiously optimistic" that Trump policies would be good for both the economy and the environment 
'The economics have never been better'
Jon Creyts, the chief executive of RMI, a nonprofit organization that promotes sustainability, said that falling costs, as much as climate concerns, were driving the energy transition 
Costs for building new solar and wind installations have fallen significantly in recent years  And once clean energy systems are in place, they are generally very cheap to maintain 
"The economics have never been better," he said  "We have better solutions now than we've ever had before "
John Podesta, a senior climate adviser in the Biden administration, said that while Trump moves may put up some roadblocks, much of the momentum is being driven by private industry, rather than federal policy 
"Will they kill the direction from fossil to clean? They can't really do that," Podesta said  "They can make a mess of things, and they're doing that  They can slow things down, but they can't kill it "
Electricity demand
One big reason renewables like wind and solar are expected to keep growing: the United States needs them 
After years of relatively stable power demand, the nation appetite for electricity is growing fast, driven in part by the proliferation of data centers, and the electrification of the transportation sector and buildings 
"Nothing is being built fast enough to satisfy those needs," said Scott Jacobs, the chief executive of Generate Capital, a sustainable infrastructure investment firm 
To meet that demand, companies and grid operators are looking not only to gas and coal, but also wind, solar and nuclear power 
Electric vehicles and China
Electric vehicles are another industry already feeling the effects of Trump policies  E V  sales are still growing in the United States, albeit more slowly than they were a couple years ago 
Trump, who has threatened to eliminate a tax credit for E V  buyers and stop the federal funding of new charging stations, is putting a damper on the momentum 
But carmakers have invested billions of dollars planning for a future where the internal combustion engine is a thing of the past  "In the long term, " Jacobs said, "the move to E V s seems pretty inexorable "
And if Trump moves do delay the energy transition in the United States, they are unlikely to slow it down for the rest of the world 
Other countries are racing ahead with their plans to deploy large amounts of wind and solar  And if the United States steps back from its efforts to become a leader in the renewables industry, China, which has already established itself as the world biggest supplier of solar panels and electric vehicles, will be there to pick up the slack 
"Countries that are committed to addressing climate change will be buying clean energy technologies from China," said Kelly Sims Gallagher, the dean of the Fletcher School of Law and Diplomacy at Tufts University  "The world will just go on without us "
- Lisa Friedman contributed reporting
See How Butterflies Are Surviving, or Not, Near You
It hard to count insects 
Even as scientists have found that many insect populations are in decline, they've struggled to understand the scale of what happening  Now, a groundbreaking new study offers grim answers about the status of butterflies in the contiguous United States 
In 20 years, the fleeting time it takes for a human baby to grow into a young adult, the country has lost 22 percent of its butterflies, researchers found  - Catrin Einhorn and Harry Stevens
Read the full article 
More climate news:
Global sea ice cover hit a record low in February, according to a new study highlighted by The Washington Post 
The United States is withdrawing from the Just Energy Transition Partnership, a collaboration between richer nations to help developing countries transition from coal to cleaner energy, Reuters reports 
New York State climate goals, which were already under strain, Bloomberg reports, could be hurt more by Trump tariffs against Canada
